Sects and Disagreements
beginner
5 min
The Hadith on the 73 Sects and the Saved Sect
Imam Samarqandi opens with the hadith on sects and identifies the saved sect.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the hadith about the division of the Muslim community into 73 sects, why only one sect is saved, and who that saved sect is according to the Prophet.

Core Idea
The saved sect is the great majority (al-sawad al-a'zam) who follow the Sunnah and the community.

Imam Samarqandi begins his famous work by grounding the concept of orthodoxy in a prophetic hadith. The hadith warns that the Muslim community will splinter into many sects, but only one will be saved. This lesson introduces the hadith and identifies the saved sect as the people of the Sunnah and the community (ahl al-sunnah wa al-jama'ah), also called the great majority (al-sawad al-a'zam). The author also quotes a stern warning against opposing the community.

Guided Path
1
Read the hadith and the opening
Read the Arabic passage from the basmala through the hadith and the warning. Notice how the author establishes the authority of the Prophet's way.
The hadith mentions previous communities as a pattern.
2
Identify the saved sect
The Prophet identifies the saved sect as 'ahl al-sunnah wa al-jama'ah' and 'al-sawad al-a'zam'. Reflect on why these names are significant.
3
Understand the warning
The hadith says that opposing the community by a handspan removes the yoke of Islam. Consider what this means for unity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم الحمد لله رب العالمين وصلى الله على سيدنا محمد خاتم النبيين والمرسلين وعلى آله وصحبه أجمعين (وبعد) فإن سألت عن مذهب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم فمذهبه الطريق المستقيم كقال النبي عليه السلام إن قوم موسى افترقوا من بعده إحدى وسبعين فرقة فهلك سبعون وتخلص فرقة واحدة وقوم عيسى افترقوا من بعده اثنتين وسبعين فرقة فهلك إحدى وسبعون وتخلص فرقة واحدة وإن أمتي ستفترق على ثلاث وسبعين فرقة فتهلك اثنتان وسبعون فرقة وتخلص فرقة واحدة (قيل) يا رسول الله ومن تلك الفرقة قال أصحاب السنة والجماعة وهو السواد الأعظم (قال) رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم من خالف الجماعة قدر شبر فقد خلع ربقة الإسلام من عنقه وعلامة السواد الأعظم أن يكون الإنسان متصفا باثنتين وستين خصلة (أولاها) أنه لا يشك في إيمانه ولا يقول إني مؤمن إن شاء الله تعالى (والثانية) أن لا يخالف جماعة المسلمين (والثالثة) أن يصلي خلف كل بر وفاجر ويرى ذلك حقا (والرابعة) أن لا يكفر أحدا من أهل القبلة بالذنب ما لم يستحله (والخامسة) أن يصلي على جنازة كل صغير وكبير من أهل القبلة ويراه حقا (والسادسة) أن يرى تقدير الخير والشر من الله تعالى (والسابعة) أن لا يخرج على أحد من المسلمين بالسيف من غير حق (والثامنة) أن يرى (المسح)
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds, and blessings and peace be upon our master Muhammad, the Seal of the Prophets and Messengers, and upon his family and all his Companions. (To proceed): If you ask about the way (madhhab) of the Prophet (peace be upon him), his way is the straight path. For the Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'The people of Moses split after him into seventy-one sects; seventy were destroyed and one sect was saved. The people of Jesus split after him into seventy-two sects; seventy-one were destroyed and one sect was saved. And my community will split into seventy-three sects; seventy-two will be destroyed and one sect will be saved.' It was asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, which sect is that?' He said: 'The people of the Sunnah and the Community (ahl al-sunnah wa al-jama'ah), and it is the great majority (al-sawad al-a'zam).' The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) said: 'Whoever opposes the community (al-jama'ah) by a handspan has removed the yoke of Islam from his neck.' And the sign of the great majority (al-sawad al-a'zam) is that a person is characterized by sixty-two traits. The first: that he does not doubt his faith and does not say 'I am a believer if Allah wills'. The second: that he does not oppose the community of Muslims. The third: that he prays behind every righteous and sinful person and considers that to be right. The fourth: that he does not declare anyone of the qibla (i.e., Muslims) to be an unbeliever because of a sin, as long as he does not deem it lawful. The fifth: that he prays over the funeral of every young and old among the people of the qibla and considers that right. The sixth: that he believes that the decree of good and evil is from Allah Almighty. The seventh: that he does not rebel against any Muslim with the sword without just cause. The eighth: that he considers (wiping over the leather socks...)
Source pages p. 1

Commentary
This passage opens the book 'Al-Sawad al-A'zam' with the basmala and praise, then states that the Prophet's way is the straight path. It then cites a hadith about the division of earlier communities into sects and the prophecy that the Muslim community will split into 73 sects, with only one saved. When asked about this saved sect, the Prophet identifies them as 'ahl al-sunnah wa al-jama'ah' (the people of the Sunnah and the community), also called 'al-sawad al-a'zam' (the great majority). The author then quotes another hadith warning against opposing the community, and begins to enumerate the 62 traits (khisal) that characterize a member of this saved group. The first eight traits are given: (1) no doubt in one's faith and not saying 'I am a believer if Allah wills' (i.e., affirming certainty of faith without condition); (2) not opposing the Muslim community; (3) praying behind both the righteous and the sinful, considering it valid; (4) not excommunicating a Muslim for sin unless the sin is considered lawful; (5) praying over the funeral of all Muslims, young and old; (6) believing that both good and evil are decreed by Allah; (7) not rebelling against Muslims with a sword unjustly; (8) affirming the practice of wiping over the leather socks (mas'h), which is a fiqh issue but listed here as a mark of orthodoxy. The list continues beyond this page.

Key Terms
السواد الأعظم
أهل السنة والجماعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the hadith quoted, how many sects will the Muslim community split into, and how many are saved?
A) 71 sects, one saved
B) 72 sects, one saved
C) 73 sects, one saved
D) 70 sects, one saved

Key Takeaway
The saved sect is the one that follows the Sunnah and the community, called al-sawad al-a'zam, and opposing the community is a serious matter.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
After reading this hadith, do you truly consider yourself part of 'al-sawad al-a'zam'? What concrete steps do you take to ensure you are not one of those who opposes the community by even a handspan?
?
The Prophet warned that opposing the community removes the yoke of Islam from one's neck. Is there any area of your life where you have isolated yourself from the mainstream Muslim community in a way that could be seen as opposition? How will you reattach that yoke today?
?
This lesson identifies the saved sect as those who follow the Sunnah and the community. What specific deviations from the Sunnah or the community have you observed in your own practice, and what will you do this week to align yourself more closely with the path of the saved sect?

Sects and Disagreements
beginner
8 min
The First Eight Traits of the Saved Sect
Imam Samarqandi enumerates the first eight characteristics of the saved sect.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to list the first eight characteristics that define a member of the saved sect (al-sawad al-a'zam) according to Imam Samarqandi.

Core Idea
The saved sect is defined by specific beliefs and practices, beginning with certainty of faith and unity with the community.

After establishing that the saved sect is the great majority (al-sawad al-a'zam), Imam Samarqandi begins to enumerate the sixty-two traits that characterize its members. This lesson covers the first eight of these traits, which include affirming one's faith without doubt, unity with the community, praying behind any Muslim, not excommunicating others for sins, and affirming divine decree, among others.

Guided Path
1
Read the list of eight traits
Read the Arabic passage that starts with 'وعلامة السواد الأعظم' and lists the first eight traits. Read the translation alongside.
The traits are numbered in the text.
2
Examine each trait individually
Go through each trait one by one. Understand what each means: e.g., not doubting faith, not opposing the community, praying behind righteous and sinful leaders, etc.
The first trait about not saying 'insha'Allah' is a point of creed.
3
Look for common themes
Notice that the traits cover belief, practice, and social interaction. They emphasize unity and avoiding extremism.
4
Reflect on the significance
Why do you think these traits are the first ones mentioned? How do they distinguish the saved sect from others?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم الحمد لله رب العالمين وصلى الله على سيدنا محمد خاتم النبيين والمرسلين وعلى آله وصحبه أجمعين (وبعد) فإن سألت عن مذهب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم فمذهبه الطريق المستقيم كقال النبي عليه السلام إن قوم موسى افترقوا من بعده إحدى وسبعين فرقة فهلك سبعون وتخلص فرقة واحدة وقوم عيسى افترقوا من بعده اثنتين وسبعين فرقة فهلك إحدى وسبعون وتخلص فرقة واحدة وإن أمتي ستفترق على ثلاث وسبعين فرقة فتهلك اثنتان وسبعون فرقة وتخلص فرقة واحدة (قيل) يا رسول الله ومن تلك الفرقة قال أصحاب السنة والجماعة وهو السواد الأعظم (قال) رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم من خالف الجماعة قدر شبر فقد خلع ربقة الإسلام من عنقه وعلامة السواد الأعظم أن يكون الإنسان متصفا باثنتين وستين خصلة (أولاها) أنه لا يشك في إيمانه ولا يقول إني مؤمن إن شاء الله تعالى (والثانية) أن لا يخالف جماعة المسلمين (والثالثة) أن يصلي خلف كل بر وفاجر ويرى ذلك حقا (والرابعة) أن لا يكفر أحدا من أهل القبلة بالذنب ما لم يستحله (والخامسة) أن يصلي على جنازة كل صغير وكبير من أهل القبلة ويراه حقا (والسادسة) أن يرى تقدير الخير والشر من الله تعالى (والسابعة) أن لا يخرج على أحد من المسلمين بالسيف من غير حق (والثامنة) أن يرى (المسح)
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds, and blessings and peace be upon our master Muhammad, the Seal of the Prophets and Messengers, and upon his family and all his Companions. (To proceed): If you ask about the way (madhhab) of the Prophet (peace be upon him), his way is the straight path. For the Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'The people of Moses split after him into seventy-one sects; seventy were destroyed and one sect was saved. The people of Jesus split after him into seventy-two sects; seventy-one were destroyed and one sect was saved. And my community will split into seventy-three sects; seventy-two will be destroyed and one sect will be saved.' It was asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, which sect is that?' He said: 'The people of the Sunnah and the Community (ahl al-sunnah wa al-jama'ah), and it is the great majority (al-sawad al-a'zam).' The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) said: 'Whoever opposes the community (al-jama'ah) by a handspan has removed the yoke of Islam from his neck.' And the sign of the great majority (al-sawad al-a'zam) is that a person is characterized by sixty-two traits. The first: that he does not doubt his faith and does not say 'I am a believer if Allah wills'. The second: that he does not oppose the community of Muslims. The third: that he prays behind every righteous and sinful person and considers that to be right. The fourth: that he does not declare anyone of the qibla (i.e., Muslims) to be an unbeliever because of a sin, as long as he does not deem it lawful. The fifth: that he prays over the funeral of every young and old among the people of the qibla and considers that right. The sixth: that he believes that the decree of good and evil is from Allah Almighty. The seventh: that he does not rebel against any Muslim with the sword without just cause. The eighth: that he considers (wiping over the leather socks...)
Source pages p. 1

Commentary
This passage opens the book 'Al-Sawad al-A'zam' with the basmala and praise, then states that the Prophet's way is the straight path. It then cites a hadith about the division of earlier communities into sects and the prophecy that the Muslim community will split into 73 sects, with only one saved. When asked about this saved sect, the Prophet identifies them as 'ahl al-sunnah wa al-jama'ah' (the people of the Sunnah and the community), also called 'al-sawad al-a'zam' (the great majority). The author then quotes another hadith warning against opposing the community, and begins to enumerate the 62 traits (khisal) that characterize a member of this saved group. The first eight traits are given: (1) no doubt in one's faith and not saying 'I am a believer if Allah wills' (i.e., affirming certainty of faith without condition); (2) not opposing the Muslim community; (3) praying behind both the righteous and the sinful, considering it valid; (4) not excommunicating a Muslim for sin unless the sin is considered lawful; (5) praying over the funeral of all Muslims, young and old; (6) believing that both good and evil are decreed by Allah; (7) not rebelling against Muslims with a sword unjustly; (8) affirming the practice of wiping over the leather socks (mas'h), which is a fiqh issue but listed here as a mark of orthodoxy. The list continues beyond this page.

Key Terms
خصلة
لا يشك في إيمانه
تقدير الخير والشر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One must never have any doubt in their faith at all, and any doubt invalidates it.
Correction: The intended meaning is that one should affirm their faith with certainty and not use conditional phrases like 'if Allah wills' as a way of doubting. Having passing doubts or questions does not remove one from faith; the trait is about stating faith without qualification.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is the fourth trait of the saved sect according to this passage?
A) Not rebelling against Muslims
B) Not declaring a Muslim an unbeliever for a sin unless it is deemed lawful
C) Praying behind every righteous and sinful person
D) Performing funeral prayers for all Muslims

Key Takeaway
The saved sect is defined by specific traits that promote unity, certainty, and adherence to Sunni practices; the first eight include certainty of faith, unity, prayer behind all Muslims, avoiding takfir, and affirming divine decree.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever caught yourself saying 'I am a believer if Allah wills' out of doubt? What is the root of that hesitation in your heart, and how can you cultivate unwavering certainty as this trait demands?
?
When you see Muslims committing sins, do you secretly consider them less than believers? How does this passage's command to pray behind sinful imams and to not declare takfir challenge your inner judgment? Will you actively change how you view your fellow Muslims starting today?
?
Do you truly believe that both good and evil come from Allah? When personal tragedy strikes, do you submit to His decree or do you resist and question? What must you do to align your heart with this sixth trait?

Sects and Disagreements
beginner
4 min
The Prophetic Promise of a Saved Sect
The author cites hadiths about the 73 sects and identifies the saved group.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the hadith about the 73 sects, the identity of the saved group as Ahl al-Sunna wal-Jama'a (the Sawad al-Azam), and the gravity of separating from the community.

Core Idea
The saved sect is the great majority that follows the Sunnah and the community.

This passage establishes a foundational prophetic warning and promise: the Muslim community will split into many sects, but one sect will be saved. The Prophet explicitly names this saved group as Ahl al-Sunna wal-Jama'a and calls it the Sawad al-Azam (the greatest majority). This lesson unpacks the hadith and its implications for understanding orthodoxy.

Guided Path
1
Read the hadith carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice the pattern of previous nations and the repetition of the number of sects.
The hadiths about Jews and Christians serve as a backdrop to the ummah of Muhammad.
2
Identify the saved group
Find the answer the Prophet gave when asked about the saved sect. Note the terms used: 'Ahl al-Sunna wal-Jama'a' and 'al-Sawad al-Azam'.
These are not two different groups but the same group described in two ways.
3
Reflect on the warning
Read the second hadith about the rope of Islam. Ask yourself: what does it mean to separate from the community by a handspan? How does this relate to the saved group?
This warning shows the importance of communal unity within the bounds of Sunnah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم الحمد لله رب العالمين وصلى الله على سيدنا محمد خاتم النبيين والمرسلين وعلى آله وصحبه اجمعين ( وبعد ) فان سألت عن مذهب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم فمذهبه الطريق المستقيم كاقال النبي عليه السلام ان قوم موسى افترقوا من بعده احدى وسبعين فرقة فهلك سبعون وتخلص فرقة واحدة وقوم عيسى افترقوا من بعده اثنتين وسبعين فرقة فهلك احدى وسبعون وتخلص فرقة واحدة وان امتى ستفترق على ثلاث وسبعين فرقة فتهلك اثنتان وسبعون فرقة وتخلص فرقة واحدة ( قيل ) يارسول الله ومن تلك الفرقة قال اصحاب السنة والجماعة وهو السواد الاعظم ( قال ) رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم من خالف الجماعة قدر شير فقد خلع ربقة الاسلام من عنقه
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds, and blessings and peace be upon our master Muhammad, the seal of the prophets and messengers, and upon his family and all his companions. To proceed: If you ask about the way (madhhab) of the Prophet, peace be upon him, his way is the straight path. For the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: 'Indeed, the people of Moses split after him into seventy-one sects; seventy perished and one sect was saved. The people of Jesus split after him into seventy-two sects; seventy-one perished and one sect was saved. And my nation will split into seventy-three sects; seventy-two will perish and one sect will be saved.' It was said: 'O Messenger of Allah, who is that sect?' He said: 'The people of the Sunnah and the community (Ahl al-Sunna wal-Jama'a), and it is the greatest majority (al-Sawad al-Azam).' The Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, said: 'Whoever separates from the community by a handspan has cast the rope of Islam from his neck.'
Source pages p. 2

Commentary
This passage presents foundational hadiths about the division of the ummah. The author begins by stating that the Prophet's way is the straight path. He then reports that the Prophet informed that the Jews (people of Moses) split into 71 sects, only one saved; the Christians (people of Jesus) split into 72, only one saved; and this ummah will split into 73, with only one saved. When asked about the saved sect, the Prophet identified it as 'Ahl al-Sunna wal-Jama'a' (the people of the prophetic way and the community), also called 'al-Sawad al-Azam' (the greatest majority). The author then cites another hadith warning that anyone who separates from the community by even a handspan has removed the bond of Islam from his neck, meaning such separation is a serious sin. This establishes the authority and identity of the orthodox group.

Key Terms
السواد الأعظم
أهل السنة والجماعة
ربقة الإسلام

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the number 73 is literal or that the saved group is a specific named subgroup rather than defined by adherence to Sunnah and community.
Correction: The saved group is defined by its commitment to the Sunnah and unity with the community, not by a particular label or an exact numerical count.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith, how many sects will the nation of Muhammad (peace be upon him) split into, and which one is saved?
A) 71, the one that follows the Prophet
B) 72, the one that follows the companions
C) 73, the one called Ahl al-Sunna wal-Jama'a
D) 70, the one that follows the Quran

Key Takeaway
The Prophet explicitly identified the saved group as Ahl al-Sunna wal-Jama'a, the great majority, and warned that separating from them severs one's bond with Islam.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the Prophet explicitly identified the saved group as Ahl al-Sunna wal-Jama'a, what does that demand of you in terms of your loyalty to the mainstream Muslim community? Do you actively seek to remain connected, or do you allow pride in personal opinions to distance you? What is one concrete step you can take this week to strengthen your ties with the majority of Ahl al-Sunna?
?
The Prophet warned that separating from the community by even a handspan severs the rope of Islam from your neck. Can you recall a time when you justified holding a belief or practice that set you apart from the mainstream? How did that affect your sense of belonging to the ummah? What will you do today to realign yourself with the saved group?
?
The saved group is called 'the greatest majority'—not a fringe minority. Does your heart accept that salvation is found in being part of the large, ordinary community of believers, or do you secretly wish to be seen as special by holding a unique position? How does your ego react to the idea that simply following the Sunnah and staying with the Jamāʿah is enough?

Sects and Disagreements
beginner
3 min
The Virtue of Abu Bakr
Affirms Abu Bakr's excellence and rightful caliphate.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni belief that Abu Bakr is the most excellent person after the Prophet and that his caliphate is legitimate.

Core Idea
Abu Bakr is the best of the companions and the rightful caliph after the Prophet.

Imam Samarqandi begins a series of articles affirming key Sunni positions on the Companions. The first establishes the unparalleled merit of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, setting the foundation for a proper understanding of the caliphate. This lesson clarifies that merit and legitimacy go hand in hand.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text for article 25 carefully. Notice the phrase 'أحد من أصحابه ولا من أمته' which emphasizes universality.
The text uses 'يعلم' (knows) indicating certainty.
2
Identify the two claims
The passage makes two claims: 1) Abu Bakr is the most excellent after the Prophet among all Companions and the ummah. 2) His caliphate is true (rightful). Note that both are presented as necessary beliefs.
3
Reflect on the significance
Think about why this article is placed first among the caliphs. It establishes a hierarchy that counters both Shi'a (preferring Ali) and extremist Sunni views (elevating others above Abu Bakr).
This is a foundational article in Sunni political theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
( والخامسة والعشرون ) أن يعلم أنه لم يكن من بعد النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم احد من اصحابه ولا من امته افضل من ابي بكر الصديق رضى الله عنه ويرى خلافته حقا ( والسادسة والعشرون ( ان يرى ان افضل الناس بعد ابى بكر عمر بن الخطاب رضى الله عنه وبعده عثمان بن عفان وبعده على بن ابى طالب رضى الله عنهم ويرى خلافتهم حقا ( والسابعة والعشرون ( ان لا يقع في اصحاب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ولا يغتابهم ) والثامنة والعشرون ) ان يعتقد ان الله يغضب ويرضى لاحد من الورى ) والتاسعة والعشرون ) ان يعتقد ان رؤية الله تعالى بلا كيف حق ) والثلاثون ( ان يعتقد أن مراتب الأنبياء اعلى وافضل من مراتب الاولياء )
(25th) That he know that after the Prophet (peace be upon him) there was none from his Companions nor from his community more excellent than Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) and consider his caliphate true. (26th) That he consider that the most excellent of people after Abu Bakr is Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him), and after him Uthman ibn Affan, and after him Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with them all) and consider their caliphate true. (27th) That he not revile the Companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him) nor backbite them. (28th) That he believe that Allah becomes angry and is pleased with any of creation. (29th) That he believe that seeing Allah Almighty without how is true. (30th) That he believe that the ranks of the prophets are higher and more excellent than the ranks of the saints.
Source pages p. 3

Commentary
This passage covers articles 25-30. Articles 25 and 26 establish the hierarchy of the first four caliphs in order of excellence: Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali, each with the legitimacy of their caliphate. Article 27 prohibits reviling or backbiting any Companion. Article 28 affirms that Allah has attributes of wrath and pleasure towards creation – these are real, not metaphorical. Article 29 affirms the vision of Allah (ru'ya) in the Hereafter 'without how' (bila kayf), avoiding anthropomorphism. Article 30 states the superiority of prophets over saints, a key Sunni principle against excessive veneration of saints.

Key Terms
أبو بكر الصديق
خلافة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What does article 25 require a Sunni to believe about Abu Bakr?
A) He is equal to Umar
B) He is the most excellent after the Prophet
C) He is the most excellent after Ali
D) He is the first Muslim

Key Takeaway
A Sunni must know and believe that Abu Bakr is the most excellent person after the Prophet and that his caliphate is legitimate.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The passage commands you to 'know' that Abu Bakr is the most excellent after the Prophet. Is there any part of your heart that hesitates or gives preference to another? What will you do today to align your heart with this truth?
?
If you were to be asked by a sectarian who denies Abu Bakr's caliphate, would your belief be firm enough to respond with certainty? What investment have you made in learning the proofs of his superiority?
?
The passage says 'after the Prophet there was none ... more excellent than Abu Bakr'. Does your daily conduct reflect that you honour him as the second greatest human? How will you manifest this belief in your words and actions from now on?

The Hierarchy of the Rightly Guided Caliphs
Lists the order of excellence of the four caliphs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni order of excellence among the first four caliphs: Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali.

Core Idea
The caliphs are ranked in order of merit: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali.

After establishing the supreme merit of Abu Bakr, Imam Samarqandi lists the next three caliphs in order. This article defines the Sunni position on the relative virtues of the Companions, a key distinction from other sects that elevate Ali above the rest.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic text
Read article 26 carefully. Note the sequence: after Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali. The phrase 'ويرى خلافتهم حقاً' applies to all.
2
Observe the inclusive language
The article says 'أفضل الناس بعد أبي بكر' – notice 'الناس' (people) not just 'الصحابة' (Companions). This extends the hierarchy to all humanity.
3
Consider the theological implications
This hierarchy counters the Shi'a view that Ali was the most excellent after the Prophet. It also rejects the Kharijite position that Uthman and Ali were not rightful caliphs.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
( والخامسة والعشرون ) أن يعلم أنه لم يكن من بعد النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم احد من اصحابه ولا من امته افضل من ابي بكر الصديق رضى الله عنه ويرى خلافته حقا ( والسادسة والعشرون ( ان يرى ان افضل الناس بعد ابى بكر عمر بن الخطاب رضى الله عنه وبعده عثمان بن عفان وبعده على بن ابى طالب رضى الله عنهم ويرى خلافتهم حقا ( والسابعة والعشرون ( ان لا يقع في اصحاب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ولا يغتابهم ) والثامنة والعشرون ) ان يعتقد ان الله يغضب ويرضى لاحد من الورى ) والتاسعة والعشرون ) ان يعتقد ان رؤية الله تعالى بلا كيف حق ) والثلاثون ( ان يعتقد أن مراتب الأنبياء اعلى وافضل من مراتب الاولياء )
(25th) That he know that after the Prophet (peace be upon him) there was none from his Companions nor from his community more excellent than Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) and consider his caliphate true. (26th) That he consider that the most excellent of people after Abu Bakr is Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him), and after him Uthman ibn Affan, and after him Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with them all) and consider their caliphate true. (27th) That he not revile the Companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him) nor backbite them. (28th) That he believe that Allah becomes angry and is pleased with any of creation. (29th) That he believe that seeing Allah Almighty without how is true. (30th) That he believe that the ranks of the prophets are higher and more excellent than the ranks of the saints.
Source pages p. 3

Commentary
This passage covers articles 25-30. Articles 25 and 26 establish the hierarchy of the first four caliphs in order of excellence: Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali, each with the legitimacy of their caliphate. Article 27 prohibits reviling or backbiting any Companion. Article 28 affirms that Allah has attributes of wrath and pleasure towards creation – these are real, not metaphorical. Article 29 affirms the vision of Allah (ru'ya) in the Hereafter 'without how' (bila kayf), avoiding anthropomorphism. Article 30 states the superiority of prophets over saints, a key Sunni principle against excessive veneration of saints.

Key Terms
عمر بن الخطاب
عثمان بن عفان
على بن ابى طالب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to article 26, who is the most excellent after Abu Bakr?
A) Uthman
B) Ali
C) Umar
D) The Prophet

Key Takeaway
A Sunni must believe the order of excellence among the caliphs: Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali, and that each caliphate was legitimate.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever caught yourself feeling that this ranking of the caliphs contradicts your admiration for a particular Companion? Will you surrender your personal preference to this revealed truth?
?
How does your heart truly respond to the phrase 'ويرى خلافتهم حقاً'? Do you affirm Uthman's caliphate as fully legitimate despite the controversies, or is there a lingering doubt you need to uproot?
?
If you were to fully internalize that Abu Bakr is the most excellent after the Prophet, how would that change the way you speak about him and defend his place? Can you commit to teaching this order to your family without hesitation?

Prohibition of Reviling the Companions
Prohibits reviling and backbiting the Companions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that it is obligatory for a Sunni to refrain from reviling or backbiting any Companion of the Prophet.

Core Idea
Companions are to be honored, not reviled or backbitten.

Having established the hierarchy of the caliphs, Imam Samarqandi now lays down a universal rule applicable to all Companions. This article prohibits any form of verbal abuse or backbiting against the Prophet's Companions, protecting their honor as a matter of faith.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic
Article 27 uses two verbs: 'لا يقع' (he does not revile) and 'يغتاب' (backbite). Understand the difference.
2
Identify the prohibition
The article prohibits both open reviling and hidden backbiting. This covers all forms of disparagement.
3
Reflect on the scope
The prohibition applies to 'أصحاب النبي' (all Companions), not just the caliphs. This is a broad protection.

Source Text

Allah's Anger and Pleasure with Creation
Affirms Allah's attributes of anger and pleasure.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni belief that Allah has real attributes of anger and pleasure towards creation.

Core Idea
Allah's anger and pleasure are real, not metaphorical.

Imam Samarqandi now turns to divine attributes. This article affirms that Allah becomes angry and pleased with certain individuals from creation. This counters those who interpret such attributes as mere metaphors, insisting they are real without anthropomorphism.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic
Note the phrase 'يغضب ويرضى' (becomes angry and is pleased). The verb forms indicate action.
2
Understand the scope
Allah's anger and pleasure are directed 'لأحد من الورى' (to any of creation). This includes believers and disbelievers appropriately.
3
Reflect on why this matters
Affirming these attributes upholds the Sunni position of tafwid (consigning meaning to Allah) while avoiding ta'til (denial of attributes).

Source Text

The Vision of Allah Without How
Affirms the vision of Allah without modality.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni belief that the believers will see Allah in the Hereafter, but without any modality (bila kayf).

Core Idea
Seeing Allah in the afterlife is true, but not through physical vision or comprehension.

The vision of Allah (ru'ya) is a central eschatological belief. Imam Samarqandi asserts it as true, but immediately qualifies it with 'bila kayf' (without how), a safeguard against anthropomorphism. This lesson explains why both affirmation and qualification are necessary.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic
Note the phrase 'رؤية الله تعالى بلا كيف حق'. The three key elements: ru'ya (vision), Allah ta'ala, bila kayf (without how), haqq (true).
2
Understand the qualification
Bila kayf means we do not ask how Allah will be seen. We affirm the vision but reject any physical or spatial interpretation.
3
Connect to Sunni theology
This is a middle path between those who deny vision (Mu'tazila) and those who anthropomorphize (Mushabbiha).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
( والخامسة والعشرون ) أن يعلم أنه لم يكن من بعد النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم احد من اصحابه ولا من امته افضل من ابي بكر الصديق رضى الله عنه ويرى خلافته حقا ( والسادسة والعشرون ( ان يرى ان افضل الناس بعد ابى بكر عمر بن الخطاب رضى الله عنه وبعده عثمان بن عفان وبعده على بن ابى طالب رضى الله عنهم ويرى خلافتهم حقا ( والسابعة والعشرون ( ان لا يقع في اصحاب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ولا يغتابهم ) والثامنة والعشرون ) ان يعتقد ان الله يغضب ويرضى لاحد من الورى ) والتاسعة والعشرون ) ان يعتقد ان رؤية الله تعالى بلا كيف حق ) والثلاثون ( ان يعتقد أن مراتب الأنبياء اعلى وافضل من مراتب الاولياء )
(25th) That he know that after the Prophet (peace be upon him) there was none from his Companions nor from his community more excellent than Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) and consider his caliphate true. (26th) That he consider that the most excellent of people after Abu Bakr is Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him), and after him Uthman ibn Affan, and after him Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with them all) and consider their caliphate true. (27th) That he not revile the Companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him) nor backbite them. (28th) That he believe that Allah becomes angry and is pleased with any of creation. (29th) That he believe that seeing Allah Almighty without how is true. (30th) That he believe that the ranks of the prophets are higher and more excellent than the ranks of the saints.

Article 30 states the superiority of prophets over saints, a key Sunni principle against excessive veneration of saints.

B) It is true and without modality

?

Superiority of Prophets over Saints
Affirms the superiority of prophets over saints.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni belief that the ranks of the prophets are higher than those of the saints.

Core Idea
Prophets are superior to all saints, regardless of the saint's piety.

The final article in this passage clarifies a point of potential confusion: while saints are honored, their status never exceeds that of prophets. This guards against exaggerated veneration of awliya that might undermine prophetic primacy.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic
Note the comparative 'أعلى وأفضل' (higher and more excellent). The article asserts both height of rank and excellence.
2
Understand the scope
The superiority is of 'مراتب الأنبياء' over 'مراتب الأولياء' – the entire category of prophets over saints.
3
Reflect on the intention
This prevents the extremist idea that a saint could reach prophetic status. Even the greatest saint (e.g., Abu Bakr) remains below the lowest prophet.

Source Text

?

?

beginner

Iblis and the Pre-Islamic Status of Abu Bakr and Umar
Two articles on temporal states of belief and disbelief.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Iblis was once a believer and that Abu Bakr and Umar were disbelievers before Islam, affirming that disbelief applies to pre-Islamic polytheists.

Core Idea
Faith and disbelief are judged by Allah based on one's state, not later status.

Imam Samarqandi presents two articles that clarify the status of individuals before and after their actions. The first deals with Iblis, who worshipped Allah but later fell into disbelief. The second addresses the two greatest Companions, affirming that they were disbelievers when they worshipped idols before Islam. This helps us understand that divine judgment is based on actual belief at the time.

Guided Path
1
Read the two articles
Read the Arabic and translation carefully. Notice that both articles use the phrase 'at the time of' (waqtama) to indicate a specific period.
2
Understand the case of Iblis
Reflect on how Iblis was a believer while worshipping Allah. This shows that his later disbelief was due to arrogance, not original creation.
3
Understand the case of Abu Bakr and Umar
Recognize that before Islam, they were disbelievers like any other polytheist. Their later greatness does not erase their past disbelief.
This counters claims that they were never disbelievers.
4
Consider the theological implication
These articles teach that faith and disbelief are determined by one's actual state at the time, not by later reputation or divine foreknowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
( والسابعة والخمسون) ان يعلم ان ابليس لعنه الله لما كان يعبد الله كان مؤمنا عند الله وعند الملائكة ) والثامنة والخمسون ) ان يعلم ان ابابكر وعمر وقتا ما كانا يعبدان الصنم كانا كافرين عند الله وعند ملائكته ) والتاسعة والخمسون ) ان يعلم ان الامر لا يسقط عن المحب من اجل المحبة ) والستون ) ان يرى ان القنوط من رحمة الله تعالى كفر ) والحادية والستون ) ان يرى خوف الخاتمة من الله تعالى حقا
(57th) That he know that Iblis (may Allah curse him) when he was worshipping Allah was a believer in the sight of Allah and the angels. (58th) That he know that Abu Bakr and Umar when they were worshipping the idol were disbelievers in the sight of Allah and His angels. (59th) That he know that the command does not fall from the beloved [meaning?] on account of love. (60th) That he consider that despair of the mercy of Allah Almighty is disbelief. (61st) That he consider that fear of the evil end from Allah Almighty is true.
Source pages p. 4

Commentary
This passage covers articles 57-61. Article 57 states that Iblis (Satan) was a believer when he worshipped Allah, implying his disbelief came later due to arrogance. Article 58 states that Abu Bakr and Umar, before Islam, when they worshipped idols, were disbelievers in Allah's sight. This affirms that disbelief applies to pre-Islamic polytheists, even those who later became the best of Muslims. Article 59 says 'the command does not fall from the beloved on account of love' – meaning that love for someone does not cancel divine commands or accountability. Article 60 declares that despair of Allah's mercy is kufr (disbelief), a severe warning. Article 61 says that fear of the evil end (su' al-khatima) from Allah is true – i.e., one should fear ending one's life in disbelief, which is a valid concern.

Key Terms
ابليس
ابابكر وعمر قبل الاسلام

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that Abu Bakr and Umar were never disbelievers, even before Islam.
Correction: Article 58 explicitly states they were disbelievers when worshipping idols, affirming their pre-Islamic polytheism.

Checkpoint
According to article 57, what was Iblis' status when he worshipped Allah?
A) Disbeliever
B) Believer
C) Uncertain
D) Neither

Key Takeaway
A person's faith or disbelief is judged by Allah based on their actual state at that time, regardless of their later destiny.

Love, Despair, and Fear of the End
Three articles on love, despair, and fear in relation to faith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that love for someone does not cancel divine commands, that despair of Allah's mercy is disbelief, and that fear of a bad end is a true religious concern.

Core Idea
Love does not override obligation; despair is unbelief; fear of the end is truth.

Imam Samarqandi presents three more articles that deal with inner states and their relation to faith. He teaches that love for someone does not negate the commands of Allah, that losing hope in Allah's mercy is itself an act of disbelief, and that fearing one's final fate is a valid and true emotion. These are essential for maintaining a balanced spiritual life.

Guided Path
1
Read the three articles
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the distinct topics: love, despair, and fear.
2
Understand article 59
Reflect on how love for someone does not exempt them from following Allah's commands. This counters the idea that beloved individuals are above the law.
The phrase 'al-amr la yasqut 'an al-muhibb' means divine commands are binding regardless of love.
3
Understand article 60
Learn that despair of Allah's mercy is kufr. This is a strong warning against losing hope, which implies disbelief in Allah's power to forgive.
4
Understand article 61
Recognize that fear of an evil end (su' al-khatima) is true and should be taken seriously. It encourages one to remain faithful until death.

?

?
Are you truly afraid of dying in disbelief? That fear is true—what one action does it demand from you right now to secure your end?

The Validity of Praying Behind Any Imam
Ruling on praying behind righteous and sinful imams with hadith evidence

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that according to Imam Samarqandi, prayer behind a Muslim imam is valid regardless of his personal sins, provided he is not an innovator or disbeliever, and that this ruling is supported by prophetic hadith.

Core Idea
Pray behind any Muslim imam; only innovation breaks the line.

This lesson addresses a practical question: Should you pray behind an imam known to sin? Imam Samarqandi clarifies the ruling, emphasizing that sinfulness does not invalidate the prayer, but innovation and disbelief do. He even warns against rejecting this position, calling those who do so innovators. A supporting hadith reinforces the principle of not excommunicating fellow Muslims.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the repeated phrase 'every righteous and sinful' and the exception for innovators.
The text contains some OCR uncertainties; focus on the clear rulings.
2
Identify the exceptions
The author states prayer is permissible behind anyone except an innovator or a disbeliever. Find that condition in the text.
The exception is critical: innovation, not sin, invalidates the imamate.
3
Understand the polemic
The author criticizes the Rafidah for rejecting prayer behind sinful imams. Consider why this ruling matters for community unity and avoiding sectarianism.
The Rafidah are a Shiite group; the author refutes their position.
4
Examine the prophetic hadith
Read the four teachings of the Prophet narrated by Mahmud al-Shami. The third teaching directly supports the ruling: 'pray behind every imam'.
The hadith also includes not excommunicating Muslims and praying funeral prayers over them.
5
Reflect on implications
Think about how this ruling affects your practice: should you avoid praying behind an imam with a sinful reputation? The answer is no, unless he is an innovator or disbeliever.
This teaches tolerance and unity among Muslims.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثالثة وما ذكرنا انه ينبغي للمؤمن ان يرى الصلاة خلف كل بروفاجر [؟] حقا ولا يكون مثل الر ، افض [؟] لأنهم لا يصلون خلف كل بروفاجر ولا يرونها حقا ) واعلم ( ان الصلاة جائزة خلف كل احد برا كان او فاجرا زانيا كان او شارب الخمر يحيث لا يكون مبتدعا لان الصلاة خلف المبتدع والكافر غير جائزة ومن لم ير الصلاة خلف كل بروفاجر [؟] نه و مبتدع ) اخبرنا ( الثقات بإسنادهم عن محمود الشامى انه قال لاصحابه في مرضه الذي مات فيه أربعة لم احدثكموهن عن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم فانا محد ثمكم [؟] اليوم قال قال رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم لا تكفروا اهل قبلتكم وأن عملوا الكبيرة والصلاة على كل ميت والصلاة خلف كل امام والجهاد مع كل اميرالى آخر الحديث
The third question. And what we have mentioned, that it is befitting for the believer to consider prayer behind every righteous and sinful [imam] as valid, and not to be like the Rafidah, because they do not pray behind every righteous and sinful and do not consider it valid. And know: prayer is permissible behind anyone, righteous or sinful, even an adulterer or a wine-drinker, as long as he is not an innovator, because prayer behind an innovator and a disbeliever is not permissible. And whoever does not consider prayer behind every righteous and sinful as valid is an innovator. The trustworthy informed us with their chains from Mahmud al-Shami that he said to his companions in the illness in which he died: 'Four things I have not narrated to you from the Prophet, peace be upon him, and today I will narrate them: The Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, said: Do not excommunicate the people of your qibla even if they commit major sins, and [pray] the funeral prayer over every deceased, and pray behind every imam, and strive (jihad) with every ruler.' to the end of the hadith.
Source pages p. 6

Commentary
This passage is a ruling on the permissibility of praying behind any imam, regardless of his piety or sinfulness, as long as he is not an innovator. The author explains that the believer should consider it valid, unlike the Rafidah (a Shiite group) who do not pray behind sinful imams. The rule is that prayer is valid behind any Muslim imam, even if he is an adulterer or wine-drinker, provided he is not an innovator (mubtadi') or disbeliever. Anyone who denies this is himself an innovator. The author then narrates a hadith from Mahmud al-Shami who, on his deathbed, reveals four teachings from the Prophet: (1) do not excommunicate Muslims who commit major sins, (2) pray funeral prayer over every deceased Muslim, (3) pray behind every imam, and (4) perform jihad with every ruler. This hadith supports the ruling.

Key Terms
الصلاة خلف الامام
البر والفاجر
المبتدع
الكافر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that one should not pray behind a sinful imam or that the prayer is invalid.
Correction: The passage clarifies that prayer behind any Muslim imam is valid, even if he is sinful, as long as he is not an innovator or disbeliever.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, behind which type of imam is prayer not permissible?
A) A righteous imam
B) A sinful imam who drinks wine
C) An innovator (mubtadi')
D) An imam who commits adultery

Key Takeaway
Prayer behind any Muslim imam is valid as long as he is not an innovator or disbeliever, reflecting the principle of not excommunicating people of the qibla.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt reluctant to pray behind someone because you judged their character? How does this hadith challenge your pride and call you to humility?
?
Is there a Muslim community or person you have mentally 'excommunicated' due to their sins? What concrete step can you take today to restore the bond of brotherhood and prayer unity?
?
The Prophet commanded prayer behind every imam and forbade excommunication. Are you willing to follow an imam whose lifestyle you disapprove of? What does that reveal about your reliance on Allah's acceptance rather than human perfection?

Obedience to the Sultan and Validity of Praying Behind Rulers
Samarqandi rules on praying behind any ruler and forbids rebellion, citing Quran.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that obedience to the sultan is obligatory even if he is sinful, that praying behind any ruler is valid, and that rebelling against him is forbidden, with the Quranic basis for this ruling.

Core Idea
Loyalty to the sultan is a religious duty, not a political option.

This passage from Imam Samarqandi addresses a critical issue of political theology: the relationship of the Muslim community to its rulers. The author teaches that the obligation to obey authority remains intact regardless of the ruler's personal piety, and that rebellion is a deviation from the path of Ahl al-Sunna. Let's explore his arguments and the Quranic evidence he provides.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once carefully, then read the English translation. Notice the concise style and the key terms.
2
Identify the three key rulings
The passage contains three interlinked rulings: (1) the validity of praying 'Id and Friday behind any ruler, (2) the obligation to obey the sultan, and (3) the prohibition of rebellion. Identify each and see how they connect.
Note the strong language: 'prays behind every ruler' and 'no rebellion with sword or disobedience'.
3
Understand the Quranic proof
The author cites Quran 4:59, interpreting 'those in authority' as the sultans. Reflect on how this verse grounds the obligation to obey.
Consider how other scholars have interpreted this verse, but stick to the author's meaning here.
4
Reflect on the sectarian implication
The author labels those who disobey the sultan as 'Kharijites'. Think about why this label is used and what it reveals about the Sunni position on political obedience.
The Kharijites historically rebelled against unjust rulers; this passage opposes that stance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة التاسعة: وما ذكرنا أنه يصلى خلف كل أمير صلاة العيدين والجمعة، ويبرأ حقا لأن طاعة السلطان فريضة، وإن كان مثقوب الأذنين. ولا يجوز الخروج عليه بالسيف ولا بالعصيان له. فإن عدل كان الأجر له، وإن ظلم كان الوزر عليه. ولا بد من طاعة السلطان بكل حال، لأن من عصى السلطان ولم يعطه فهو خارجي لقوله تعالى: {أطيعوا الله وأطيعوا الرسول وأولي الأمر منكم} يعني السلاطين.
The ninth question: And what we mentioned is that one prays behind every ruler (amir) the prayers of the two 'Ids and Friday, and it is correct because obedience to the sultan is an obligation (farida), even if he has pierced ears (i.e., is a sinner or of low status). And it is not permissible to rebel against him with the sword or by disobeying him. For if he is just, the reward is for him; if he is unjust, the burden (wizr) is upon him. And obedience to the sultan is necessary in every case, because whoever disobeys the sultan and does not give him his due is a Khariji (outsider), by His saying: 'Obey Allah, obey the Messenger, and those in authority among you' meaning the sultans.
Source pages p. 10

Commentary
This passage is a ruling on political obedience. The author declares that it is valid to pray the 'Id and Friday prayers behind any ruler, regardless of his personal righteousness, even if he is a flagrant sinner ('pierced ears' is an expression for a dissolute person). He then states that rebellion (khuruj) against the ruler is forbidden, both by armed revolt and by disobedience. The leader's personal conduct does not affect the obligation to obey; if he is just, he gets reward; if unjust, he incurs sin, but the subject must still obey. To disobey and withhold allegiance is to follow the Kharijite path, who declared rebellion against unjust rulers permissible. The author grounds this in the Quranic verse commanding obedience to 'those in authority' (ulu al-amr), which he interprets as the sultans.

Key Terms
طاعة السلطان
خروج
أولي الأمر
مثقوب الأذنين
خارجي
جمعة
عيد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that prayers behind a sinful or unjust ruler are invalid, and that rebellion against such a ruler is permissible or even obligatory.
Correction: The passage affirms that prayer behind any ruler is valid and that obedience is obligatory regardless of his personal state. Rebellion is categorically forbidden.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling on praying behind an unjust ruler?
A) It is invalid; one should rebel against him.
B) It is valid, and obedience to him is obligatory.
C) It is valid only if he is just.
D) It is forbidden to pray behind any ruler.

Key Takeaway
Obedience to the sultan is obligatory in all circumstances, and rebellion is a path of the Kharijites.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you see injustice in your community or country, does your heart incline toward criticism or rebellion? How does this passage call you to a different response—one of obedience while leaving the ruler's account with Allah? Are you willing to carry that tension without acting on your desire to disobey?
?
The author says that even if the ruler has 'pierced ears'—a sinner—you must still obey. Is there someone in authority over you whom you disrespect or disregard because of their flaws? Will you repent from that and genuinely seek to obey them for Allah's sake, while keeping your own soul accountable?
?
The Kharijites rebelled based on a principle they thought was right. Where in your own life do you justify small acts of rebellion—against parents, teachers, or leaders—by focusing on their faults? What step will you take today to correct that attitude and submit to legitimate authority, trusting Allah's justice?

The Ten Companions Guaranteed Paradise
Samarqandi affirms that the ten Companions named by the Prophet are destined for Paradise.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to testify that the ten Companions named by the Prophet are in Paradise, and the gravity of doubting or denying this.

Core Idea
Testifying for the ten Companions' Paradise is obligatory; denial is innovation.

In this passage, Imam al-Samarqandi addresses a key point of Sunni belief: the ten Companions whom the Prophet explicitly named as inhabitants of Paradise. The author makes clear that acknowledging this is not optional—it is a required article of faith. Whoever includes or doubts any of them falls into misguidance. Let us examine the text and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation to grasp the direct statement. Note the strong language used.
2
Identify the ruling
Focus on the phrase 'فمن ضمن فيهم أو في أحد منهم فإنه ضال مبتدع'. Understand that 'ضمن' here implies doubt or denial. The ruling is clear: such a person is misguided and an innovator.
3
Reflect on the list
The Prophet named himself and nine others. Consider why this explicit naming is significant for Sunni creed and how it distinguishes from other sects.
The list includes the four rightly-guided caliphs and other eminent Companions.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما قلنا أنه ينبغي له أن يشهد للعشرة من أصحاب رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم بالجنة، فمن ضمن فيهم أو في أحد منهم فإنه ضال مبتدع. سماهم رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم فقال: أنا وأبو بكر وعمر وعثمان وعلي وطلحة والزبير وسعد وسعيد وعبد الرحمن بن عوف وأبو عبيدة بن الجراح في الجنة. رضوان الله عليهم أجمعين. وهذا القدر كفاية للعاقل.
And what we said: that he ought to testify for the ten from the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وسلم with Paradise. So whoever includes them or includes one of them [in doubt or denial] is misguided and an innovator. The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وسلم named them and said: 'I, Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, Talha, al-Zubayr, Sa'd, Sa'id, Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, and Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah are in Paradise.' May Allah be pleased with them all. And this amount is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 16

Commentary
This passage addresses the belief in the ten Companions who were given glad tidings of Paradise (al-ashara al-mubashshara). The author states that one must testify that they are in Paradise. Whoever includes them (i.e., doubts or denies) or includes any one of them is 'ضال مبتدع' (misguided and an innovator). The Prophet himself named them: himself, then Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, Talha, al-Zubayr, Sa'd, Sa'id (probably Sa'id ibn Zayd), Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, and Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah. This list includes the first four caliphs and others. The author concludes that this is sufficient for the intelligent.

Key Terms
عشرة مبشرون
شهادة بالجنة
ضال مبتدع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that testifying for Paradise for the ten Companions is optional or that denying it is not a major error.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that whoever includes (doubts/denies) them is misguided and an innovator, making it obligatory to affirm.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling on one who doubts that any of the ten Companions are in Paradise?
A) He is a disbeliever.
B) He is misguided and an innovator.
C) He is still a believer but sinful.
D) He is excused because they are not mentioned in the Quran.

Key Takeaway
It is obligatory to believe that the ten Companions named by the Prophet are in Paradise; denying or doubting this is innovation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that these ten Companions are in Paradise? If so, how does that affect your love for them? If not, what does that say about your acceptance of the Prophet's words?
?
The author says denying one of them is innovation. Have you ever doubted the status of any Companion? What would it take for you to correct that?
?
These ten were promised Paradise, but what about you? Are you living as if you have a similar guarantee? This lesson reminds us that testimony is based on revelation, not assumption. How can you earn a share in that promise through action?

Abu Bakr's Superiority and Caliphate
Samarqandi affirms Abu Bakr's superiority and supports it with the cave verse.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Abu Bakr is considered the most excellent of the Companions and that his caliphate is supported by Quranic evidence.

Core Idea
Abu Bakr is the best of the Companions, confirmed by the Quran.

In this passage, Imam Samarqandi presents a core belief of Ahl al-Sunnah: the superiority of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq over all other Companions. He not only states this as a matter of necessary knowledge but also provides a direct scriptural proof from the Quran. As we study this text, we will see how the verse of the cave serves as a decisive evidence for Abu Bakr’s excellence and rightful succession.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to grasp the author's claim.
Notice the emphatic language: 'لم يكن... أحد... أفضل من أبي بكر'.
2
Identify the two parts of the passage
The passage has a clear two-part structure: first a statement of belief about Abu Bakr's superiority and caliphate, then a proof from the Quran.
The second part begins with 'واعلم أن فضل أبي بكر...'.
3
Examine the Quranic proof
The author cites Surah al-Tawba, verse 40, which mentions Abu Bakr as the 'second of two' in the cave. Reflect on how this establishes his virtue.
This verse is used by scholars to show Abu Bakr's special companionship with the Prophet.
4
Connect to the broader creed
Consider why this belief is important: it affirms the correct order of the Khulafa al-Rashidun and protects against innovations.
Ahl al-Sunnah hold that the best after the Prophet are Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما قلنا أنه ينبغي له أن يعلم أنه لم يكن من بعد النبي عليه السلام أحد من الصحابة ولا من أمته أفضل من أبي بكر الصديق رضي الله عنه، ويراه حقا بعد النبي عليه السلام خليفة على الحق حقا. واعلم أن فضل أبي بكر قد صح وثبت بالكتاب والخبر. أما الكتاب فقوله تعالى: {ثاني اثنين إذ هما في الغار إذ يقول لصاحبه لا تحزن إن الله معنا}.
And what we said: that he ought to know that there was not after the Prophet عليه السلام anyone from the Companions nor from his nation more excellent than Abu Bakr al-Siddiq رضي الله عنه, and to consider him truly as the caliph after the Prophet upon truth, rightfully. And know that the excellence of Abu Bakr is established and confirmed by the Book and the Sunnah. As for the Book, it is His saying the Exalted: {The second of two when they were in the cave, when he said to his companion, 'Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us.}
Source pages p. 16

Commentary
This passage asserts the superiority of Abu Bakr over all other Companions and affirms his rightful caliphate. The author states it is obligatory to know that after the Prophet, no one from the Companions or the ummah is more excellent than Abu Bakr. He must be considered the true caliph after the Prophet. The proof is from both Quran and hadith: the Quranic verse 'The second of two when they were in the cave' (al-Tawba 40) is cited, referring to Abu Bakr as the companion in the cave. This verse establishes his virtue. The rest of the proof (the Sunnah part) is not included in this window (likely on the next page).

Key Terms
أبو بكر الصديق
أفضل الصحابة
خلافة
ثاني اثنين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may believe that another Companion (e.g., Ali or Umar) is superior to Abu Bakr.
Correction: The Quranic verse of the cave and the consensus of Ahl al-Sunnah establish Abu Bakr's superiority.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, who is the most excellent of the Companions after the Prophet?
A) Umar ibn al-Khattab
B) Uthman ibn Affan
C) Ali ibn Abi Talib
D) Abu Bakr al-Siddiq

Key Takeaway
Abu Bakr al-Siddiq is the most virtuous of the Companions and the rightful successor to the Prophet, as proven by the Quranic verse of the cave.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read the verse 'Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us,' does your heart resonate with the same calm certainty that the Prophet demonstrated? In what area of your life right now are you failing to trust that Allah is truly 'with you'? What one step will you take this week to act on that belief?
?
Do you genuinely accept that Abu Bakr, a man you never met, is the best of this ummah after the Prophet? Or do you subtly elevate your own opinions or modern figures above the divine ranking? How would your words and respect for the Companions change if you truly believed the Quran itself singles out Abu Bakr?
?
If asked to defend the belief in Abu Bakr's caliphate without resorting to sectarian slogans, could you articulate it using the very words of the Quran? Are you prepared to stand before Allah with a creed built on revelation rather than blind following? What do you need to learn more deeply to be confident in this?

The Virtue of Umar ibn al-Khattab
Samarqandi proves Umar's superiority after Abu Bakr using Quran and Sunnah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Quranic and prophetic proofs for Umar's superiority after Abu Bakr.

Core Idea
Umar's virtue is proven by two divine sources.

In this lesson, we examine the Sunnī doctrine regarding the status of ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb. Imam al-Samarqandī establishes that after Abū Bakr, ʿUmar is the most excellent of the Companions, and he supports this claim with explicit evidence from the Qurʾān and the Sunna.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Let the author's claim and evidence come through.
2
Identify the Qur’anic proof
Find the verse cited and note how the author specifies it refers to Umar based on a particular interpretation.
The verse is 'O Prophet, sufficient is Allah for you and those who follow you of the believers.'
3
Identify the two hadith proofs
The author presents two hadiths: one about the muhaddath (inspired speaker) and another about the two viziers. Locate both in the Arabic.
4
Reflect on the significance
Ask yourself why this doctrine is important for Sunni creed. How do these proofs establish Umar's rank after Abu Bakr?
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وماذ كرنا أنه ينبغى له ان يعلم انه لم يكن من امة محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم بعد ابي بكر الصديق رضى الله عنه افضل من عمر بن الخطاب رضى الله عنه وبراء خليفة حقا بعد ابى بكر رضى الله تعالى عنه واعلم ان فضل عمر بن الخطاب رضى الله عنه قد صح وبين بالكتاب والسنة اما الكتاب فقوله تعالى يا ايها النبي حسبك ومن أبعك من المؤمنين وهو عمر بن الخطاب رضى الله عنه وقال النبي عليه السلام لم تك امة قبل امتى الاوكان فيها محدث ومحدث امتى هو عمر وقال عليه السلام أن لى وزيرين فى الارض اما الوزيران اللذان فى السماء فهما جبريل وميكائيل واما الوزيران اللذان فى الارض فيهما ابو بكر وعمر رضى الله عنهما وهذا كفاية للعاقل
We mentioned that it is necessary for him to know that there was not, in the nation of Muhammad (peace be upon him) after Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him), anyone more virtuous than Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him), and he considers him a rightful caliph after Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him). Know that the virtue of Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) is established and clarified by the Book and the Sunnah. As for the Book, Allah Most High says: 'O Prophet, sufficient is Allah for you and those who follow you of the believers' – and he is Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him). The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'There was no nation before my nation except that there was a muhaddath (inspired speaker) in it, and the muhaddath of my nation is Umar.' And he (peace be upon him) said: 'I have two viziers on earth; as for the two viziers in heaven, they are Jibril and Mika'il, and as for the two viziers on earth, they are Abu Bakr and Umar (may Allah be pleased with them both).' This is sufficient for the intelligent person.
Source pages p. 17

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal proof establishing the superiority of Umar ibn al-Khattab over the rest of the Companions after Abu Bakr. It begins by stating the obligatory belief: one must know that no one in the Muslim nation after Abu Bakr is more excellent than Umar, and that Umar is a rightful caliph. The author then proves this virtue through two sources: the Quran and the Sunnah. The Quranic evidence is the verse 'O Prophet, sufficient is Allah for you and those who follow you of the believers' – the author identifies 'those who follow you' specifically as Umar (a tafsir attributed to some early scholars). The prophetic evidence consists of two hadiths: first, that every nation had a muhaddath (a person divinely inspired with correct opinions), and that Umar is that of this nation; second, that the Prophet's two viziers on earth are Abu Bakr and Umar, just as Jibril and Mika'il are his viziers in heaven. The passage concludes that this proof is sufficient for any rational person.

Key Terms
فضل
خلافة
محدث
وزيران

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The verse 'sufficient for you and those who follow you' refers to all believers generally.
Correction: Samarqandi specifies it as referring to Umar based on a specific interpretation, not all believers.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which two individuals are the Prophet's viziers on earth?
A) Jibril and Mika'il
B) Abu Bakr and Umar
C) Umar and Uthman
D) Abu Bakr and Ali

Key Takeaway
Umar ibn al-Khattab is the most virtuous Companion after Abu Bakr, as proven by Quran and Sunnah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

What specific change can you make today to align your heart with what Allah and His Messenger have declared?
?
The author says 'this is sufficient for the intelligent person.' Are you that intelligent person? Have you truly satisfied your heart with these proofs, or do you still harbor doubts about the order of the caliphs? Commit to reciting this evidence until it settles in your heart.
?
If you were asked to defend Umar's caliphate using only the Quran and hadith from this passage, could you do it? Practice explaining this to someone, and see if your own conviction deepens.

The Virtue of Uthman ibn Affan
Samarqandi affirms Uthman's superiority after Abu Bakr and Umar.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni position on the ranking of the rightly guided caliphs and the evidence for Uthman's virtue after Abu Bakr and Umar.

Core Idea
The Prophet explicitly ranked Uthman as the third most virtuous after Abu Bakr and Umar.

Imam Samarqandi presents a foundational Sunni belief: the order of virtue among the Companions, specifically that Uthman ibn Affan holds the third rank after Abu Bakr and Umar. This lesson examines the evidence from the Prophet's own words and the warning against disputing their status.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage carefully
Read the Arabic text once without analyzing. Then read the literal translation to grasp the author's direct statement.
Notice that the passage begins with a required belief and then provides evidence.
2
Identify the required belief
Find the statement that one must know that Uthman is the most virtuous after Abu Bakr and Umar. Understand that this is a matter of creed.
The word 'ينبغي' (ought) indicates obligation.
3
Examine the prophetic hadith
The Prophet names the four most virtuous: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali. Note the order and that Ali is included as fourth, not excluded.
This hadith is the primary proof for the ranking.
4
Reflect on the warning
The Prophet forbids speaking ill of them, warning that it leads to division. This shows the importance of respecting all Companions.
The warning reinforces the need to accept this ranking without dispute.
5
Summarize the lesson
Conclude that Uthman's virtue is established by clear textual evidence and that the intelligent person accepts it.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما نانا انه يذني له ان يعلم أنه ليس احد في هذه الامة بعد ان بكر وعمر رضى الله عنهما افضل من عثمان بن عفان رضی الله عنه و راه بعد هما خليفة حقا وفضله ظاهر في قوله عليه السلام ان افضل هذه الامة بعدى أبو بكر وعمر وعثمان وعلى رضى الله عنهم اجمعين ثم قال لا تنطقوا فيهم ولا تقولن الاخيرا كلانشقوا وهذا كفاية للعاقل
We mentioned that it is necessary for him to know that there is no one in this nation after Abu Bakr and Umar (may Allah be pleased with them both) more virtuous than Uthman ibn Affan (may Allah be pleased with him), and he considers him a rightful caliph after them both. His virtue is evident in his (peace be upon him) statement: 'The most virtuous of this nation after me are Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ali (may Allah be pleased with them all).' Then he said: 'Do not speak about them, and say only good, lest you split.' This is sufficient for the intelligent person.
Source pages p. 17

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal proof establishing Uthman's rank as the third most excellent companion after Abu Bakr and Umar. The author begins by stating the required belief: one must know that after Abu Bakr and Umar, the most virtuous person in the Muslim nation is Uthman, and that he is a rightful caliph. The proof is a hadith in which the Prophet (peace be upon him) explicitly names the four best of his nation in order: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, then Ali. The Prophet then warns against speaking ill of them or causing division. The passage concludes that this is sufficient for a rational person.

Key Terms
فضل
خلافة
صحابة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the hadith includes only the first three caliphs, excluding Ali.
Correction: The hadith explicitly includes Ali as the fourth, confirming the order of virtue.

Checkpoint

C) Abu Bakr, Umar, Ali, and Hasan
D) Umar, Uthman, Ali, and Talha

Key Takeaway
Uthman ibn Affan is the third most virtuous companion after Abu Bakr and Umar, as established by explicit prophetic statement.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
The passage calls this 'sufficient for the intelligent person.' Is your heart fully satisfied with this prophetic proof, or do you still feel the need for more arguments? What does your lingering doubt reveal about your trust in the Prophet's words, and what will you do to strengthen it?

The Virtue of Ali ibn Abi Talib Among the Companions
Samarqandi proves Ali's superiority after the first three caliphs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al‑Samarqandi establishes the rank of Ali ibn Abi Talib after Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman using a Quranic interpretation.

Core Idea
Ali’s virtue after the first three caliphs is confirmed by Quranic allusion and Sunni tradition.

In this passage from al‑Sawad al‑A‘zam, Imam al‑Samarqandi affirms the excellence of Ali ibn Abi Talib after the first three caliphs. He bases his argument on a traditional interpretation of a Quranic verse and warns against speaking ill of any companion. Let’s examine his proof and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Note that the Arabic may contain minor OCR errors; the meaning is clear.
2
Identify the doctrinal claim
The author states a required belief: one must know that after Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman, no one is more virtuous than Ali, and he is a rightful caliph. This is the central claim.
This claim is a Sunni position on the ranking of the caliphs.
3
Analyze the Quranic proof
The author interprets each phrase of Quran 48:29 as referring to a specific caliph. See how each phrase is matched: 'those with him' → Abu Bakr, 'severe against disbelievers' → Umar, 'merciful among themselves' → Uthman, 'bowing and prostrating' → Ali. This allegorical tafsir is the evidence for Ali's virtue.
This interpretation is not universal; it is a traditional Sunni view.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وماقلنا انه ينبني له ان يعلم انه لم يكن في هذه الامة ولا في الصحابة بعد ابي بكر وعمر و عثمان افضل من على بن ابى طالب رضى الله عنهم اجمعين ويراء خليفة حقا وفضله مبين فى قوله تعالى محمد رسول الله والذين معه يعنى ابابكر اشداء على الكفار يعنى عمر رحماء بينهم يعنى عثمان تراهم ركما سجدا يعنى على بن ابي طالب رضى الله عنهم اجمعين فانظر لاتقولن فيهم الاخير الكيلا تلمن وهذا كفاية للعاقل
We said that it is necessary for him to know that there was not in this nation nor among the companions after Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman anyone more virtuous than Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with them all), and he considers him a rightful caliph. His virtue is evident in the saying of Allah Most High: 'Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah; and those with him' – meaning Abu Bakr – 'are severe against the disbelievers' – meaning Umar – 'merciful among themselves' – meaning Uthman – 'you see them bowing and prostrating' – meaning Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with them all). So look, do not speak about them except good, lest you be blamed. This is sufficient for the intelligent person.
Source pages p. 17

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal proof establishing Ali's rank as the fourth most excellent companion after Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman. The author states the required belief: one must know that after the first three caliphs, no one is more virtuous than Ali, and he is a rightful caliph. The proof is an allegorical interpretation of a Quranic verse: Allah describes those with the Prophet; the author interprets each phrase as referring to a specific caliph: 'those with him' is Abu Bakr, 'severe against the disbelievers' is Umar, 'merciful among themselves' is Uthman, and 'you see them bowing and prostrating' is Ali. This tafsir is attributed to early scholars. The passage warns against speaking ill of them, concluding that this is sufficient for a rational person.

Key Terms
فضل
خلافة
تفسير
آية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that Ali is the most virtuous companion overall or that the Quranic verse explicitly names the caliphs.
Correction: The passage teaches that Ali is the most virtuous after the first three, and the verse is interpreted allegorically to refer to the caliphs, not explicitly.

Checkpoint
In the author's interpretation of Quran 48:29, which phrase refers to Ali ibn Abi Talib?
A) 'Those with him'
B) 'Severe against the disbelievers'
C) 'Merciful among themselves'
D) 'You see them bowing and prostrating'

Key Takeaway
Imam al‑Samarqandi affirms that Ali ibn Abi Talib is the most virtuous companion after Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman, using an allegorical interpretation of Quran 48:29.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly respect the companions of the Prophet equally, or do you find yourself favoring some over others? The passage warns against speaking ill of them – is there any ill thought or speech about the companions that you need to repent from today? What step will you take to rid your heart of that?
?
When you read the allegorical interpretation of Quran 48:29, does it increase your certainty in the wisdom of the Sunni ranking of the caliphs? If not, what doubt lingers, and how will you seek knowledge to resolve it?
?
The passage says 'This is sufficient for the intelligent person.' Are you that intelligent person? Do you accept this proof as sufficient, or do you find yourself needing more evidence? Commit to studying the lives of the first four caliphs with an open heart so that you may love them all as they deserve.
