Belief in the Reading of the Book of Deeds
Samarqandi states the obligation to believe in the reading of the book on Judgment Day.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to affirm the literal reading of the book of deeds on Judgment Day and the severe consequence of denying it.

Core Idea
The reading of the book of deeds is a literal, physical event affirmed by Quranic verses.

In this brief but powerful passage, Imam Samarqandi establishes a core belief about the Day of Judgment: that every person will be given their book of deeds and will read it. Denying this reality, after the revelation of clear Quranic verses, is an act of disbelief. The author provides direct scriptural evidence and declares the matter settled for the intelligent believer.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Let the words of Samarqandi sink in before analyzing.
Pay attention to the strong language: 'whoever denies... is a disbeliever'.
2
Identify the two components
Notice that the passage has two main parts: (1) the obligation to affirm the reading of the book, and (2) the warning that denial leads to disbelief.
3
Study the Quranic proofs
Read the two verses cited (al-Isra 13-14 and 71). Reflect on how they support Samarqandi's claim that reading the book is true.
The first verse emphasizes personal accountability: 'Read your book; sufficient against you today is your own reckoner.'
4
Reflect on the conclusion
Samarqandi ends by saying this is sufficient for the intelligent. Consider why he says that and what it means for your own faith.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما قلنا أنه ينبغي له أن يقر بقراءة الكتاب يوم القيامة ويراه حقا. ومن أنكر هذا ورد الآيات فهو كافر بالله تعالى، لأن قراءة الكتاب حق لقوله تعالى: {وكل إنسان ألزمناه طائره في عنقه ونخرج له يوم القيامة كتابا يلقاه منشورا * اقرأ كتابك كفى بنفسك اليوم عليك حسيبا} وقوله تعالى: {فأما من أوتي كتابه يمينه فأولئك يقرؤون كتابهم ولا يظلمون فتيلا}. وهذا كفاية للعاقل.
And what we said: that he ought to affirm the reading of the book on the Day of Resurrection and consider it true. And whoever denies this and rejects the verses is a disbeliever in Allah the Exalted, because the reading of the book is true by His saying the Exalted: {And every human being We have fastened his omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open. Read your book; sufficient against you today is your own reckoner.} And His saying the Exalted: {Then as for him who is given his book in his right hand, those will read their book and they will not be wronged a whit.} And this is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage defines a doctrinal obligation: to affirm the reality of the book of deeds being read on Judgment Day. The author states that one must believe in this and consider it true. He then declares that denying it, after the revelation of the verses, constitutes disbelief. The proof is from two Quranic verses. The first verse (al-Isra 13-14) states that every person will have his book presented to him and be told to read it. The second verse (al-Isra 71) says that the righteous will read their books in joy. The author concludes that this evidence is sufficient for the intelligent believer.

Key Terms
كتاب
قراءة الكتاب
يوم القيامة
حسيب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the 'book' is metaphorical.
Correction: The author insists it is a literal, physical book that will be read.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling for one who denies the reading of the book of deeds?
A) He is a sinner but still a believer.
B) He is a disbeliever (kafir).
C) He is not held accountable.
D) He is considered ignorant.

Key Takeaway
Affirming the literal reading of the book of deeds on Judgment Day is a necessary belief, and denial after clear revelation is disbelief.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to read your own book right now, before any intercession, what would be the first entry that makes you tremble? What are you doing today to change that entry before tomorrow's reading?
?
The verse says 'كفى بنفسك اليوم عليك حسيبا' – your own self is sufficient as reckoner. You cannot blame anyone else. In what area of your life are you most likely to make excuses instead of admitting what your own conscience knows? Will you sit alone tonight and write down that area honestly?
?
Some receive their book in their right hand and read it with joy (or sadness implied). Which group do you truly expect to be in, and what one concrete action can you take in the next week to increase your hope of being among those who read it on the right?

Belief in the Reckoning (Hisab) on Judgment Day
Samarqandi mandates belief in the divine reckoning on Judgment Day and provides scriptural proofs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that affirming the reckoning on Judgment Day is obligatory, and its proofs from the Quran and Sunnah.

Core Idea
The reckoning is a core belief; its denial is disbelief.

This lesson establishes the necessity of believing in the divine reckoning (hisab) on Judgment Day. Imam Samarqandi presents the obligation, warns of the consequence of denial, and provides clear scriptural evidence from the Quran and the Prophet's teachings. As you read, note how each proof reinforces the reality of this momentous event.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Let the author's argument unfold naturally.
Notice the direct style: obligation, consequence, then proofs.
2
Understand the obligation and consequence
Samarqandi states that one must affirm the reckoning and consider it true. Denial with rejection of verses is kufr. Reflect on the seriousness.
This sets the tone for the rest of the lesson.
3
Identify each scriptural proof
List the four Quranic verses and the hadith. Notice how each verse uses the word 'reckoning' or 'judgment' in a context of accountability.
The author interprets 'Day of Judgment' as 'Day of Reckoning'.
4
Reflect on the hadith about wealth
The hadith shows that even halal wealth brings reckoning, while haram brings punishment. This counters the idea that reckoning is only for disbelievers.
Consider how this affects daily life.
5
Summarise the lesson
Retell the passage in your own words: what is obligatory, what is the proof, and why it matters for a believer.
This helps internalise the material.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما ذكرنا أنه ينبغي له أن يقر بالحساب يوم القيامة ويراه حقا. ومن أنكر الحساب ورد الآيات فهو كافر بالله. والدليل على أن الحساب حق قوله تعالى: {مالك يوم الدين} يعني الحساب. وقوله تعالى: {كفى بنفسك اليوم عليك حسيبا}. وقوله تعالى: {فسوف يحاسب حسابا يسيرا}. وقوله تعالى: {ولم أدر ما حسابيه}. وقال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم حين ذكر الأموال: حلالها حساب وحرامها عذاب. وهذا كفاية للعاقل.
And what we mentioned: that he ought to affirm the reckoning on the Day of Resurrection and consider it true. And whoever denies the reckoning and rejects the verses is a disbeliever in Allah. And the proof that the reckoning is true is His saying the Exalted: {Master of the Day of Judgment} meaning the reckoning. And His saying the Exalted: {Sufficient against you today is your own reckoner.} And His saying the Exalted: {He will then be reckoned with an easy reckoning.} And His saying the Exalted: {And I did not know what my reckoning was.} And the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم said when mentioning wealth: 'Its halal is reckoning and its haram is punishment.' And this is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage establishes the necessity of believing in the reckoning (hisab) on Judgment Day. The author begins by restating the obligation: one must affirm and consider it true. Denial accompanied by rejection of Quranic verses is an act of disbelief. Four Quranic proofs are cited: (1) 'Master of the Day of Judgment' where 'judgment' is interpreted as 'reckoning'; (2) 'Sufficient against you today is your own reckoner' (al-Isra 14); (3) 'He will be reckoned with an easy reckoning' (al-Inshiqaq 8); (4) 'And I did not know what my reckoning was' (al-Haqqah 26). A hadith is also cited: The Prophet said that concerning wealth, its halal brings reckoning and its haram brings punishment. The author again concludes that this is sufficient for the intelligent believer.

Key Terms
حساب
يوم الدين
حسيب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think reckoning is only for disbelievers.
Correction: The hadith shows that even halal wealth is subject to reckoning, so it applies to all.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT a proof cited for the reality of the reckoning?
A) مالك يوم الدين
B) كفى بنفسك اليوم عليك حسيبا
C) وإن منكم إلا واردها
D) فسوف يحاسب حسابا يسيرا

Key Takeaway
Belief in the reckoning is obligatory; multiple Quranic verses and a hadith prove its reality.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that every word and action will be accounted for, what would change in your daily behavior? What specific area of your life are you avoiding accountability for?
?
The Prophet said that even halal wealth brings reckoning. How will you prepare to answer for the blessings you have been given? Will you start today by listing them and thanking Allah?

Belief in the Sirat: Obligation and Quranic Proofs
Samarqandi states obligation and gives two Quranic proofs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to affirm the reality of the Sirat bridge and the Quranic evidence for it.

Core Idea
Belief in the Sirat is obligatory, confirmed by the Quran.

Samarqandi begins this lesson by stating the obligation to affirm the Sirat. He then provides two Quranic verses as proof. This lesson will help you see how the Quran itself establishes the reality of the Sirat and why every Muslim must believe in it.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the author's direct statement of obligation.
2
Identify the obligation
Find the phrase that states it is obligatory to affirm the Sirat.
It is: 'ينبغي له أن يقر بالصراط أنه حق'
3
Examine the first Quranic proof
Look at the verse from Surah Maryam (71-72) and understand how it is used as evidence for the Sirat.
The verse speaks of 'coming to it' interpreted as crossing the bridge.
4
Examine the second Quranic proof
Consider the verse from Surah al-Fajr (14) and its interpretation regarding the bridge.
The verse 'Indeed your Lord is ever watchful' is interpreted as angels watching at the bridge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما قلنا أنه ينبغي له أن يقر بالصراط أنه حق، لقوله تعالى: {وإن منكم إلا واردها كان على ربك حتما مقضيا * ثم ننجي الذين اتقوا} الآية. وقوله تعالى: {إن ربك لبالمرصاد} يعني الملائكة يرصدون العباد على جسر جهنم. وقال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم: خلق الله تعالى على النار جسرا وهو الصراط، وجعل عليه سبع قناطر: أحدها من السيف وأظلم من الليل، كل قنطرة مسيرة ثلاثة آلاف سنة: ألف سنة صعود، وألف سنة هبوط، وألف سنة استواء. ويحبس العبد في كل قنطرة ويسأل عما أمر الله تعالى، فيسأل في القنطرة الأولى عن الإيمان، وفي الثانية عن الصلاة، وفي الثالثة عن الزكاة، وفي الرابعة عن الصوم، وفي الخامسة عن الحج، وفي السادسة عن الاغتسال من الجنابة، وفي السابعة عن حق الوالدين. وهذا كفاية للعاقل.
And what we said: that he ought to affirm the Sirat, that it is true, by His saying the Exalted: {And there is none of you but will come to it; this is upon your Lord an inevitable decree. Then We shall save those who feared Allah} the verse. And His saying the Exalted: {Indeed your Lord is ever watchful} meaning the angels watch the servants on the bridge of Hell. And the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم said: 'Allah the Exalted created a bridge over the Fire, and it is the Sirat, and He placed upon it seven arches: one of them is [like] a sword and darker than the night, each arch [spans] a distance of three thousand years: one thousand years ascent, one thousand years descent, and one thousand years level. And the servant is detained at each arch and asked about what Allah the Exalted commanded: at the first arch he is asked about faith, at the second about prayer, at the third about zakat, at the fourth about fasting, at the fifth about Hajj, at the sixth about bathing from janabah, and at the seventh about the rights of parents.' And this is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage affirms the reality of the Sirat bridge and describes its details. The author begins by stating the obligation to believe in it. Two Quranic proofs are given: first, 'And there is none of you but will come to it' (Maryam 71-72), interpreted as crossing the bridge; second, 'Indeed your Lord is ever watchful' (al-Fajr 14), interpreted as the angels watching at the bridge. Then a hadith is cited describing the Sirat as a bridge over Hell with seven arches (qanatir). The description emphasizes its difficulty: each arch is like a sword, darker than night, and of immense length. At each arch, the servant is stopped and questioned about specific religious duties: faith, prayer, zakat, fasting, Hajj, ritual purity, and rights of parents. This shows that belief in the Sirat entails belief in a real, physical bridge and a detailed questioning.

Key Terms
صراط
وارد
مرصاد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What does the author use as evidence for the obligation to believe in the Sirat?
A) Consensus of scholars
B) Two Quranic verses
C) A hadith
D) Rational proof

Key Takeaway
Belief in the Sirat is obligatory based on explicit Quranic evidence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were standing on the edge of the Sirat tonight, what deeds would you wish you had done differently? Write one concrete action you will take today to prepare for that crossing.
?
The verse says 'Then We shall save those who feared Allah.' Is your life marked by a genuine fear of Allah that drives you to obey Him? Or do you live as if the Sirat is a distant story? Be honest: what is one area where you lack that fear?
?
The angels are watching on the bridge. Does that reality change how you live this week? Commit to one specific act of obedience you will do because you know you are being watched.

5 min
The Sirat Bridge: Hadith Description and Seven Arches
Samarqandi narrates the hadith describing the Sirat and seven arches.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the detailed description of the Sirat bridge as narrated in the hadith, including its seven arches and the questioning at each.

Core Idea
The Sirat is a real bridge over Hell with seven arches of immense difficulty.

The Prophet's hadith provides a vivid description of the Sirat bridge. It is a physical bridge over Hell with seven arches, each of which is like a sword and darker than night. The servant is stopped at each arch and questioned about their religious obligations. This lesson explores that powerful narration.

Guided Path
1
Read the hadith part
Read the Arabic and translation of the hadith. Notice the details about the bridge and arches.
2
Understand the physical description
Note the description: the bridge is over the Fire, each arch is like a sword and darker than night, and the immense distances.
Each arch is 3000 years of travel.
3
List the seven arches and questions
Identify the seven questions asked at each arch: faith, prayer, zakat, fasting, Hajj, ritual purity, and rights of parents.
4
Reflect on the purpose
Consider why this detailed description is given and what it teaches about accountability.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما قلنا أنه ينبغي له أن يقر بالصراط أنه حق، لقوله تعالى: {وإن منكم إلا واردها كان على ربك حتما مقضيا * ثم ننجي الذين اتقوا} الآية. وقوله تعالى: {إن ربك لبالمرصاد} يعني الملائكة يرصدون العباد على جسر جهنم. وقال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم: خلق الله تعالى على النار جسرا وهو الصراط، وجعل عليه سبع قناطر: أحدها من السيف وأظلم من الليل، كل قنطرة مسيرة ثلاثة آلاف سنة: ألف سنة صعود، وألف سنة هبوط، وألف سنة استواء. ويحبس العبد في كل قنطرة ويسأل عما أمر الله تعالى، فيسأل في القنطرة الأولى عن الإيمان، وفي الثانية عن الصلاة، وفي الثالثة عن الزكاة، وفي الرابعة عن الصوم، وفي الخامسة عن الحج، وفي السادسة عن الاغتسال من الجنابة، وفي السابعة عن حق الوالدين. وهذا كفاية للعاقل.
And what we said: that he ought to affirm the Sirat, that it is true, by His saying the Exalted: {And there is none of you but will come to it; this is upon your Lord an inevitable decree. Then We shall save those who feared Allah} the verse. And His saying the Exalted: {Indeed your Lord is ever watchful} meaning the angels watch the servants on the bridge of Hell. And the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم said: 'Allah the Exalted created a bridge over the Fire, and it is the Sirat, and He placed upon it seven arches: one of them is [like] a sword and darker than the night, each arch [spans] a distance of three thousand years: one thousand years ascent, one thousand years descent, and one thousand years level. And the servant is detained at each arch and asked about what Allah the Exalted commanded: at the first arch he is asked about faith, at the second about prayer, at the third about zakat, at the fourth about fasting, at the fifth about Hajj, at the sixth about bathing from janabah, and at the seventh about the rights of parents.' And this is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage affirms the reality of the Sirat bridge and describes its details. The author begins by stating the obligation to believe in it. Two Quranic proofs are given: first, 'And there is none of you but will come to it' (Maryam 71-72), interpreted as crossing the bridge; second, 'Indeed your Lord is ever watchful' (al-Fajr 14), interpreted as the angels watching at the bridge. Then a hadith is cited describing the Sirat as a bridge over Hell with seven arches (qanatir). The description emphasizes its difficulty: each arch is like a sword, darker than night, and of immense length. At each arch, the servant is stopped and questioned about specific religious duties: faith, prayer, zakat, fasting, Hajj, ritual purity, and rights of parents. This shows that belief in the Sirat entails belief in a real, physical bridge and a detailed questioning.

Key Terms
صراط
قنطرة
جسر جهنم
وارد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Sirat is only a spiritual concept with no literal reality.
Correction: The hadith describes it with physical attributes: a bridge over Hell, with arches of extreme difficulty.

Checkpoint
At which arch is the servant asked about Hajj?
A) Second arch
B) Third arch
C) Fifth arch
D) Seventh arch

Key Takeaway
The Sirat is a real, physical bridge over Hell with seven arches where every servant is questioned about their obligations.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You will be stopped at each of the seven arches. Which arch do you fear the most—the one about your prayer, your zakat, or your treatment of your parents? What is one concrete change you can make today to prepare for that specific question?
?
The hadith says each arch is like a sword and darker than night. Do you truly believe you will cross that bridge? If so, are you living as if every prayer, every fast, every deed counts toward that crossing? What obligation have you been neglecting that you need to take more seriously starting now?
?
Imagine you are standing at the first arch, being asked about your iman. Can you confidently say your faith is strong enough to pass? What two things will you do this week to increase your certainty in the unseen realities that this hadith describes?

4 min
Belief that Paradise and Hell are Created and Eternal
Samarqandi affirms that Paradise and Hell are created and eternal, refuting those who deny or claim their annihilation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic doctrine that Paradise and Hell are already created and eternal, and be able to refute those who deny or claim their annihilation.

Core Idea
Paradise and Hell exist now and forever – created by Allah, never to perish.

Imam al-Samarqandi upholds a key article of Sunni belief: that Paradise and Hell are created entities that already exist and will never cease. This lesson examines the two erroneous positions he refutes – those who say Paradise and Hell will be created later (on Judgment Day) and those who say they will eventually perish – and presents the textual proofs from the Quran and Hadith that establish this doctrine. Let's read the passage and understand the arguments.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Samarqandi uses a very direct style, citing Quran and Hadith as evidence.
2
Identify the two erroneous positions
The passage categorically rejects two views: (1) that Paradise and Hell will be created later, and (2) that they are created but will perish. Find these in the text and note the labels (disbeliever, Jahmi).
The term 'Jahmi' refers to followers of Jahm ibn Safwan.
3
Examine the Quranic proof from Adam
Look at the argument from the story of Adam. Why does the existence of the tree and the command to dwell prove that Paradise existed? Consider the logic: if Paradise were not created, there would be no tree to eat from and no place to be expelled from.
The verses are: {اسكن أنت وزوجك الجنة} and {فأزلهما الشيطان عنها فأخرجهما مما كانا فيه}.
4
Note the Hadith proof from Mi'raj
The Prophet was shown Paradise, Hell, and the houris on the night of Mi'raj. This indicates their present existence at that time.
This hadith is well-known and supports the doctrine of creation.
5
Conclude with the eternity of bliss
The passage asserts that the bliss of Paradise does not perish and there is no death in it. This refutes the claim of annihilation and confirms eternal life for the inhabitants.
Many reports support this, as the author mentions.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما قلنا أنه ينبغي له أن يعلم أن الجنة والنار مخلوقتان ويراهما حقا، فمن قال أن الله تعالى يخلقهما بعد وينكر قوله تعالى فهو كافر بالله، ومن قال أنهما مخلوقتان ولكن تفنيان ويعنى ما فيهما وأهلهما فهو جهمي. واعلم أن الجنة والنار مخلوقتان لا شك فيهما، ألا ترى إلى قوله تعالى لآدم: {اسكن أنت وزوجك الجنة} أمرهما بالسكون فيها ونهاهما عن أكل الشجرة، وقوله تعالى: {ولا تقربا هذه الشجرة} فلما لم تكن الجنة مخلوقة بعد فأين كانت هذه الشجرة حتى أكلاها؟ وإن كانت الجنة لم تخلق كان أمر الله تعالى إياهما بالسكون فيها والنهي عن أكل الشجرة محالا. وقوله تعالى: {فأزلهما الشيطان عنها فأخرجهما مما كانا فيه} فلما لم تكن الجنة مخلوقة بعد فمن أين أخرجهما؟ وقال عليه الصلاة والسلام: عرض علي في ليلة المعراج النار والجنة والحور العين. واعلم أن نعيم الجنة لا يفنى ولا موت فيها، وفي هذا أخبار كثيرة. وهذا كفاية للعاقل فإنهم ترشد.
And what we said: that he ought to know that Paradise and the Fire are created and consider them true. So whoever says that Allah the Exalted will create them later and denies His saying the Exalted is a disbeliever in Allah. And whoever says that they are created but they will perish and it means what is in them and their inhabitants, he is a Jahmi. And know that Paradise and the Fire are created, no doubt about them. Do you not see His saying the Exalted to Adam: {Dwell, you and your wife, in Paradise} He commanded them to dwell in it and forbade them from the tree, and His saying the Exalted: {And do not approach this tree.} So when Paradise was not yet created, where was this tree so that they could eat from it? And if Paradise were not created, the command of Allah the Exalted to them to dwell in it and the prohibition from eating the tree would be impossible. And His saying the Exalted: {Then Satan caused them to slip from it and took them out of what they were in.} So when Paradise was not yet created, from where did he take them out? And he عليه الصلاة والسلام said: 'There were shown to me on the night of the Mi'raj the Fire, Paradise, and the houris.' And know that the bliss of Paradise does not perish and there is no death in it. And there are many reports about this. And this is sufficient for the intelligent, for they are guided.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage defends the doctrine that Paradise and Hell are already created and eternal. The author begins by stating the obligation to know this. Then he identifies two opposing errors: (1) those who say Allah will create them later (i.e., only on Judgment Day) are disbelievers; (2) those who say they are created but will eventually perish (fana') are Jahmis (followers of Jahm ibn Safwan). The proof is from the Quranic story of Adam: Allah commanded Adam to dwell in Paradise and forbade him from the tree. If Paradise were not yet created, there would be no tree to forbid. Also, when Adam and Eve were expelled, they were taken out of something; that something must have existed. A hadith from the Mi'raj is cited: the Prophet was shown Paradise, Hell, and the houris, indicating their present existence. Finally, the author asserts that the bliss of Paradise is eternal and there is no death there, supported by many reports. This evidence is sufficient for the intelligent.

Key Terms
جنة
نار
مخلوقتان
جهمي
فناء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Paradise and Hell are not yet created; they will be brought into existence on Judgment Day.
Correction: The Quran and Hadith show that Paradise and Hell exist now – Adam dwelt in Paradise, and the Prophet saw them on the Mi'raj.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling on one who says Paradise and Hell will be created later (i.e., not yet created)?
A) He is a Jahmi.
B) He is a disbeliever (kafir).
C) He is a sinner but still a believer.
D) He is simply mistaken.

Key Takeaway
Paradise and Hell are created now, eternal, and their inhabitants will never die, as proven by the Quran and Hadith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly act as if Paradise and Hell are real and present right now, or is that reality distant in your daily choices? What would change if you truly believed they exist at this moment?
?
If someone argued that Paradise and Hell will eventually perish, how would your hope in Allah's justice be affected? What from this lesson would you hold onto?
?
The Prophet was shown Paradise and Hell on the night of Mi'raj. If you were to see them now, how would your priorities shift? What is holding you back from living with that urgency today?

Belief in Allah’s Direct Reckoning on Judgment Day
Samarqandi affirms direct reckoning with four Quranic proofs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that on Judgment Day, Allah will directly reckon with every servant without any intermediary, supported by multiple Quranic verses.

Core Idea
Allah questions each servant directly—no angels or prophets as intermediaries.

In this passage, Imam Samarqandi teaches a fundamental belief concerning the Day of Judgment: Allah Himself will directly call each servant to account. There will be no intermediaries—no angels or prophets—between Allah and the individual. He supports this with four Quranic verses that emphasize the thoroughness of the reckoning and the testimony of the body parts. This lesson unpacks that passage.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Carefully read the Arabic passage once, then its literal translation. Let the author's own words establish the teaching before you analyze.
Notice the emphatic tone: 'ما بينه وبين عباده بغير واسطة'.
2
Identify the core claim
The passage states that Allah will reckon directly without any intermediary. This is the single doctrine to be known.
The phrase 'ما بينه وبين عباده' stresses exclusivity.
3
Examine the four Quranic proofs
Samarqandi cites four verses: (1) surah al-Hijr 92-93, (2) al-Kahf 49, (3) an-Nur 24, (4) Fussilat 20. Each reinforces the direct nature of the reckoning and the testimony of limbs.
Verse 4 explicitly mentions body parts testifying.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
The author ends with 'وهذا كفاية للعاقل' — this is sufficient for the intelligent. Reflect on why direct reckoning is a necessary belief.
It underscores divine justice and individual accountability.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما قلنا أنه ينبغي له أن يعلم أن الله تعالى يحاسب عبيده يوم القيامة ما بينه وبين عباده بغير واسطة. فالله تعالى يسأل العبد والعبد يجيب عما يسأل. قال الله تعالى: {فوربك لنسألنهم أجمعين عما كانوا يعملون}. وقوله تعالى: {لا يغادر صغيرة ولا كبيرة إلا أحصاها}. وقوله تعالى: {يوم تشهد عليهم ألسنتهم} الآية. وقوله تعالى: {شهد عليهم سمعهم وأبصارهم وجلودهم} الآية. وهذا كفاية للعاقل.
And what we said: that he ought to know that Allah the Exalted will reckon with His servants on the Day of Resurrection directly between Him and His servants without any intermediary. So Allah the Exalted asks the servant and the servant answers what he is asked. Allah the Exalted said: {So by your Lord, We will surely question them all about what they used to do.} And His saying the Exalted: {It leaves out nothing small or great but has enumerated it.} And His saying the Exalted: {On the Day when their tongues will testify against them} the verse. And His saying the Exalted: {Their hearing, their sight, and their skins will testify against them} the verse. And this is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 16

Commentary
This passage teaches that Allah will directly reckon with His servants on Judgment Day, with no intermediary. The author states this as an obligation to know. He then cites four Quranic proofs: (1) 'So by your Lord, We will surely question them all' (al-Hijr 92-93); (2) 'It leaves out nothing small or great but has enumerated it' (al-Kahf 49); (3) 'On the Day when their tongues will testify against them' (an-Nur 24); (4) 'Their hearing, their sight, and their skins will testify against them' (Fussilat 20). These verses emphasize that Allah Himself will question every soul, and even the limbs will bear witness. The author concludes that this is sufficient for the intelligent.

Key Terms
حساب
واسطة
سؤال
شهادة الجوارح

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that prophets or angels intercede in the reckoning, acting as intermediaries.
Correction: The passage explicitly states 'without any intermediary' (بغير واسطة). Allah directly questions each servant, and even the limbs testify.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, how will Allah reckon with His servants on Judgment Day?
A) Through the angels
B) Through the prophets
C) Directly without any intermediary
D) Through the book of deeds only

Key Takeaway
Allah will directly reckon with each servant without any intermediary, as affirmed by scripture.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you knew that on Judgment Day you will stand alone before Allah with no one to speak for you, what habit or sin are you hiding behind the excuse that 'it's small'? Will you not change it now before it is read back to you from the book that leaves nothing out?
?
On that Day, your own tongue will testify against you. Is there a word you have spoken in private that you would be ashamed to hear announced? What will you do today to ensure your tongue bears witness for you, not against you?
?
Your hearing, sight, and skin will be called as witnesses. What have you allowed your eyes and ears to indulge in that you would not want them to reveal? Are you ready to face that testimony without an intermediary?

Seeing Allah in Paradise: A Literal Vision
Samarqandi affirms literal vision of Allah in Paradise.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni doctrine that believers will see Allah in Paradise with their physical eyes, without resemblance or modality, and the scriptural proofs for this belief.

Core Idea
Believers will see Allah as clearly as the full moon with certainty.

Imam Samarqandi now addresses a fundamental belief of Ahl al-Sunna: the vision of Allah in the Hereafter. This is not a metaphorical or spiritual seeing but a real, literal vision—yet without any likeness or 'how'. The author presents the standard Sunni position, supported by Quranic verses and Prophetic hadith, and explicitly rejects those who limit it to the heart. As you read, notice how he uses the moon analogy to establish certainty and how he cites the Companions' interpretation of 'more' in the Quran.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the source_arabic once, then the translation. Pay attention to the key terms: رؤية, بلا مثال, بلا كيف.
The author uses a very direct style—'اعلم' (know) indicates a matter of certainty.
2
Identify the main claim
What exactly is the belief Samarqandi is defending? He says believers see Allah 'without likeness and without modality'.
This refutes both anthropomorphism and pure metaphor.
3
Find the three proofs
The author gives three scriptural proofs: two Quranic verses and one hadith. List them in order.
The hadith about seeing the moon is repeated twice.
4
Understand the refutation
Who does Samarqandi call misguided? What is their error? He rejects the idea of seeing 'with the eye of the heart' only.
This is a direct refutation of those who spiritualize the vision.
5
Reflect on the conclusion
The author ends by saying 'This is sufficient for the intelligent person.' Why? Because the proofs are clear and definitive.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما ذكرنا انه ينبغي له ان يعلم ان اهل الجنة يرون الله تعالى بلا مثال ولا كيف اعلم ان المؤمنين برون ربهم في الجنة بلا شبهة ولاشك كما يرى الرجل القمر ليلة البدر فهل يشك احد فى النظر إلى البدرانه ليس عمر وكذلك المؤمنون يرون الله تعالى رؤية حقا ولا يشكون انه ربهم بلامثال ولا كيف فمن انكر رؤية الله تعالى وقال لا يرونه بعين الرأس ولكن يرونه بعين القلب فه وضال مبتدع لان الله تعالى قال الذين احسنوا الحسنى وزيادة وقد فسر اصحاب رسول الله صلى الله تعالى عليه وسلم الزيادة برؤية الله تعالى وقال الله تعالى وجوه يومئذ ناضرة الى ربها ناظرة وقال رسول الله تعالى عليه وسلم انكم سترون ربكم كما ترون القمر ليلة البدر ولا تضامون اى لا تشكون في رؤية الله تعالى ( اخبرنا ( الثقات بإسنادهم عن ابن عمر عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم انه قال اكرمكم على الله في الجنة من نظر وجهه غدوة وعشياتم تلا قوله تعالى وجوة يومئذ ناضرة الى ربها ناظرة وهذا كفاية للعاقل
We mentioned that it is necessary for him to know that the people of Paradise see Allah Most High without likeness and without modality. Know that the believers see their Lord in Paradise without doubt and without uncertainty, just as a man sees the moon on the night of the full moon. Does anyone who looks at the moon doubt that it is the moon? Likewise, the believers see Allah Most High a real seeing, and they do not doubt that He is their Lord, without likeness and without modality. Whoever denies seeing Allah Most High and says, 'They do not see Him with the eye of the head, but they see Him with the eye of the heart,' he is misguided and an innovator, because Allah Most High said: 'For those who do good is the best (reward) and more.' The companions of the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and grant him peace) interpreted 'more' as seeing Allah Most High. And Allah Most High said: 'Faces on that day are radiant, looking at their Lord.' And the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and grant him peace) said: 'You will see your Lord just as you see the moon on the night of the full moon, and you will not be harmed' – i.e., you will not doubt in seeing Allah Most High. Trustworthy narrators informed us with their chain from Ibn Umar from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) that he said: 'The most honored of you with Allah in Paradise is the one who looks at His face morning and evening.' Then he recited His saying: 'Faces on that day are radiant, looking at their Lord.' This is sufficient for the intelligent person.
Source pages p. 18

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal defense of the literal vision of Allah (ru'ya) by the believers in Paradise. The author states the required belief: the people of Paradise will see Allah without resemblance (tashbih) or modality (takyeef). He affirms that believers see their Lord with certainty, using the analogy of seeing the moon: just as one has no doubt when seeing the moon, believers will see Allah and know He is their Lord. He then refutes those who claim the vision is only with the heart, labeling them misguided innovators. The proofs are three: (1) Quranic: 'For those who do good is the best and more' – the 'more' (ziyada) is interpreted by the Companions as seeing Allah; (2) Quranic: 'Faces on that day are radiant, looking at their Lord' – directly asserting vision; (3) Prophetic hadith: 'You will see your Lord as you see the moon,' and another hadith stating that the most honored will see Allah's face morning and evening. The passage concludes that this is sufficient for the rational person.

Key Terms
رؤية
الجنة
زيادة
تشبيه
تكييف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that seeing Allah in Paradise is only a spiritual or metaphorical vision with the heart, not a literal physical sight.
Correction: Samarqandi explicitly states that it is a real seeing with the eye of the head (عين الرأس), without resemblance or modality, and rejects the heart-only view as innovation.

Checkpoint
What do the Companions interpret 'more' (زيادة) in Quran 10:26 to mean?
A) More reward in Paradise
B) Seeing Allah
C) Multiplication of good deeds
D) Eternal life

Key Takeaway
The Sunni belief is that believers will see Allah in Paradise with their physical eyes, clearly and without doubt, but without any likeness or modality, as proven by the Quran and hadith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The Prophet said the most honored in Paradise will see Allah's face morning and evening. What is your real desire: to see Allah, or to enjoy Paradise without Him? Are you living today in a way that reflects that desire?
?
If you truly believed you will see Allah with certainty like seeing the moon, would you fear death as you do? What holds you back from longing for that moment?
?
The author calls those who deny literal vision 'misguided innovators.' Yet many Muslims today interpret it metaphorically. Are you willing to stand firm on this belief even if it seems strange to others? Where does your allegiance lie—with the Salaf or with rationalism?

4 min
The Beatific Vision: Seeing Allah in Paradise
Samarqandi affirms the beatific vision with Quran and hadith, refuting deniers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni doctrine of the beatific vision (رؤية الله) in Paradise, the scriptural proofs for it, and the condemnation of those who deny it.

Core Idea
Believers will see Allah with their eyes in Paradise—no shadow of doubt, no likeness.

In this passage, Imam al-Samarqandi tackles one of the most exhilarating yet fiercely debated topics in Islamic theology: the vision of Allah by the believers in Paradise. He leaves no room for ambiguity, affirming that it is a real, physical sight, and he brands any denial as innovation. Let us examine his scriptural proofs and his uncompromising stance.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the confident tone and the clear structure.
Samarqandi uses a very direct style.
2
Identify the key scriptural proofs
List the three scriptural proofs: two Quranic verses and one hadith. See how each supports the doctrine.
The hadith about seeing the moon is particularly vivid.
3
Understand the refutation
Samarqandi explicitly rejects the metaphorical interpretation. Note his harsh language ('ضال مبتدع') and why.
This shows the seriousness of the issue.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثلاثون
وما ذكرنا انه ينبغي له ان يعلم ان اهل الجنة يرون الله تعالى بلا مثال ولا كيف اعلم ان المؤمنين يرون ربهم في الجنة بلا شبهة ولا شك كما يرى الرجل القمر ليلة البدر فهل يشك احد فى النظر إلى البدر انه ليس القمر؟ وكذلك المؤمنون يرون الله تعالى رؤية حقا ولا يشكون انه ربهم بلا مثال ولا كيف فمن انكر رؤية الله تعالى وقال لا يرونه بعين الرأس ولكن يرونه بعين القلب فهو ضال مبتدع لان الله تعالى قال الذين احسنوا الحسنى وزيادة وقد فسر اصحاب رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم الزيادة برؤية الله تعالى وقال الله تعالى وجوه يومئذ ناضرة الى ربها ناظرة وقال رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم انكم سترون ربكم كما ترون القمر ليلة البدر ولا تضامون اى لا تشكون في رؤية الله تعالى اخبرنا الثقات بإسنادهم عن ابن عمر عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم انه قال اكرمكم على الله في الجنة من نظر وجهه غدوة وعشيا ثم تلا قوله تعالى وجوه يومئذ ناضرة الى ربها ناظرة وهذا كفاية للعاقل
Question thirty: What we mentioned that he should know that the people of Paradise see God Almighty without likeness or modality. Know that the believers see their Lord in Paradise without doubt or uncertainty, just as a man sees the moon on the night of the full moon. So does anyone doubt in looking at the moon that it is not the moon? Likewise the believers see God Almighty a true vision and do not doubt that He is their Lord, without likeness or modality. Whoever denies seeing God Almighty and says they do not see Him with the eye of the head but see Him with the eye of the heart, he is an astray innovator. Because God Almighty said: {Those who do good shall have the best reward and more} (Quran 10:26), and the Companions of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him) interpreted 'more' as seeing God Almighty. And God Almighty said: {Faces on that day are radiant, looking at their Lord} (Quran 75:22-23). And the Messenger of God (peace be upon him) said: 'You will see your Lord as you see the moon on the night of the full moon, and you will not be harmed (i.e., you will not doubt in seeing God Almighty).' The trustworthy informed us with their chains from Ibn 'Umar from the Messenger of God (peace be upon him) that he said: 'The most honored of you to God in Paradise is the one who looks at His face morning and evening.' Then he recited the verse: {Faces on that day are radiant, looking at their Lord}. This is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 18

Commentary
This passage is a proof and refutation. Samarqandi begins by stating the belief that believers will see God in Paradise without resemblance or modality. He compares it to seeing the moon clearly, implying no doubt. He then condemns those who deny the beatific vision or claim it is only mental, labeling them astray innovators. He provides textual evidence: the verse about 'more (زيادة)' from Surah Yunus, which the Companions interpreted as the vision of God; the verse from Surah al-Qiyama where faces look at their Lord; and a hadith comparing the vision to seeing the full moon, with the phrase 'لا تضامون' explained as 'no doubt'. He also cites a hadith about the most honored person looking at God's face morning and evening. The passage concludes that this is sufficient for the intelligent.

Key Terms
رؤية الله
بلا مثال ولا كيف
ضال مبتدع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Seeing God is only with the heart, not with the physical eyes.
Correction: Samarqandi condemns this as innovation (ضال مبتدع) and affirms literal vision with the eyes, supported by Quran and hadith.

Checkpoint
What evidence does Samarqandi give for the vision of God in Paradise?
A) The verse 'وجوه يومئذ ناضرة إلى ربها ناظرة' and the hadith about seeing the moon.
B) Only the hadith about the moon.
C) The verse 'الذين أحسنوا الحسنى وزيادة' without interpretation.
D) He says the Companions agreed without textual proof.

Key Takeaway
The vision of Allah in Paradise is a real, physical sight, affirmed by Quran and Sunnah, and denial is innovation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly long to see Allah, or are you satisfied with the fleeting pleasures of this world? What would change in your daily worship if the desire to see His face was the driving force?
?
If you knew that the most honored among you in Paradise will look at Allah's face morning and evening, what actions are you performing today to be among them?
?
The source says this evidence is 'sufficient for the intelligent'. Are you among the intelligent who accepts this truth, or do you allow doubts from worldly philosophies to cloud your certainty? Will you let this reality shape how you live right now?

Reconciliation with Opponents in the Afterlife
Imam al-Samarqandi explains the divine resolution of interpersonal disputes in the afterlife.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Allah resolves interpersonal injustices in the afterlife by transferring good deeds, and why this is an act of perfect justice.

Core Idea
In the afterlife, Allah settles all accounts—even by transferring good deeds.

This passage from Imam al-Samarqandi addresses a practical concern: what happens to unresolved grievances between people when they die? The author presents a clear doctrine that Allah will compensate the wronged party from the wrongdoer's good deeds. This is not injustice but perfect justice. The passage also warns against those who deny this transfer and refutes a false analogy.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the strong language: 'he is an innovator.'
2
Identify the core claim
The passage states that if someone dies with unresolved grievances, Allah will give the opponent from the wrongdoer's good deeds. Find this statement and note the condition: 'without having satisfied him and without repenting.'
3
Understand the justice rationale
The author immediately clarifies that this is not injustice (jور) but justice (عدل). Ask yourself: why would someone think it's unjust? How does the author refute that?
The transfer of good deeds is an act of divine justice, not arbitrariness.
4
Examine the refutation
The passage counters a false analogy from Adam's inheritance. Note how the author compares the denial to the doctrine of the Magians. Think about why this analogy is weak according to the author.
The author dismisses the argument as resembling a deviant practice.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الحادية والاربعون وما قلنا أنه ينبغي له أن يعلم أن من كان له خصم وخرج من الدنيا ولم يرضه ولم يتب فانه يعطى الله تعالى من حسناته خصمه في الآخرة بقدر خصومته. وهذا لا يكون من الله جورا بل يكون عدلا. ومن رأى أخذ مال المسلم أو غيره ولم يرضه في الدنيا ويقول لا يعطى الله تعالى من حسناتي إلى خصمائي في الآخرة فهو مبتدع. وربما يدعي ويقول إن آدم عليه السلام مات ولا ينقسم ماله بين أولاده، فمن أخذ شيئا فهو له. وهذا مذهب يشبه مذهب المجوس بقربانهم أمهاتهم وبناتهم وأخواتهم. والذي شرحناه في هذا الباب كفاية للمعاقل.
The forty-first question: And what we said: That he should know that whoever has an opponent and leaves this world without having satisfied him and without repenting, then Allah Most High will give his opponent from his good deeds in the afterlife to the extent of the dispute. And this is not from Allah injustice but is justice. And whoever sees the taking of a Muslim's property or otherwise and does not satisfy him in this world, and says: 'Allah Most High will not give from my good deeds to my opponents in the afterlife,' he is an innovator. And perhaps he claims and says: 'Adam, peace be upon him, died and his property was not divided among his children, so whoever takes something, it is his.' And this doctrine resembles the doctrine of the Magians (Zoroastrians) in their permitting their mothers, daughters, and sisters. And what we have explained in this chapter is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 25

Commentary
This passage is a theological ruling on the resolution of interpersonal injustices in the afterlife. It asserts as a matter of belief that if a person dies with an unresolved grievance against someone (i.e., he wronged another and did not seek forgiveness or make amends), Allah will compensate the wronged party by transferring some of the wrongdoer's good deeds to him in the Hereafter. This is not injustice on Allah's part but is an act of justice. The author condemns as an innovator anyone who denies this divine compensation, particularly those who say that Allah will not give their good deeds to their opponents. The passage then addresses a false argument: some claim that just as Adam's property was not divided among his children after his death, so too a person's good deeds cannot be transferred. The author dismisses this analogy as resembling the permissive practices of the Magians (Zoroastrians) who allowed incestuous marriages. The point is to emphasize that God's justice is perfect and He will settle all accounts. The chapter's explanation is deemed sufficient for the intelligent.

Key Terms
خصم
حسنات
عدل
جور
مجوس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Unresolved grievances in this world cannot be compensated after death; Allah ignores them.
Correction: Allah will transfer good deeds from the wrongdoer to the wronged party in the afterlife, as an act of perfect justice.

Checkpoint
How does Allah compensate for unresolved grievances in the afterlife according to this passage?
A) He punishes the wrongdoer directly
B) He transfers good deeds from the wrongdoer to the wronged
C) He ignores the grievance if both are believers
D) He creates new good deeds for the wronged

Key Takeaway
Allah's justice ensures that every unresolved grievance in this life is settled in the afterlife, even if it requires transferring good deeds.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is there someone you have wronged and never sought forgiveness from? What concrete step will you take today to make amends, instead of merely hoping Allah will compensate them with your good deeds?
?
Do you ever tell yourself, 'I don't need to apologize—let Allah handle it in the afterlife'? How does this passage challenge that thought, and what would true repentance require of you right now?
?
If you knew that every unresolved argument with a family member, friend, or colleague would cost you your good deeds in the Hereafter, what would you do differently starting tomorrow?

Settling Disputes in the Afterlife
Samarqandi explains the transfer of good deeds to settle claims in the Hereafter.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic doctrine that Allah compensates wronged parties by transferring good deeds from the wrongdoer in the Hereafter, and why denying this is considered innovation.

Core Idea
Allah's justice ensures no wrong goes uncompensated, even beyond this life.

This lesson addresses a key aspect of Islamic eschatology: how interpersonal wrongs are settled when a person dies without making amends. Imam al-Samarqandi explains that Allah in His justice will compensate the wronged party from the wrongdoer's good deeds. We will read the original text, explore the reasoning behind this doctrine, and understand why denying it leads to innovation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
First, read the Arabic passage slowly. Then read the literal translation. Notice the core claim: Allah gives from the wrongdoer's good deeds to his opponent.
The Arabic has some uncertain words (جو رابل) but the meaning is clear from context.
2
Identify the doctrine and its justification
The author states that this transfer is justice (عدل), not injustice. He warns that whoever denies it is an innovator (مبتدع). Reflect on why this is a matter of 'aqidah.
3
Consider the refutation of the opposing argument
The author refutes a possible objection by comparing it to the practice of the Magians, which shows that the denial is based on a false analogy. Understand the strength of the refutation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الحادية والاربعون
وما قلنا انه ينبغي له ان يعلم ان من كان له خصم وخرج من الدنيا ولم يرضه ولم يتب فانه يعطى الله تعالى من حسناته خصمه فى الاخرة بقدر خصومته وهذا لا يكون من الله جو رابل يكون عدلا ومن رأى اخذ مال المسام اوغيره ولم يرضه فى الدنيا ويقول لا يعطى الله تعالى من حسناتى الى خصمائى فى الاخرة فهو مبتدع وربما يدعى ويقول ان آدم عليه السلام مات ولا ينقسم ماله بين اولاده فمن اخذ شيأ فهوله وهذا مذهب يشبه مذهب المجوس بقربانهم امهاتهم وبناتهم واخواتهم والذى شرحناه في هذا الباب كفاية للمعاقل
Issue forty-one: And we said that it is befitting for him to know that whoever has an opponent and leaves the world without satisfying him and without repenting, then Allah Almighty will give from his good deeds to his opponent in the Hereafter to the extent of his claim. And this is not from Allah injustice [?] but is justice. And whoever sees that one has taken property of a Muslim [?] or other and did not satisfy him in the world and says, 'Allah Almighty will not give from my good deeds to my opponents in the Hereafter,' he is an innovator. And perhaps he claims and says that Adam, peace be upon him, died and his property does not divide among his children, so whoever takes something, it is his [?]. And this school resembles the school of the Magians in their offering of their mothers, daughters, and sisters. And what we have explained in this chapter is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 25

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal ruling about divine justice in the afterlife concerning interpersonal wrongs. The author states that it is necessary for a believer to know that if a person had an opponent (خصم) whom he wronged and then died without satisfying him or repenting, Allah will transfer some of the wrongdoer's good deeds to the opponent in the Hereafter to compensate for the injustice. The author emphasizes that this is not injustice (جور) from Allah but is perfect justice (عدل). Then he warns against the innovated view of someone who, having taken someone else's property or committed a wrong without seeking forgiveness, claims that Allah will not give any of his good deeds to his opponents. Such a person is an innovator (مبتدع). The author compares this deviant reasoning to the practice of the Magians (المجوس) who consider it lawful to marry their mothers, daughters, and sisters—that is, it is a baseless analogy. The passage concludes that the explanation provided is sufficient for the intelligent person.

Key Terms
خصم
حسنات
عدل
مبتدع
مجوس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people think that if they die without settling a dispute, Allah will not compensate the wronged party, or that it is unjust for Allah to transfer good deeds.
Correction: The passage teaches that Allah's giving of good deeds to opponents is an act of perfect justice, not injustice. Denying this makes one an innovator.

Checkpoint
What happens to a wrongdoer who dies without satisfying his opponent or repenting?
A) He is forgiven without any compensation.
B) His punishment is doubled.
C) Allah gives from the wrongdoer's good deeds to the opponent in the Hereafter.
D) The opponent receives nothing.

Key Takeaway
Allah's justice ensures that no wrong goes without recompense, even by transferring good deeds in the afterlife.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is there someone you have wronged and not yet made amends with? What would it mean for your good deeds to be transferred because you avoided repentance? Will you take a step today to seek their forgiveness?
?
Do you ever comfort yourself with the thought that your good deeds are your own and cannot be taken away? How does this passage challenge that assumption, and what changes in your attitude toward others?
?
Reflect on a time you dismissed the need to seek forgiveness from someone you harmed. How does the certainty of divine justice in the Hereafter change your urgency to reconcile now, before it is too late?

Affirmation of Resurrection and the Ruling on Its Denier
Samarqandi affirms resurrection and declares its denier a disbeliever, citing Qur'an 20:55.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that affirming resurrection after death is an obligation of faith and that denying it constitutes disbelief, based on the Qur'anic proof from Surah Ta-Ha.

Core Idea
Denying resurrection is kufr; affirming it is essential faith.

In this passage, Imam al-Samarqandi lays down a foundational creedal point: resurrection after death is a reality affirmed by the Qur'an. He declares that anyone who denies it is not merely mistaken but a disbeliever, specifically called a dahri (materialist). Let's examine his argument and the proof he offers.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage once
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to get the overall flow. Notice the strong language: 'whoever denies is a disbeliever.'
2
Identify the key terms
Note the terms: البعث (resurrection), القيامة (the Rising), الساعة (the Hour), and دهري (materialist). Understand their significance.
3
Reflect on the proof
The author cites verse 20:55 from Surah Ta-Ha. Consider how this verse proves bodily resurrection from the earth.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الخمسون وما قلنا انه ينبني له ان يقر بالبعث بعد الموت وان من انكر البعث فهو كافر يسمى دهريا وان البعث حق لقوله تعالى منها خلقناكم وفيها نعيدكم ومنها نخرجكم تارة اخرى ومن انكر هذا فهو كافر فينبغي للمؤمن ان يقر بالقيامة والساعة لان من انكر القيامة فقد كفر بالله واعلم ان القيامة حق والاستعداد [؟] لها واجب لقوله تعالى ونفخ في الصور فصعق من في السموات ومن في الارض وقوله تعالى وتزودوا فإن خير الزاد التقوى واتقون وقوله تعالى يوم وقوله يوم ينفخ في الصور وقوله يوم يفر المرء من اخيه وقوله يوم يقوم الناس لرب العالمين وقوله في يوم كان مقداره خمسين الف سنة فمن انكر هذا فهو كافر بالله وهذا كفاية للعاقل والله اعلم
The fiftieth issue: And we said that he should affirm resurrection (al-ba`th) after death, and that whoever denies resurrection is a disbeliever called a materialist (dahriyy). And that resurrection is true, due to His saying the Exalted: 'From it We created you, and into it We shall return you, and from it We shall bring you out another time.' And whoever denies this is a disbeliever. So the believer should affirm the Resurrection (al-qiyamah) and the Hour because whoever denies the Resurrection has disbelieved in Allah. And know that the Resurrection is true, and preparation [?] for it is obligatory, due to His saying the Exalted: 'And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and on earth will be stunned except...' and His saying: 'And take provision, for the best provision is piety and fear Me.' and His saying: 'on a Day' and His saying: 'On the Day when the Trumpet is blown' and His saying: 'On the Day when a man flees from his brother' and His saying: 'On the Day when mankind will stand before the Lord of the worlds' and His saying: 'in a Day whose measure is fifty thousand years.' So whoever denies this is a disbeliever in Allah. This is sufficient for the intelligent. And Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 29

Commentary
This passage is a declaration regarding the reality of resurrection after death. The author states that the believer must affirm resurrection (al-ba`th) after death. He then declares that whoever denies resurrection is a disbeliever and is called a dahri (materialist/atheist). He provides a Qur'anic proof from Surah Ta-Ha (20:55): 'From it We created you, and into it We shall return you, and from it We shall bring you out another time.' Affirming resurrection is obligatory for believers. He then lists several other Qur'anic verses that speak of the events of the Day of Judgment: the blowing of the Trumpet, the flight of kin, standing before the Lord, and the length of the Day (50,000 years). Denial of any of these constitutes disbelief in Allah. Preparation for this day is obligatory. The passage serves as a basic creedal affirmation of bodily resurrection, a cornerstone of Sunni eschatology (samiyyat).

Key Terms
البعث
القيامة
الساعة
دهري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That resurrection is only spiritual or metaphorical.
Correction: The passage asserts bodily resurrection from the earth, as indicated by the verse 'from it We created you... and from it We shall bring you out another time.'

Checkpoint
What term does Samarqandi use for a person who denies resurrection?
A) Kafir
B) Dahri
C) Mubtadi'
D) Munafiq

Key Takeaway
Affirming resurrection is an essential part of faith; denial makes one a disbeliever called a dahri.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live as if the Resurrection is certain, or do your daily choices reflect doubt? If you deny it even in your actions, what does that say about your faith? Will you today list three ways you can concretely prepare for that Day?
?
When you hear the verse 'From it We created you, and into it We shall return you,' does it stir in you the reality that your life is temporary and you will be held accountable? How can you make that awareness transform how you use your time today?
?
The author says the denier of resurrection is 'dahri'—a materialist. Do you ever find yourself acting as if this world is all there is, focusing only on material gains? What step will you take today to redirect your heart to the Hereafter?

The Necessity of Preparation for the Day of Judgment
Samarqandi asserts the truth of the Resurrection and the obligation of preparation, citing multiple verses about the Day.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that preparation for the Day of Judgment is obligatory, based on multiple Qur'anic descriptions of that Day.

Core Idea
Preparation for the Day of Judgment is wajib; denial of its events is kufr.

Imam al-Samarqandi continues by emphasizing that the Resurrection is true and that preparing for it is obligatory. He then lists several Qur'anic verses describing the dramatic events of the Day, from the blowing of the Trumpet to the flight of relatives and the long duration. Denial of any of these is disbelief.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the phrase 'الاستعداد لها واجب' (preparation for it is obligatory).
2
List the verses cited
The author cites: the Trumpet blast (stunning), provision of piety, the day of the Trumpet, fleeing from brother, standing before Lord, and a day of 50,000 years. Identify each.
3
Understand the warning
The passage concludes that whoever denies any of this is a disbeliever. This shows the gravity of acknowledging the eschatological events.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

The fiftieth issue: And we said that he should affirm resurrection (al-ba`th) after death, and that whoever denies resurrection is a disbeliever called a materialist (dahriyy). And that resurrection is true, due to His saying the Exalted: 'From it We created you, and into it We shall return you, and from it We shall bring you out another time.' And whoever denies this is a disbeliever. So the believer should affirm the Resurrection (al-qiyamah) and the Hour because whoever denies the Resurrection has disbelieved in Allah. And know that the Resurrection is true, and preparation [?] for it is obligatory, due to His saying the Exalted: 'And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and on earth will be stunned except...' and His saying: 'And take provision, for the best provision is piety and fear Me.' and His saying: 'on a Day' and His saying: 'On the Day when the Trumpet is blown' and His saying: 'On the Day when a man flees from his brother' and His saying: 'On the Day when mankind will stand before the Lord of the worlds' and His saying: 'in a Day whose measure is fifty thousand years.' So whoever denies this is a disbeliever in Allah. This is sufficient for the intelligent. And Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 29

Commentary
This passage is a declaration regarding the reality of resurrection after death. The author states that the believer must affirm resurrection (al-ba`th) after death. He then declares that whoever denies resurrection is a disbeliever and is called a dahri (materialist/atheist). He provides a Qur'anic proof from Surah Ta-Ha (20:55): 'From it We created you, and into it We shall return you, and from it We shall bring you out another time.' Affirming resurrection is obligatory for believers. He then lists several other Qur'anic verses that speak of the events of the Day of Judgment: the blowing of the Trumpet, the flight of kin, standing before the Lord, and the length of the Day (50,000 years). Denial of any of these constitutes disbelief in Allah. Preparation for this day is obligatory. The passage serves as a basic creedal affirmation of bodily resurrection, a cornerstone of Sunni eschatology (samiyyat).

الصور
التقوى
الاستعداد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That preparation for the Day is optional or only for scholars.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that preparation is obligatory (واجب) for believers.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the duration of the Day mentioned?
A) 1,000 years
B) 50,000 years
C) 100 years
D) 500 years

Key Takeaway
The Resurrection is true and preparation for it is obligatory; denial of these Qur'anic descriptions is disbelief.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you truly prepared for the Day of Judgment, or are you living as if it is far away? What specific actions will you take today to turn your preparation into a reality?
?
When you read about the events of that Day—the Trumpet, the flight from relatives, the standing before your Lord—does it stir fear and urgency in your heart, or have you become desensitized? How can you cultivate a living awareness of this reality?
?
You know that denial of any aspect of the Resurrection is disbelief. But have you examined your own heart for subtle forms of denial—like neglecting to prepare, or assuming you have plenty of time? What is one thing you must change right now to align your life with the truth of this lesson?

Ruling on Wiping over Khuffs
Samarqandi states the ruling on wiping over khuffs and warns against rejecting it.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the duration for wiping over khuffs for resident and traveler, and the creedal stance regarding its rejection.

Core Idea
Wiping over khuffs is an established practice with defined limits; rejecting it signals deviance.

Imam al-Samarqandi presents the fiqh ruling on wiping over leather socks (khuffs) during wudu. He specifies the duration for a resident and a traveler, and warns that rejecting this practice aligns one with the Rawafid sect. This passage combines a legal ruling with a creedal boundary.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice the concise style and the two parts: ruling and warning.
The passage starts with 'Issue fifty-four'.
2
Identify the key details
Find the duration for the resident (muqim) and the traveler (musafir). Note when the count starts ('from the time of the impurity').
The resident gets one day and one night; the traveler gets three days and nights.
3
Understand the creedal dimension
Notice that the rejection of this ruling is attributed to the Rawafid, a deviant sect. This shows the ruling is considered necessary knowledge.
The phrase 'This is sufficient for the intelligent' emphasizes clarity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسألة الرابعة والخمسون: ينبغي له أن يرى المسح على الخفين للمقيم يوما وليلة وللمسافر ثلاثة أيام ولياليهما من وقت الحدث ومن أبى المسح على الخفين فهو من الروافض وهذا كفاية للعاقل.
Issue fifty-four: He should hold that wiping over the khuffs (leather socks) for the resident is one day and one night, and for the traveler three days and their nights, from the time of the impurity. Whoever rejects wiping over the khuffs is from the Rawafid (a deviant sect). This is sufficient for the intelligent person.
Source pages p. 33

Commentary
This passage states a fiqh ruling on al-mash 'ala al-khuffayn. It defines the duration for a resident (muqim) as one day and one night, and for a traveler (musafir) as three days and their nights, starting from the time of the impurity event. It warns that rejecting this established practice aligns one with the Rawafid, a sect known for rejecting such rulings. The conclusion 'This is sufficient for the intelligent' signals the end of the issue.

Key Terms
المسح على الخفين
الروافض

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Wiping over khuffs is optional or a bid'ah.
Correction: The passage presents it as a necessary ruling of the Sunnah; rejection is a sign of deviance.

Checkpoint
What is the duration for wiping over khuffs for a traveler, according to this passage?
A) One day and one night
B) Three days and their nights
C) From sunrise to sunset
D) Until the next prayer

Key Takeaway
The ruling on wiping over khuffs is a clear sunnah practice; rejecting it is a deviation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a sunnah practice that is easy to dismiss or neglect, like wiping over khuffs, do you feel a pull to follow it or to rationalize abandoning it? What does your instinct reveal about your submission to the Prophet's way?
?
The passage ends with 'This is sufficient for the intelligent person.' Are you that intelligent person, or do you find yourself seeking further excuses or arguments to avoid accepting clear rulings? Examine your heart: is there any sunnah you resist due to pride or convenience?
?
Reflect on the warning: rejecting this practice marks one as being from the Rawafid. Do you ever judge others for differing in fiqh details, or do you recognize that rejecting established transmissions is a serious matter of creed? How can you ensure your devotion to the Sunnah is not merely intellectual but practical?

Blood and Pus Break Wudu
Samarqandi rules on blood/pus breaking wudu and gives a general purity principle.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that flowing blood, pus, and similar substances break wudu and require immediate renewal, and that intentionally neglecting this renders one an innovator whose prayer is invalid.

Core Idea
Ritual purity requires immediate renewal when any internal substance exits the body.

Ritual purity is essential for prayer, and certain bodily discharges nullify it. Imam Samarqandi explains that flowing blood, pus, and similar substances break wudu regardless of intent, and failing to repeat wudu makes one an innovator. This lesson establishes the ruling based on a general principle of interior-to-exterior flow.

Guided Path
1
Read the ruling on blood and pus
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to understand the specific ruling: when blood or pus flows from a wound, wudu is broken and must be renewed.
Notice that the ruling includes both intentional and unintentional flow.
2
Identify the general principle
The passage then gives a general rule: anything inside that appears outside breaks wudu; anything outside that enters inside breaks the fast (unless forgotten). Understand this principle.
This principle extends beyond blood and pus to other substances.
3
Consider the consequence of neglect
The passage warns that neglecting to repeat wudu makes one a mubtadi' (innovator) and praying behind such a person is invalid. Reflect on the importance of this ruling.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسألة السادسة والخمسون: وما نقول: إنه ينبغي له أن يعلم أنه إذا سال الدم والقيح وما أشبه ذلك من جرح فقد انتقض الوضوء ويرى إعادة الوضوء حقا. فاعلم أن كل شيء في باطن الإنسان إذا بان في ظاهره أو سال من الباطن إلى الظاهر فقد انتقض به الوضوء وكل ظاهر إذا دخل في الباطن يفسد صومه إلا أن يكون ناسيا. فمن احتجم أو سال من بدنه دم أو قيح وما أشبه ذلك متعمدا أو غير متعمد ولم يعد وضوءه فهو مبتدع لا تجوز الصلاة خلفه لأنه يصلي بغير وضوء. وهذا القدر كفاية للعاقل والله أعلم.
Issue fifty-six: And what we say: He should know that when blood, pus, and the like flow from a wound, then wudu has been broken, and he holds that repeating wudu is obligatory. Know that anything inside a person that appears on his exterior or flows from the interior to the exterior breaks his wudu. And anything exterior that enters the interior breaks his fast, unless he is forgetful. So whoever undergoes cupping or from whose body flows blood or pus or the like, intentionally or unintentionally, and does not repeat his wudu, is an innovator (mubtadi'). It is not permissible to pray behind him because he prays without wudu. This is sufficient for the intelligent, and Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 34

Commentary
This lesson gives a fiqh ruling related to purity (taharah). It states that any substance that exits the body from a wound—such as blood or pus—breaks wudu, and that repeating wudu is obligatory. It then provides a general principle: any internal substance that appears externally or flows out breaks wudu. Similarly, anything external that enters the interior (e.g., food and drink) breaks the fast, unless one forgets. The passage warns that neglecting to repeat wudu after such discharges makes one an innovator (mubtadi'), and praying behind such a person is invalid because he prays without wudu. This establishes the importance of proper ritual purity.

Key Terms
وضوء
نقض
دم
قيح
مبتدع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only intentional discharge breaks wudu; unintentional flow does not require renewal.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that blood or pus flowing 'intentionally or unintentionally' breaks wudu, so both cases require renewal.

Checkpoint
What happens to wudu according to this passage when blood flows from a wound unintentionally?
A) It remains valid
B) It is broken and must be renewed
C) It is broken only if the blood is a lot
D) It is broken only if intentional

Key Takeaway
Any substance exiting the body, including blood and pus, breaks wudu and requires immediate renewal, and neglecting this invalidates one's prayer.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever downplayed a small amount of blood or pus from a wound, delaying renewing your wudu? How does this passage's clear ruling that even unintentional flow breaks wudu confront your complacency? What will you do the next time you notice such a discharge?
?
The author declares that one who prays without renewing wudu after such discharge is an innovator and his prayer is invalid. Is there any area of your worship where you have compromised for convenience, knowing the ruling? What step will you take today to ensure your prayer is built on correct purity?
