Allah's Existence
intermediate
6 min
The Middle Path Between Qadariyya, Jabriyya, and Munzala
Samarqandi contrasts the correct believer's stance with three deviant sects.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the errors of three deviant sects regarding divine decree and the correct balanced position taught by the author.

Core Idea
True belief in divine decree requires affirming Allah's justice, human responsibility, and the need for repentance.

After laying out the proper responses to divine decree, Samarqandi addresses three distorted views that emerged in early Islamic history: the Qadariyya (who deny Allah's decree of evil), the Jabriyya (who deny human free will), and the Munzala (who hold a middle position on forgiveness). He shows how the correct believer avoids all three by simultaneously affirming Allah's justice and blaming oneself.

Guided Path
1
Identify the three sects and their errors
Read the passage where Samarqandi names the Qadari, Jabri, and Munzala. Understand what each one gets wrong: Qadari denies Allah's decree of evil as just, Jabri denies self-blame, Munzala denies forgiveness without repentance.
The name 'Munzala' is uncertain; it may refer to Murji'a or Mu'tazila.
2
Learn how to disassociate from each sect
Samarqandi provides a threefold formula: see the decree as just (avoid Qadari), repent and seek forgiveness (avoid Munzala), and blame yourself (avoid Jabri). Notice how he uses Quranic verses to support each action.
3
Apply the balance
Understand that the correct path is a combination: affirming Allah's decree as just while taking moral responsibility for your actions. This avoids extremes of fatalism and denial of divine power.
This balance is key to Sunni orthodoxy.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واعلم أن قضاء الله تعالى على أربعة أوجه: قضاء الطاعة، وقضاء المعصية، وقضاء النعمة، وقضاء الشدة. والمذهب المستقيم في ذلك: إذا قضى الله تعالى للعبد بالطاعة يستقبلها بالجهد والإخلاص حتى يكرمه الله تعالى بالتوفيق لقوله تعالى: {والذين جاهدوا فينا لنهدينهم سبلنا}. وإذا قضى الله تعالى بالمعصية يستقبلها بالاستغفار والتوبة والندامة حتى يرزقه الله تعالى التوبة والمغفرة لقوله تعالى: {إن الله يحب التوابين ويحب المتطهرين}. وإذا قضى الله تعالى بالنعمة للعبد فعليه بالشكر والسخاء حتى يكرمه الله تعالى بالزيادة لقوله تعالى: {لئن شكرتم لازيدنكم}. وإذا قضى الله تعالى بالشدة يستقبلها بالصبر والرضى حتى يعطيه الله كرامة الآخرة لقوله تعالى: {إنما يوفى الصابرون أجرهم بغير حساب} وقال الله تعالى: {والله يحب الصابرين}. وينبغي لك إذا وقعت في المعصية أن ترى قضاء الوقوع من الله عدلا ولا ترضى من نفسك الوقوع فيه وتتوب وتستغفر منه، لأن القدرى لا يرى قضاء الوقوع من الله عدلا ولا يرى الجبرى الملامة من نفسه والمنتزلى[؟] لا يرى المغفرة بغير التوبة. فإذا رأيت قضاء الوقوع من الله تعالى عدلا فقد تبرأت من مذهب القدرية، وإذا تبت واستغفرت الله تعالى فقد تبرأت من مذهب المنزلة[؟]، وإذا رأيت قضاء الوقوع من الله تعالى عدلا فقد عملت بهذه الآية: {قل كل من عند الله}، وإذا رأيت الملامة لنفسك فقد عملت بهذه الآية: {ربنا ظلمنا أنفسنا وإن لم تغفر لنا وترحمنا لنكونن من الخاسرين}، وإذا تبت واستغفرت الله تعالى فقد عملت بهذه الآية: {استغفروا ربكم إنه كان غفارا}. واعلم أن من لم يؤمن بالقضاء ولم ير تقدير الخير والشر من الله تعالى فهو مبتدع وهذه الحجة كفاية للمعاقل.
Know that the decree of Allah Most High is of four aspects: the decree of obedience, the decree of disobedience, the decree of blessing, and the decree of hardship. The correct way in that is: when Allah decrees obedience for a servant, he meets it with effort and sincerity so that Allah honors him with success (tawfiq), by His saying: 'And those who strive in Us, We will surely guide them to Our ways.' And when Allah decrees disobedience for a servant, he meets it with seeking forgiveness (istighfar), repentance (tawba), and regret (nadama) so that Allah provides him with repentance and forgiveness, by His saying: 'Indeed Allah loves those who repent and loves those who purify themselves.' And when Allah decrees blessing for a servant, upon him is gratitude (shukr) and generosity (sakha') so that Allah honors him with increase, by His saying: 'If you are grateful, I will surely increase you.' And when Allah decrees hardship for a servant, he meets it with patience (sabr) and contentment (rida) so that Allah gives him the honor of the Hereafter, by His saying: 'Indeed the patient will be paid their reward without account' and Allah said: 'And Allah loves the patient.' And it is incumbent upon you, when you fall into disobedience, to see the decree of the occurrence as just from Allah and not to be pleased with yourself for falling into it, and to repent and seek forgiveness from it, because the Qadari does not see the decree of the occurrence as just from Allah, and the Jabri does not see blame in himself, and the Munzali[؟] does not see forgiveness without repentance. So if you see the decree of the occurrence as just from Allah, you have disassociated from the way of the Qadariyya; and if you repent and seek forgiveness from Allah, you have disassociated from the way of the Munzala[؟]; and if you see the decree of the occurrence as just from Allah, you have acted upon this verse: 'Say: All is from Allah'; and if you see blame in yourself, you have acted upon this verse: 'Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers'; and if you repent and seek forgiveness from Allah, you have acted upon this verse: 'Ask forgiveness of your Lord; indeed He is ever Forgiving.' And know that whoever does not believe in the decree (qada) and does not see the determination (taqdir) of good and evil from Allah, he is an innovator (mubtadi'), and this proof is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 9

Commentary
This passage is a practical theological instruction on how a believer should understand and respond to divine decree (qada). It begins by classifying divine decree into four types based on what is decreed: obedience, disobedience, blessing, and hardship. For each type, the correct response is prescribed with supporting Quranic verses. For obedience, the response is effort and sincerity. For disobedience, it is seeking forgiveness, repentance, and regret. For blessing, it is gratitude and generosity. For hardship, it is patience and contentment. The author then addresses the specific case of falling into sin. He instructs the believer to see the decree of the occurrence as just (adl) from Allah, not to be pleased with oneself, and to repent. He contrasts this with the positions of three deviant sects: the Qadariyya (who deny that Allah decrees evil), the Jabriyya (who deny human responsibility), and the Munzala (possibly the Murji'a or Mu'tazila, who hold a middle position on forgiveness). By seeing the decree as just, one disassociates from Qadariyya; by repenting, one disassociates from Munzala; and by blaming oneself, one acts on the verse 'Say: All is from Allah' (4:78) and on the verse 'Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves' (7:23), while repentance aligns with 'Ask forgiveness of your Lord' (71:10). The passage concludes by stating that denial of Allah's decree and predetermination of good and evil constitutes innovation, and this argument suffices for the rational.

Key Terms
القدرية
الجبرية
المنزلة
عدل
ملامة
توبة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Accepting divine decree means one should not blame oneself for sins (Jabri position).
Correction: The correct belief is to see the decree as just from Allah while also blaming oneself and repenting, as Samarqandi teaches.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which sect denies that Allah's decree of evil is just?
A) Jabriyya
B) Qadariyya
C) Munzala
D) Not mentioned

Key Takeaway
To be a true believer in divine decree, one must affirm both Allah's justice in decreeing all events and one's own responsibility to repent and blame oneself for sins.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin, do you truly submit to Allah's decree as just, or do you harbor resentment? How would your heart change if you fully embraced His justice in that moment?
?
Do you honestly blame yourself for your sins, or do you shift responsibility to circumstances or others? Where does the Jabri error show up in your excuses?
?
Repentance is commanded even though the sin was decreed. Have you ever postponed repentance, assuming you could repent later? What does that delay reveal about your trust in Allah's forgiveness?

Allah's Existence
beginner
3 min
The Necessity of Believing in Divine Decree
Samarqandi warns that denial of divine decree is innovation and states the proof is sufficient.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that belief in Allah's comprehensive decree (qada and qadar) is obligatory, and that this teaching provides a rational proof against those who deny it.

Core Idea
Denial of divine decree is innovation, and the arguments presented suffice for the rational mind.

Samarqandi concludes his teaching with a strong warning: whoever does not believe that all good and evil are determined by Allah is an innovator (mubtadi'). He then asserts that the proofs he has provided are sufficient for those who reason. This lesson underscores the fundamental importance of this belief.

Guided Path
1
Understand the gravity of denying decree
Read the final sentence. Note that the author equates denial of taqdir of good and evil with innovation. Reflect on why this is so central.
This shows the seriousness of the belief.
2
Consider the sufficiency of the proof
The author says this argument is enough for the intelligent. Think about the arguments he has presented: the fourfold decree, the balanced response, the refutation of deviant sects, and the Quranic verses. They collectively form a comprehensive case.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واعلم أن قضاء الله تعالى على أربعة أوجه: قضاء الطاعة، وقضاء المعصية، وقضاء النعمة، وقضاء الشدة. والمذهب المستقيم في ذلك: إذا قضى الله تعالى للعبد بالطاعة يستقبلها بالجهد والإخلاص حتى يكرمه الله تعالى بالتوفيق لقوله تعالى: {والذين جاهدوا فينا لنهدينهم سبلنا}. وإذا قضى الله تعالى بالمعصية يستقبلها بالاستغفار والتوبة والندامة حتى يرزقه الله تعالى التوبة والمغفرة لقوله تعالى: {إن الله يحب التوابين ويحب المتطهرين}. وإذا قضى الله تعالى بالنعمة للعبد فعليه بالشكر والسخاء حتى يكرمه الله تعالى بالزيادة لقوله تعالى: {لئن شكرتم لازيدنكم}. وإذا قضى الله تعالى بالشدة يستقبلها بالصبر والرضى حتى يعطيه الله كرامة الآخرة لقوله تعالى: {إنما يوفى الصابرون أجرهم بغير حساب} وقال الله تعالى: {والله يحب الصابرين}. وينبغي لك إذا وقعت في المعصية أن ترى قضاء الوقوع من الله عدلا ولا ترضى من نفسك الوقوع فيه وتتوب وتستغفر منه، لأن القدرى لا يرى قضاء الوقوع من الله عدلا ولا يرى الجبرى الملامة من نفسه والمنتزلى[؟] لا يرى المغفرة بغير التوبة. فإذا رأيت قضاء الوقوع من الله تعالى عدلا فقد تبرأت من مذهب القدرية، وإذا تبت واستغفرت الله تعالى فقد تبرأت من مذهب المنزلة[؟]، وإذا رأيت قضاء الوقوع من الله تعالى عدلا فقد عملت بهذه الآية: {قل كل من عند الله}، وإذا رأيت الملامة لنفسك فقد عملت بهذه الآية: {ربنا ظلمنا أنفسنا وإن لم تغفر لنا وترحمنا لنكونن من الخاسرين}، وإذا تبت واستغفرت الله تعالى فقد عملت بهذه الآية: {استغفروا ربكم إنه كان غفارا}. واعلم أن من لم يؤمن بالقضاء ولم ير تقدير الخير والشر من الله تعالى فهو مبتدع وهذه الحجة كفاية للمعاقل.
Know that the decree of Allah Most High is of four aspects: the decree of obedience, the decree of disobedience, the decree of blessing, and the decree of hardship. The correct way in that is: when Allah decrees obedience for a servant, he meets it with effort and sincerity so that Allah honors him with success (tawfiq), by His saying: 'And those who strive in Us, We will surely guide them to Our ways.' And when Allah decrees disobedience for a servant, he meets it with seeking forgiveness (istighfar), repentance (tawba), and regret (nadama) so that Allah provides him with repentance and forgiveness, by His saying: 'Indeed Allah loves those who repent and loves those who purify themselves.' And when Allah decrees blessing for a servant, upon him is gratitude (shukr) and generosity (sakha') so that Allah honors him with increase, by His saying: 'If you are grateful, I will surely increase you.' And when Allah decrees hardship for a servant, he meets it with patience (sabr) and contentment (rida) so that Allah gives him the honor of the Hereafter, by His saying: 'Indeed the patient will be paid their reward without account' and Allah said: 'And Allah loves the patient.' And it is incumbent upon you, when you fall into disobedience, to see the decree of the occurrence as just from Allah and not to be pleased with yourself for falling into it, and to repent and seek forgiveness from it, because the Qadari does not see the decree of the occurrence as just from Allah, and the Jabri does not see blame in himself, and the Munzali[؟] does not see forgiveness without repentance. So if you see the decree of the occurrence as just from Allah, you have disassociated from the way of the Qadariyya; and if you repent and seek forgiveness from Allah, you have disassociated from the way of the Munzala[؟]; and if you see the decree of the occurrence as just from Allah, you have acted upon this verse: 'Say: All is from Allah'; and if you see blame in yourself, you have acted upon this verse: 'Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers'; and if you repent and seek forgiveness from Allah, you have acted upon this verse: 'Ask forgiveness of your Lord; indeed He is ever Forgiving.' What does Samarqandi say about someone who does not believe in Allah's taqdir of good and evil?

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly see the determination of good and evil from Allah in your own life, or do you sometimes attribute outcomes to sheer luck or your own planning? What would it mean to change that perspective today?
?
The author says this proof is sufficient for the intelligent. If you find yourself doubting the reality of divine decree, is it because the evidence is insufficient, or because your heart is not submitting? What step will you take to investigate the proofs you have been given?
?
Consider an area of your life where you feel resentment about your circumstances. How would believing that both the good and the evil are from Allah change your reaction? What would you need to let go of?

Allah's Existence
intermediate
5 min
God's Absolute Will and Human Responsibility
Samarqandi affirms God's absolute will and the possibility of change in destiny.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that God's absolute will does not negate human responsibility; rather, the possibility of change in destiny makes obedience meaningful.

Core Idea
God does as He wills, and the fortunate may become wretched.

Samarqandi begins by establishing a foundational principle: God's will is absolute and He is not questioned. He then introduces the crucial idea that a person's final state is not fixed in this life; the fortunate may become wretched and vice versa. This sets the stage for understanding why human actions matter.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice how Samarqandi grounds his argument in God's absolute authority before discussing change in destiny.
2
Identify the key assertion
Find the statement that 'the fortunate one may become wretched and the wretched one may become fortunate.' Why does Samarqandi say this is necessary?
3
Reflect on the implications
Ask yourself: If destiny were fixed, what would be the consequence for obedience and disobedience? Samarqandi gives three consequences: obedience wouldn't benefit, disobedience wouldn't harm, and disbelievers would be excused.
This logical implication is central to the lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثالثة والثلاثون: وما ذكرنا أنه ينبغي له أن يعلم أن الله تعالى ما شاء فعل وما شاء يفعل، له الحكم وليس أحد عليه الحكم، بل هو يفعل ما يشاء ويحكم ما يريد، لا يسأل عما يفعل وهم يسألون. اعلم وتيقن أن السعيد قد يشقى وأن الشقي قد يسعد، ولو لم يكن كذلك ما كان ينفع المطيع طاعة وما كان يضر العاصي معصية، ولكان الكفار معذورين عند ربهم بكفرهم. والدليل على صحة ما قلنا قوله تعالى: {يمحو الله ما يشاء ويثبت وعنده أم الكتاب} وقوله تعالى: {والله يحكم لا معقب لحكمه وهو سريع الحساب} وقوله تعالى: {إذا أراد شيئا أن يقول له كن فيكون}. وقال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم: «إن الرجل يكون ما بينه وبين الجنة إلا شبر فيجري على يديه شر فيختم له بالشقاوة، وإن الرجل يكون بينه وبين النار شبر فيجري على يديه خير وعمل صالح فيختم له بالسعادة». وروي عن عمر بن الخطاب رضي الله عنه أنه كان يدعو ويقول: اللهم يا رب إن كنت كتبت اسمي في ديوان الأشقياء فاصرفه إلى ديوان السعداء بفضلك يا رب. وروي عن عبد الله بن مسعود مثل هذا. واعلم أن الله لا يضيع أجر المحسنين، وقال الله تعالى: {من عمل صالحا فلنفسه ومن أساء فعليها}. ومن قال قد جف القلم بما هو كائن وفعل الله ما شاء فهو مبتدع. والذي يقول السعيد من سعد في بطن أمه والشقي من شقي في بطن أمه، فهذا من جهة الرزق والأجل والحياة، لأن رزق بعض العباد ضيق ورزق بعض العباد واسع، وحياة بعض العباد أقصر وحياة بعض العباد أطول. وقال عليه السلام: «كل مولود يولد على الفطرة» يعني الملة، إلا أن أبويه يهودانه وينصرانه ويمجسانه. فمن مات من أولاد الكفار واليهود والنصارى والمجوس أو المؤمنين، مصيرهم إلى الجنة، لأن النبي عليه السلام قال: «رفع عن أمتي الخطأ والنسيان وما استكرهوا عليه» عن النائم حتى يستيقظ، وعن المجنون حتى يفيق، وعن الصبي حتى يحتلم. فلو أن أحدا سعد في بطن أمه، فإذا بلغ مبلغ الرجال وعمل عمل السعداء أسعده الله بفضله. ولو لم يكن كذلك لما نفع أحدا طاعته ولما ضر أحدا معصيته. وهذا مذهب الجبرية. وفي هذا كفاية للعاقل.
The thirty-third question: And we mention that it is incumbent upon him to know that Allah Almighty does whatever He wills and will do whatever He wills; His is the judgment and no one has judgment over Him. Rather, He does what He wills and judges what He desires. He is not asked about what He does, but they are asked. Know and be certain that the fortunate one may become wretched and the wretched one may become fortunate. If it were not so, obedience would not benefit the obedient, disobedience would not harm the disobedient, and the disbelievers would be excused before their Lord for their disbelief. The proof of the correctness of what we say is His statement: {Allah erases what He wills and establishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book} and His statement: {And Allah judges; there is no reverser of His judgment, and He is swift in reckoning} and His statement: {When He wills something, He only says to it 'Be' and it is.} And the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: 'A man may be as close as a span to Paradise, then evil deeds overtake him and he ends up wretched; and a man may be as close as a span to Hellfire, then good deeds and righteous work overtake him and he ends up fortunate.' And it is narrated from Umar ibn al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him, that he used to supplicate: 'O Allah, my Lord, if You have written my name in the register of the wretched, transfer it to the register of the fortunate by Your favor, my Lord.' And it is narrated from Abdullah ibn Mas'ud similar to this. And know that Allah does not waste the reward of the doers of good. And Allah said: {Whoever does righteous deeds, it is for his own soul; whoever does evil, it is against it.} Whoever says 'the pen has dried with what is to be and Allah does what He wills' is an innovator. And the one who says 'the fortunate is fortunate in his mother's womb and the wretched is wretched in his mother's womb' – this is with regard to provision, lifespan, and life, for the provision of some servants is narrow and the provision of some servants is ample, and the life of some servants is shorter and the life of some servants is longer. And he, peace be upon him, said: 'Every child is born upon the fitrah' – meaning the religion – except that his parents make him a Jew, a Christian, or a Zoroastrian. So whoever dies among the children of the disbelievers, Jews, Christians, Zoroastrians, or believers – their destination is Paradise, because the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: 'The pen is lifted from my community concerning mistakes, forgetfulness, and what they are coerced into' – upon the sleeper until he wakes, upon the insane until he recovers, and upon the child until he reaches puberty. So if someone was fortunate in his mother's womb, when he reaches the age of men and does the deeds of the fortunate, Allah makes him fortunate by His grace. If it were not so, obedience would not benefit anyone and disobedience would not harm anyone. And this is the doctrine of the Jabriyya. And in this is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 20

Commentary
This passage is a theological argument affirming God's absolute will and the possibility of change in a person's final state (fortunate or wretched). Samarqandi begins by stating that God does whatever He wills, has sole judgment, and is not questioned about His actions. He then asserts that the fortunate (sa'id) may become wretched (shaqi) and vice versa; otherwise, obedience and disobedience would be meaningless and disbelievers would be excused. He provides three Quranic proofs: (1) Allah erases and establishes what He wills from the Preserved Tablet (13:39); (2) Allah's judgment is irreversible (13:41); (3) Allah creates by simply saying 'Be' (36:82). He then cites a hadith about a man close to Paradise ending in Hell, and another close to Hell ending in Paradise. The supplications of Umar and Ibn Mas'ud show that the Companions prayed for change in their written destiny. He then refutes two groups: (1) those who say 'the pen has dried' (jaf al-qalam) and (2) those who say that one's fate is fixed in the womb. He explains that statements about provision and lifespan are predestined, but a person's final state depends on their actions. He cites the hadith on fitra and the hadith on lifting the pen from children, concluding that children of disbelievers go to Paradise. He states that if someone were predetermined to be fortunate, when he reaches adulthood and does good deeds, God grants him fortune. If the opposite, then obedience would be futile – but that is the doctrine of the Jabriyya (extreme predestination). The passage thus upholds both divine decree and human responsibility.

Key Terms
مشيئة
سعيد
شقي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that because God's will is absolute, human actions are irrelevant and destiny is unchangeable.
Correction: Samarqandi argues exactly the opposite: God's will includes the possibility of change, making actions meaningful.

Checkpoint
What would be the consequence if the fortunate could not become wretched?
A) Obedience would be meaningless
B) Disbelievers would be excused
C) Both A and B
D) Neither

Key Takeaway
God's absolute will does not preclude change in destiny; rather, it makes human responsibility meaningful.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that your current state of faith is permanent, or are you striving with the fear that you might become wretched? What specific change in your life would make you more certain of your fate?
?
The lesson says that if destiny were fixed, disbelievers would be excused. How does this truth challenge any excuses you make for your own sins?
?
If you knew that your obedience could change your destiny, what would you start doing today that you have been avoiding?

Proofs for Change in Destiny and Refutation of Fatalism
Samarqandi provides Quranic, prophetic, and companion evidence for change in destiny and refutes fatalism.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Quranic and prophetic evidence for the possibility of change in destiny and why saying 'the pen has dried' is an innovation.

Core Idea
Quran and hadith show that destiny can change, and fatalism is bid'ah.

Samarqandi supports the previous assertion with three Quranic verses and a hadith. He also cites supplications of the Companions who prayed for a change in their written fate. He then explicitly refutes those who claim that 'the pen has dried' – a fatalistic view that denies any change.

Guided Path
1
Examine the Quranic proofs
Read the three verses quoted: 13:39, 13:41, and 36:82. What do they teach about God's power to change?
2
Consider the hadith and Companions' supplication
The hadith describes a man close to Paradise ending in Hell. Umar and Ibn Mas'ud prayed for transfer from one register to another. What does this imply about destiny?
3
Understand the refutation
Samarqandi declares that whoever says 'the pen has dried' is an innovator. Why is this view problematic?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثالثة والثلاثون: وما ذكرنا أنه ينبغي له أن يعلم أن الله تعالى ما شاء فعل وما شاء يفعل، له الحكم وليس أحد عليه الحكم، بل هو يفعل ما يشاء ويحكم ما يريد، لا يسأل عما يفعل وهم يسألون. اعلم وتيقن أن السعيد قد يشقى وأن الشقي قد يسعد، ولو لم يكن كذلك ما كان ينفع المطيع طاعة وما كان يضر العاصي معصية، ولكان الكفار معذورين عند ربهم بكفرهم. والدليل على صحة ما قلنا قوله تعالى: {يمحو الله ما يشاء ويثبت وعنده أم الكتاب} وقوله تعالى: {والله يحكم لا معقب لحكمه وهو سريع الحساب} وقوله تعالى: {إذا أراد شيئا أن يقول له كن فيكون}. وقال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم: «إن الرجل يكون ما بينه وبين الجنة إلا شبر فيجري على يديه شر فيختم له بالشقاوة، وإن الرجل يكون بينه وبين النار شبر فيجري على يديه خير وعمل صالح فيختم له بالسعادة». وروي عن عمر بن الخطاب رضي الله عنه أنه كان يدعو ويقول: اللهم يا رب إن كنت كتبت اسمي في ديوان الأشقياء فاصرفه إلى ديوان السعداء بفضلك يا رب. وروي عن عبد الله بن مسعود مثل هذا. واعلم أن الله لا يضيع أجر المحسنين، وقال الله تعالى: {من عمل صالحا فلنفسه ومن أساء فعليها}. ومن قال قد جف القلم بما هو كائن وفعل الله ما شاء فهو مبتدع. والذي يقول السعيد من سعد في بطن أمه والشقي من شقي في بطن أمه، فهذا من جهة الرزق والأجل والحياة، لأن رزق بعض العباد ضيق ورزق بعض العباد واسع، وحياة بعض العباد أقصر وحياة بعض العباد أطول. وقال عليه السلام: «كل مولود يولد على الفطرة» يعني الملة، إلا أن أبويه يهودانه وينصرانه ويمجسانه. فمن مات من أولاد الكفار واليهود والنصارى والمجوس أو المؤمنين، مصيرهم إلى الجنة، لأن النبي عليه السلام قال: «رفع عن أمتي الخطأ والنسيان وما استكرهوا عليه» عن النائم حتى يستيقظ، وعن المجنون حتى يفيق، وعن الصبي حتى يحتلم. فلو أن أحدا سعد في بطن أمه، فإذا بلغ مبلغ الرجال وعمل عمل السعداء أسعده الله بفضله. ولو لم يكن كذلك لما نفع أحدا طاعته ولما ضر أحدا معصيته. وهذا مذهب الجبرية. وفي هذا كفاية للعاقل.
The thirty-third question: And we mention that it is incumbent upon him to know that Allah Almighty does whatever He wills and will do whatever He wills; His is the judgment and no one has judgment over Him. Rather, He does what He wills and judges what He desires. He is not asked about what He does, but they are asked. Know and be certain that the fortunate one may become wretched and the wretched one may become fortunate. If it were not so, obedience would not benefit the obedient, disobedience would not harm the disobedient, and the disbelievers would be excused before their Lord for their disbelief. The proof of the correctness of what we say is His statement: {Allah erases what He wills and establishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book} and His statement: {And Allah judges; there is no reverser of His judgment, and He is swift in reckoning} and His statement: {When He wills something, He only says to it 'Be' and it is.} And the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: 'A man may be as close as a span to Paradise, then evil deeds overtake him and he ends up wretched; and a man may be as close as a span to Hellfire, then good deeds and righteous work overtake him and he ends up fortunate.' And it is narrated from Umar ibn al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him, that he used to supplicate: 'O Allah, my Lord, if You have written my name in the register of the wretched, transfer it to the register of the fortunate by Your favor, my Lord.' And it is narrated from Abdullah ibn Mas'ud similar to this. And know that Allah does not waste the reward of the doers of good. And Allah said: {Whoever does righteous deeds, it is for his own soul; whoever does evil, it is against it.} Whoever says 'the pen has dried with what is to be and Allah does what He wills' is an innovator. And the one who says 'the fortunate is fortunate in his mother's womb and the wretched is wretched in his mother's womb' – this is with regard to provision, lifespan, and life, for the provision of some servants is narrow and the provision of some servants is ample, and the life of some servants is shorter and the life of some servants is longer. And he, peace be upon him, said: 'Every child is born upon the fitrah' – meaning the religion – except that his parents make him a Jew, a Christian, or a Zoroastrian. So whoever dies among the children of the disbelievers, Jews, Christians, Zoroastrians, or believers – their destination is Paradise, because the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: 'The pen is lifted from my community concerning mistakes, forgetfulness, and what they are coerced into' – upon the sleeper until he wakes, upon the insane until he recovers, and upon the child until he reaches puberty. So if someone was fortunate in his mother's womb, when he reaches the age of men and does the deeds of the fortunate, Allah makes him fortunate by His grace. If it were not so, obedience would not benefit anyone and disobedience would not harm anyone. And this is the doctrine of the Jabriyya. And in this is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 20

Commentary
This passage is a theological argument affirming God's absolute will and the possibility of change in a person's final state (fortunate or wretched). Samarqandi begins by stating that God does whatever He wills, has sole judgment, and is not questioned about His actions. He then asserts that the fortunate (sa'id) may become wretched (shaqi) and vice versa; otherwise, obedience and disobedience would be meaningless and disbelievers would be excused. He provides three Quranic proofs: (1) Allah erases and establishes what He wills from the Preserved Tablet (13:39); (2) Allah's judgment is irreversible (13:41); (3) Allah creates by simply saying 'Be' (36:82). He then cites a hadith about a man close to Paradise ending in Hell, and another close to Hell ending in Paradise. The supplications of Umar and Ibn Mas'ud show that the Companions prayed for change in their written destiny. He then refutes two groups: (1) those who say 'the pen has dried' (jaf al-qalam) and (2) those who say that one's fate is fixed in the womb. He explains that statements about provision and lifespan are predestined, but a person's final state depends on their actions. He cites the hadith on fitra and the hadith on lifting the pen from children, concluding that children of disbelievers go to Paradise. He states that if someone were predetermined to be fortunate, when he reaches adulthood and does good deeds, God grants him fortune. If the opposite, then obedience would be futile – but that is the doctrine of the Jabriyya (extreme predestination). The passage thus upholds both divine decree and human responsibility.

Key Terms
جف القلم
محو
إثبات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Fatalists believe that since everything is written, prayer and effort are useless.
Correction: Samarqandi cites Quranic verses showing that Allah erases and establishes, and the Prophet and Companions prayed for change, refuting fatalism.

Checkpoint
According to Samarqandi, what is the status of someone who says 'the pen has dried'?
A) A true believer
B) A disbeliever
C) An innovator
D) Excused

Key Takeaway
The Quran and Sunnah affirm that destiny can change through supplication and deeds; fatalism is a rejected innovation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a difficult situation, do you truly believe that your sincere supplication can change what has been decreed, or do you silently accept it as unavoidable? Will you commit to making a specific dua today for a challenge you face, trusting that Allah can erase and establish?
?
The hadith shows a man close to Paradise ending in Hell due to evil deeds. Is there an area in your life where you feel spiritually 'safe' but are neglecting small sins? What one action will you take today to ensure your deeds lead to a good end?
?
Umar ibn al-Khattab begged Allah to transfer his name to the register of the fortunate. Do you have the same urgency about your final destiny, or do you assume your current state is fixed? What would you ask Allah to change in your life right now if you truly believed it could be erased and rewritten?

Allah's Existence
intermediate
6 min
Fitrah, Children, and the Rejection of Jabriyya
Samarqandi refutes predestinarian extremes, affirming fitrah and children's fate.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the hadith on fitrah and the lifting of the pen show that children are born pure and that predetermined fate in the womb applies only to provision and lifespan, not final spiritual state.

Core Idea
Children of disbelievers go to Paradise, and Jabriyya view is false.

Samarqandi addresses a common misunderstanding: that one's spiritual fate is fixed in the womb. He clarifies that statements about being 'fortunate in his mother's womb' refer only to provision and lifespan, not final state. He then cites the hadith on fitrah and the lifting of the pen to argue that children who die before puberty are in Paradise. He concludes that this opposes the Jabriyya doctrine.

Guided Path
1
Understand the clarification
Samarqandi explains that 'the fortunate in his mother's womb' refers only to rizq and lifespan, not to final salvation. Note the examples given.
2
Examine the fitrah hadith
The Prophet said every child is born on fitrah, then parents turn him. What does this tell us about children's default state?
3
Learn about the lifting of the pen
The pen is lifted for children, sleeper, insane. So children who die before puberty are not accountable. Where do they go?
4
Reject the Jabriyya conclusion
Samarqandi states that if one's fate were fixed in the womb, then actions wouldn't matter – that is Jabriyya doctrine. Why is this insufficient?

Commentary
This passage is a theological argument affirming God's absolute will and the possibility of change in a person's final state (fortunate or wretched). Samarqandi begins by stating that God does whatever He wills, has sole judgment, and is not questioned about His actions. He then asserts that the fortunate (sa'id) may become wretched (shaqi) and vice versa; otherwise, obedience and disobedience would be meaningless and disbelievers would be excused. He provides three Quranic proofs: (1) Allah erases and establishes what He wills from the Preserved Tablet (13:39); (2) Allah's judgment is irreversible (13:41); (3) Allah creates by simply saying 'Be' (36:82). He then cites a hadith about a man close to Paradise ending in Hell, and another close to Hell ending in Paradise. The supplications of Umar and Ibn Mas'ud show that the Companions prayed for change in their written destiny. He then refutes two groups: (1) those who say 'the pen has dried' (jaf al-qalam) and (2) those who say that one's fate is fixed in the womb. He explains that statements about provision and lifespan are predestined, but a person's final state depends on their actions. He cites the hadith on fitra and the hadith on lifting the pen from children, concluding that children of disbelievers go to Paradise. He states that if someone were predetermined to be fortunate, when he reaches adulthood and does good deeds, God grants him fortune. If the opposite, then obedience would be futile – but that is the doctrine of the Jabriyya (extreme predestination). The passage thus upholds both divine decree and human responsibility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believe that every child is born upon fitrah and that children of disbelievers go to Paradise, how does that change the way you view parents who raise their children in other religions? Are you moved to have compassion or to share the truth with them? What will you do this week to act on this belief?
?
The passage argues that if fate were completely fixed, obedience and disobedience would be meaningless. Reflect on your own life: do you sometimes act as if your actions don't matter—like you're just going through the motions? In what area of your obedience to Allah have you become lazy or fatalistic? Identify one specific act of worship where you will consciously choose to exert effort this week, knowing it truly counts.
?
The Prophet said the pen is lifted from children until puberty. This means children are not accountable. But what about you? You are accountable now. Are you living as if you are still under the pen? In what way are you delaying repentance or avoiding responsibility? Make a firm intention today to start fulfilling a duty you have been neglecting.

Prophethood
beginner
3 min
Prophets are higher than saints
Samarqandi affirms the superiority of prophets over saints.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic belief that prophets hold a higher rank with Allah than saints.

Core Idea
Prophets are higher than saints in rank with Allah.

In this short passage, Imam al-Samarqandi states a clear and fundamental belief of Ahl al-Sunnah: the prophets (anbiya) occupy a station with Allah that is above that of the saints (awliya). This is not a matter of personal virtue but of divine selection. We will examine this statement and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice the wording: 'necessary for him to know' emphasizes the importance of this belief.
The passage is incomplete due to OCR, but the core statement is clear.
2
Identify the key terms
Look at the terms 'anbiya' (prophets) and 'awliya' (saints). Understand that they are distinct categories in Islamic theology.
Saints are close friends of Allah, but prophets are messengers with a higher station.
3
Reflect on the implication
Consider why this distinction matters. It guards against elevating saints to the level of prophets, which would blur the unique role of prophethood.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما قلنا انه ينبغي له ان يعلم ان مراتب الانبياء عند الله تعالى اعلى من مراتب الاولياء ( فمن قال )
We said that it is necessary for him to know that the ranks of the prophets with Allah Most High are higher than the ranks of the saints. (Whoever says...)
Source pages p. 18

Commentary
This passage begins the thirty-first issue, stating a fundamental belief: the prophets (anbiya') hold higher ranks with Allah than the saints (awliya). The text cuts off with 'whoever says' (faman qala), likely beginning a refutation of those who claim otherwise. Due to the incomplete nature, only the core statement is available.

Key Terms
مراتب
الانبياء
الاولياء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people think saints are equal or superior to prophets in rank with Allah.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that the ranks of prophets are higher than those of saints.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, whose ranks are higher with Allah?
A) The saints
B) The prophets
C) Both equal
D) The angels

Key Takeaway
Prophets are superior in rank to saints with Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever find yourself admiring a saintly person more than you admire the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him)? Why? And what does this lesson teach you about where your loyalty must lie?
?
Reflect on your heart: is there any saint, living or dead, whom you hold in higher esteem than the prophets? If so, what must you change?
?
This lesson declares that knowing the superiority of prophets over saints is 'necessary for him to know'. Do you truly feel that necessity in your heart, or have you taken this for granted? What will you do to internalize it?

Prophethood
intermediate
5 min
Prophets Are Superior to Saints
Samarqandi asserts the superiority of prophets over saints and warns against the opposite view.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sunni doctrine holds that prophets are superior to saints and what the consequences are for claiming the opposite.

Core Idea
Prophets are higher than all saints; denial of this is innovation.

Samarqandi presents a crucial doctrinal boundary: the absolute superiority of prophets over saints. He not only affirms this but warns against the opposite view, labeling it as a specific innovation (Karamiyya). The passage uses Quranic verses and a prophetic hadith to anchor this hierarchy.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without over-analysing. Then read the translation to grasp the overall claim and warning.
Notice the phrase 'صار مبتدعا يسمى كراميا' – this identifies a deviant group.
2
Identify key terms and the logical structure
Find and understand the key terms: مراتب الأنبياء, مراتب الأولياء, مبتدع, كرامية. Observe the logical flow: statement of superiority → warning → reason → evidence.
3
Examine the supporting evidence
Look at the two Quranic verses and the hadith. Ask: How do these proofs support the claim that prophets are higher and that obedience to them is obedience to Allah?
The verses link obedience to Allah and the Messenger together.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الحادية والثلاثون
وما قلنا انه ينبغي له ان يعلم ان مراتب الانبياء عند الله تعالى اعلى من مراتب الاولياء فمن قال ان الاولياء مراتبهم اعلى من مراتب الانبياء صار مبتدعا يسمى كراميا لان الأولياء لا يبلغون الى مراتب الانبياء الا بعد طاعة الله تعالى ورسوله لان طاعة الانبياء هي طاعة الله تعالى لقوله تعالى ومن يطع الله والرسول فاولئك مع الذين أنعم الله عليهم الآية وقوله تعالى ومن يطع الله ورسوله يدخله جنات تجري من تحتها الانهار الاية وقال النبي عليه السلام انا سيد ولد آدم ولا فخر الى آخر الحديث وهذا كفاية للمعاقل والله اعلم
Question thirty-one: What we said that he should know that the ranks of prophets with God Almighty are higher than the ranks of saints. Whoever says that the ranks of saints are higher than the ranks of prophets becomes an innovator called a Karami (Karamiyya). Because the saints do not reach the ranks of the prophets except after obedience to God Almighty and His Messenger. Because obedience to the prophets is obedience to God Almighty, as He says: {Whoever obeys God and the Messenger, they are with those whom God has blessed} (Quran 4:69), and He says: {Whoever obeys God and the Messenger, He will admit him to gardens beneath which rivers flow} (Quran 4:13). And the Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and I do not boast' to the end of the hadith. This is sufficient for the intelligent, and God knows best.
Source pages p. 18

Commentary
This passage is a classification and refutation. Samarqandi states the doctrine that prophets are superior to saints. He then warns that anyone who claims the opposite becomes an innovator and is associated with the Karamiyya sect. The justification is that saints can only reach high ranks by following the prophets, and obedience to the prophets counts as obedience to God. He provides Quranic verses that link obedience to God and the Messenger with reward, and a hadith where the Prophet declares his precedence among humanity. The passage ends with a typical closure.

Key Terms
مراتب الأنبياء
مراتب الأولياء
مبتدع
كرامية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that saints can reach or surpass the rank of prophets.
Correction: Samarqandi asserts that prophets are superior to all saints; saints only reach high ranks through following prophets.

Checkpoint
What is the name given to those who claim that saints are higher than prophets according to this passage?
A) Karamiyya
B) Mu'tazila
C) Jahmiyya
D) Ash'ariyya

Key Takeaway
Prophets hold the highest rank among creation; obedience to them is obedience to Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that no living scholar today can surpass the Prophet (peace be upon him) in rank? How does this affect your reverence for his teachings compared to any modern guide?
?
This lesson says that obedience to the Prophet is obedience to Allah. Are there areas in your life where you prioritize your own opinion or a scholar's opinion over the explicit command of the Prophet? Commit to correcting one such area this week.
?
The Karamiyya were denounced for elevating saints above prophets. Examine your heart: do you ever feel more inspired by the stories of saints than by the life of the Prophet? What does that reveal about your spiritual priorities?

Prophethood
intermediate
10 min
Affirmation of Karamat al-Awliya'
Samarqandi proves the miracles of saints from Quran and refutes deniers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why denying the miracles of saints is considered innovation (bid'a) and how the Quran provides clear evidence for their reality without diminishing prophethood.

Core Idea
Miracles of saints are real and do not diminish prophethood; the Quran proves them.

Imam Samarqandi now addresses a crucial doctrine: the miracles of saints (karamat al-awliya'). Some deny them out of misguided fear that they might undermine the miracles of prophets. In this passage, Samarqandi not only affirms the reality of these miracles but also systematically refutes any objection. He brings compelling Quranic examples and a powerful logical argument. Read carefully to see how he establishes this teaching as a matter of orthodox belief.

Guided Path
1
Read the full passage carefully
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the structure: a ruling, an analysis of deniers, evidence, and a refutation.
The passage is dense; take your time.
2
Identify the three states of the denier
Samarqandi explains that someone who denies karamat out of concern for prophethood falls into one of three states. Find these three and note the outcome for each.
This is the core logical argument.
3
Examine the Quranic evidence
List the examples Samarqandi gives: Asif bin Barkhiya, Maryam, the People of the Cave, and the Isra'. For each, ask: Was this person a prophet? What miracle occurred?
These examples show that such wonders are not exclusive to prophets.
4
Reflect on the refutation of the objection
Samarqandi responds to an objection about traveling to Mecca in one night. He uses the Prophet's Night Journey and Iblis's speed to argue. Understand why this refutes the denier.
This step ties the lesson together.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثانية والثلاثون: وما قلنا إنه ينبغي له أن يقر بكرامة الأولياء، لأن من أنكر كرامات الأولياء فهو مبتدع. ومن أنكر كرامة الأولياء وهو يظن أن في ذلك هدم معجزات الأنبياء، فلا يخرج عن أحد أحوال ثلاثة: إما أن ينكر الآيات التي في كتاب الله تعالى أولاً، فإن أنكر الآيات فقد كفر. وإن لم ينكر الآيات وآمن بها ولكن يقول كانوا هم أنبياء فقد كفر أيضاً. وإن لم ينكر الآيات وآمن بها ولم يقل أنبياء فقد صح عنده أن هذه الكرامة كانت لغير الأنبياء ويجوز ذلك، لأن الله تعالى قال: {قال الذي عنده علم من الكتاب إني آتيك به قبل أن يرتد إليك طرفك} وكان هذا آصف بن برخيا وكان من الأولياء ولم يكن نبياً وكان من قوم سليمان بن داود. فلما جاز أن يكون من قوم سليمان كرامة الأولياء، أليس يجوز فكرامة محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم كرامة الأولياء، ومحمد خير من سليمان وأمته خير من أمته؟ فإن قال المخالف: تلك الكرامة كانت من قبل سليمان، فتقول: هذه الكرامة من قبل محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم. وقوله تعالى: {وهزي إليك بجذع النخلة} أخرج الله تعالى من الشجرة اليابسة رطباً لأجل مريم أكرمها بذلك، ومريم لم تكن نبية. وقوله تعالى: {كما دخل عليها زكريا المحراب وجد عندها رزقا} قال: {يا مريم أنى لك هذا} قالت: {هو من عند الله}. وكذلك في قصة أهل الكهف أكرمهم الله تعالى ولم يكونوا أنبياء. فلما جاز أن يكون في الأولين، لم لا يجوز أن يكون في أمة محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم كرامة الأولياء؟ وقد قال الله تعالى: {كنتم خير أمة أخرجت للناس تأمرون بالمعروف وتنهون عن المنكر}. فإن قال المخالف: إن فلانا يذهب في ليلة واحدة إلى بيت الله ويرجع، هذا لا يكون أبداً. قلنا: إن الله أكرم النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم بكرامة لم يكرم بها أحد قط حين أسري به وعُرج به إلى السموات السبع وبلغ ما شاء الله بمسيرة أربعة آلاف سنة ورجع، فهل كرامة أعظم من هذه؟ وأيضاً يقال للمخالف: المؤمن خير من الكافر، فإنا وجدنا من كان يسير من الكفار في ساعة واحدة من المشرق إلى المغرب وهو إبليس لعنه الله. فإذا كان الكافر هكذا، لا تنكر كرامة الأولياء. وهذا كفاية للعاقل.
The thirty-second question: And we say that it is incumbent upon him to affirm the miracles of the saints (karamat al-awliya'), because whoever denies the miracles of the saints is an innovator. And whoever denies the miracles of the saints thinking thereby to undermine the miracles of the prophets, he is in one of three states: either he denies the verses in the Book of Allah Almighty – then if he denies the verses, he has disbelieved; or he does not deny the verses and believes in them but says they were prophets – then he has also disbelieved; or if he does not deny the verses and believes in them and does not say they were prophets, then he has confirmed that this miracle (karama) was for other than prophets, and that is permissible. For Allah Almighty said: {He who had knowledge from the Book said, 'I will bring it to you before your glance returns to you'} – and this was Asif bin Barkhiya, who was among the saints and was not a prophet, and he was of the people of Solomon bin David. So when it is permissible that there be a miracle of the saints from among the people of Solomon, is it not permissible that there be a miracle of the saints for the sake of Muhammad, peace be upon him? And Muhammad is better than Solomon, and his community is better than his community. If the opponent says: That miracle was through Solomon, you say: This miracle is through Muhammad, peace be upon him. And His statement: {And shake toward you the trunk of the palm tree} – Allah brought out from the dry tree ripe dates for the sake of Mary, honoring her with that, and Mary was not a prophet. And His statement: {Whenever Zechariah entered upon her in the sanctuary, he found with her provision. He said, 'O Mary, from where is this?' She said, 'It is from Allah.'} And likewise in the story of the People of the Cave, Allah honored them and they were not prophets. So when it is permissible in former times, why should it not be permissible in the community of Muhammad, peace be upon him, the miracles of the saints? And Allah has said: {You are the best community ever brought forth for mankind, commanding what is right and forbidding what is wrong.} If the opponent says: So-and-so goes in one night to the House of Allah and returns – this would never happen. We say: Allah honored the Prophet, peace be upon him, with a miracle with which He has never honored anyone when He took him on the Night Journey and ascended him to the seven heavens, and he reached what Allah willed over a distance of four thousand years and returned. Is there any miracle greater than this? And also it is said to the opponent: The believer is better than the disbeliever, for we have found one who travels from among the disbelievers in one hour from the east to the west, and he is Iblis, may Allah curse him. When the disbeliever is like this, do not deny the miracles of the saints. And this is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 19

Commentary
This passage is a proof and defense of the reality of the miracles (karamat) of the saints (awliya'). It begins by stating that denying the karamat of the awliya' is innovation (bid'a). The author then considers the case of someone who denies these miracles out of fear that they might undermine the miracles of the prophets, and he analyzes three possible positions: (1) the denier denies the Quranic verses that mention these wonders – that is disbelief; (2) he accepts the verses but claims that those individuals were prophets – that is also disbelief, because he is ascribing prophethood to non-prophets; (3) he accepts the verses and does not claim prophethood for them – then he has correctly recognized that these were karamat for non-prophets, and that is permissible. Samarqandi then provides Quranic evidence: Asif bin Barkhiya (a saint) brought the throne of the Queen of Sheba to Solomon in the blink of an eye (Quran 27:40); Mary received ripe dates from a dry palm tree (Quran 19:25); Zechariah found provision with Mary (Quran 3:37); and the People of the Cave were miraculously protected (Quran 18). All these individuals were not prophets. If such wonders occurred in previous communities, they can occur in the Muslim community, which is the best community (Quran 3:110). The author then refutes an objection: someone says it is impossible for a person to travel to Mecca and return in one night. Samarqandi counters by mentioning the Prophet's Night Journey (Isra') which covered a distance of four thousand years in one night – a far greater miracle. Finally, he argues that if the disbeliever Iblis can travel from east to west in an hour, then a believer should not be denied such ability. This refutes the deniers.

Key Terms
كرامة
أولياء
معجزات
مبتدع
آصف بن برخيا
مريم
أهل الكهف
الإسراء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that acknowledging karamat of awliya' diminishes the prophethood.
Correction: Samarqandi shows they are distinct and non-contradictory; karamat do not rival prophethood.

Checkpoint
According to Samarqandi, what is the status of someone who denies the miracles of the saints because he thinks it undermines the miracles of prophets?
A) He is correct in his caution
B) He must either deny the Quranic verses or commit disbelief
C) He is a righteous believer
D) He is excused because of his good intention

Key Takeaway
Denying the miracles of saints is innovation, and the Quran provides clear examples proving their reality without undermining prophethood.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that Allah can honor ordinary believers with miracles today, or do you instinctively dismiss such reports as superstition? What would it take for you to accept a karama from someone you know?
?
If you were to witness a miracle performed by a saint, would your faith increase or would you feel threatened for the uniqueness of prophethood? Examine your heart: is there any pride that resists the idea that others might receive divine favors?
?
When you read about the karama of Mary or Asif, do you see yourself as part of the 'best community' that can experience such wonders, or do you feel unworthy? What change in your spiritual life would make you more open to receiving Allah's special favors?

Module 9 · Lesson 1

Miracles
intermediate
6 min
Affirming Saints' Miracles: The Three Cases
Samarqandi states the ruling and enumerates three logical possibilities for those who deny karamat.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why denying the miracles of saints is considered innovation and the three logical possibilities for those who claim to defend prophetic miracles.

Core Idea
Denying saints' miracles does not protect prophethood; it leads to disbelief.

Samarqandi opens this topic by stating that affirming the miracles of saints (karamat) is obligatory; denial makes one an innovator. He then addresses the concern that affirming karamat might undermine the unique miracles of prophets (mu'jizat). By analyzing three possible positions, he shows that only correct understanding avoids both disbelief and error.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening ruling
Understand the initial claim: denying karamat makes one an innovator. The author then examines the motive behind such denial.
2
Identify the three possibilities
Look for the three cases: deny the Quranic verses, accept them but claim those were prophets, or accept them without claiming prophethood. Note the consequence of each.
The first two lead to disbelief; the third is correct.
3
Study the first Quranic example
Samarqandi provides the story of Asaf ibn Barkhiya (Quran 27:40) as evidence that a non-prophet can perform a miracle. Reflect on how this supports the third case.
Asaf was a saint, not a prophet, yet he brought the throne of Bilqis.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثانية والثلثون
وما قلنا انه ينبغي له ان يقر بكرامة الأولياء لان من انكر كرامات الاولياء فهو مبتدع و من انكر كرامة الاولياء وهو يظن ان فى ذلك هدم معجزات الانبياء فلا يخرج عن احد احوال ثلاثة اما ان ينكر الايات التى فى كتاب الله تعالى اولا فان انكر الايات فقد كفر وان لم ينكر الايات و آمن بها ولكن يقول كانوا هم انبياء فقد كفر ايضا وان لم ينكر الآيات و آمن بها ولم يقل انبياء فقد صح عنده ان هذه الكرامة كانت لغير الانبياء ويجوز ذلك لان الله تعالى قال قال الذى عنده علم من الكتاب إنا آتيك به قبل ان يرتد اليك طرفك وكان هذا آصف بن برخيا وكان من الاولياء ولم يكن نيا وكان من قوم سليمان بن داود فلما جاز ان يكون من قوم سليمان كرامة الأولياء اليس يجوز فرامة محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم كرامة الأولياء ومحمد خير من سليمان وامته خير من امته فان قال المخالف تلك الكرامة كانت من قبل سليمان فتقول هذه الكرامة من قبل محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم وقوله تعالى وهزى اليك بجذع النخلة اخرج الله تعالى من الشجرة اليابسة نمرة لاجل مريم أكرمها بذلك ومريم لم تكن نبية وقوله تعالى كما دخل عليها زكريا المحراب وجد عندها رزقا قال يا مريم انى لك هذا قالت هو من عند الله وكذلك في قصة اهل الكهف اكرمهم الله تعالى ولم يكونوا انياء فلما جاز ان يكون في الأولين لم لا يجوزان يكون فى امة محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم كرامة الاولياء وقد قال الله تعالى كنتم خير امة اخرجت للناس تأمرون بالمعروف وتنهون عن المنكر فان قال المخالف ان فلانا يذهب في ليلة واحدة الى بيت الله ويرجع هذا لا يكون ابدا قلنا ان الله اكرم النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم بكرامة لم يكرم بها احدا قط حين اسرى به وعرج به السموات السبع وبلغ ماشاء الله بمسيرة اربعة آلاف سنة ورجع فهل كرامة اعظم من هذه وايضا يقال للمخالف المؤمن خير من الكافر فانا وجدنا من كان يسير من الكفار
Question thirty-two: What we said that he should affirm the miracles of saints (karamat al-awliya), because whoever denies the miracles of saints is an innovator. Whoever denies miracles of saints thinking thereby he is undermining the miracles of prophets does not escape one of three cases: either he denies the signs that are in the Book of God Almighty; if he denies the signs, he has disbelieved. If he does not deny the signs and believes in them but says they were prophets, he has disbelieved as well. And if he does not deny the signs and believes in them and does not say they were prophets, then it is confirmed for him that this miracle (karama) was for other than prophets, and that is permissible. Because God Almighty said: {The one who had knowledge of the Book said, 'I will bring it to you before your glance returns to you'} (Quran 27:40). And this was Asaf ibn Barkhiya, and he was among the saints and was not a prophet, and he was from the people of Solomon son of David. So when it was permissible to have miracles of saints from the people of Solomon, is it not permissible to have miracles of the Ummah of Muhammad (peace be upon him)? And Muhammad is better than Solomon, and his Ummah is better than his Ummah. If the opponent says: 'That miracle was through Solomon,' you say: 'This miracle is through Muhammad (peace be upon him).' And His saying: {And shake towards you the trunk of the palm tree} (Quran 19:25) – God Almighty brought forth from the dry tree fresh dates for Mary, honoring her with that, and Mary was not a prophet. And His saying: {Whenever Zachariah entered upon her in the sanctuary, he found with her provision. He said, 'O Mary, from where is this for you?' She said, 'It is from God'} (Quran 3:37). Similarly in the story of the People of the Cave, God Almighty honored them and they were not prophets. So when it was permissible among the earlier peoples, why should it not be permissible in the Ummah of Muhammad (peace be upon him) to have miracles of saints? And God Almighty said: {You are the best community ever brought forth for mankind, enjoining right and forbidding wrong} (Quran 3:110). If the opponent says: 'So-and-so goes in one night to the House of God and returns – this can never happen.' We say: Indeed God honored the Prophet (peace be upon him) with a miracle with which He never honored anyone else: when He took him by night and ascended him through the seven heavens and he reached what God willed, a distance of four thousand years, and he returned. Is there any miracle greater than this? And also it is said to the opponent: The believer is better than the disbeliever. For we have found that some disbelievers used to travel...
Source pages p. 19

Commentary
This passage is a definition and refutation with objection-and-answer. Samarqandi begins by stating that one must affirm the miracles (karamat) of saints; denial makes one an innovator. He then analyzes the position of someone who thinks that affirming karamat undermines the prophets' miracles (mu'jizat). He lists three possibilities: 1) Deny the Quranic verses about such miracles (e.g., Asaf, Mary, People of the Cave) – that is disbelief. 2) Accept the verses but claim those individuals were prophets – also disbelief. 3) Accept the verses and not claim prophethood – then it is established that these were karamat, and it is permissible. He then provides examples from the Quran: Asaf ibn Barkhiya bringing the throne of Bilqis (prophet Solomon's associate), the story of Mary and the dates, Zachariah's provision, and the People of the Cave. He argues that if such honors were given to non-prophets in past communities, then in the Ummah of Muhammad, which is the best community, it is even more appropriate. He then answers an objection: if someone says that such a feat (like traveling to Mecca in one night) is impossible, he cites the Isra and Mi'raj of the Prophet, which is even greater. The passage ends with an incomplete argument comparing believers and disbelievers in terms of travel speed.

Key Terms
كرامة الأولياء
معجزات الأنبياء
مبتدع
آصف بن برخيا

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Affirming karamat of saints reduces the uniqueness of prophetic miracles.
Correction: Samarqandi shows that karamat are different in kind and do not compete with mu'jizat; the Quran itself records such events for non-prophets.

Checkpoint
According to Samarqandi, what is the consequence for someone who accepts the Quranic verses about miracles but claims those individuals were prophets?
A) They are correct, as long as they believe in the verses.
B) They become disbelievers.
C) They are innovators but not disbelievers.
D) They are excused because of ignorance.

Key Takeaway
The correct position on saints' miracles is to affirm them as distinct from prophetic miracles, avoiding both denial and false claims of prophethood.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever been hesitant to accept the miracles of righteous people because you feared it might lessen your reverence for the prophets? If so, what would it take for you to trust the Quran's testimony over your own caution?
?
When you hear of extraordinary spiritual experiences or miracles among Muslims today, do you immediately dismiss them or seek to explain them away? How does Samarqandi's argument challenge that impulse?
?
This lesson shows that denying karamat can lead to disbelief. Is there any area of your faith where you hold back from accepting what the Quran teaches because you think you are protecting something more important? What would it mean to surrender that fear and submit fully?

Module 9 · Lesson 2

Miracles
intermediate
7 min
Quranic Proofs and Refuting Objections on Saints' Miracles
Samarqandi provides Quranic evidence and refutes an objection using Isra.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Samarqandi uses Quranic examples and the superiority of Muhammad's Ummah to argue for karamat, and how he responds to the objection of impossibility.

Core Idea
The best community deserves even greater miraculous honors than previous ones.

After establishing the principle, Samarqandi provides multiple Quranic proofs: the dates for Mary, provision for Mary, and the People of the Cave. He then argues from the virtue of this Ummah and answers the specific objection that traveling to Mecca in one night is impossible, citing the Isra and Mi'raj. The passage ends with an incomplete comparison between believers and disbelievers.

Guided Path
1
Examine the Quranic examples
Read the stories of Mary (dates, provision) and the People of the Cave. Note that none of them were prophets.
2
Understand the superiority argument
Samarqandi argues that if such honors occurred in earlier communities, they should certainly occur in the best Ummah (Quran 3:110).
3
Analyze the objection and answer
The opponent suggests that traveling long distances in one night is impossible. Samarqandi counters with the Isra and Mi'raj, which is far greater.
The passage ends before the argument about believers vs. disbelievers is complete.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثانية والثلثون
وما قلنا انه ينبغي له ان يقر بكرامة الأولياء لان من انكر كرامات الاولياء فهو مبتدع و من انكر كرامة الاولياء وهو يظن ان فى ذلك هدم معجزات الانبياء فلا يخرج عن احد احوال ثلاثة اما ان ينكر الايات التى فى كتاب الله تعالى اولا فان انكر الايات فقد كفر وان لم ينكر الايات و آمن بها ولكن يقول كانوا هم انبياء فقد كفر ايضا وان لم ينكر الآيات و آمن بها ولم يقل انبياء فقد صح عنده ان هذه الكرامة كانت لغير الانبياء ويجوز ذلك لان الله تعالى قال قال الذى عنده علم من الكتاب إنا آتيك به قبل ان يرتد اليك طرفك وكان هذا آصف بن برخيا وكان من الاولياء ولم يكن نيا وكان من قوم سليمان بن داود فلما جاز ان يكون من قوم سليمان كرامة الأولياء اليس يجوز فرامة محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم كرامة الأولياء ومحمد خير من سليمان وامته خير من امته فان قال المخالف تلك الكرامة كانت من قبل سليمان فتقول هذه الكرامة من قبل محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم وقوله تعالى وهزى اليك بجذع النخلة اخرج الله تعالى من الشجرة اليابسة نمرة لاجل مريم أكرمها بذلك ومريم لم تكن نبية وقوله تعالى كما دخل عليها زكريا المحراب وجد عندها رزقا قال يا مريم انى لك هذا قالت هو من عند الله وكذلك في قصة اهل الكهف اكرمهم الله تعالى ولم يكونوا انياء فلما جاز ان يكون في الأولين لم لا يجوزان يكون فى امة محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم كرامة الاولياء وقد قال الله تعالى كنتم خير امة اخرجت للناس تأمرون بالمعروف وتنهون عن المنكر فان قال المخالف ان فلانا يذهب في ليلة واحدة الى بيت الله ويرجع هذا لا يكون ابدا قلنا ان الله اكرم النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم بكرامة لم يكرم بها احدا قط حين اسرى به وعرج به السموات السبع وبلغ ماشاء الله بمسيرة اربعة آلاف سنة ورجع فهل كرامة اعظم من هذه وايضا يقال للمخالف المؤمن خير من الكافر فانا وجدنا من كان يسير من الكفار
Question thirty-two: What we said that he should affirm the miracles of saints (karamat al-awliya), because whoever denies the miracles of saints is an innovator. Whoever denies miracles of saints thinking thereby he is undermining the miracles of prophets does not escape one of three cases: either he denies the signs that are in the Book of God Almighty; if he denies the signs, he has disbelieved. If he does not deny the signs and believes in them but says they were prophets, he has disbelieved as well. And if he does not deny the signs and believes in them and does not say they were prophets, then it is confirmed for him that this miracle (karama) was for other than prophets, and that is permissible. Because God Almighty said: {The one who had knowledge of the Book said, 'I will bring it to you before your glance returns to you'} (Quran 27:40). And this was Asaf ibn Barkhiya, and he was among the saints and was not a prophet, and he was from the people of Solomon son of David. So when it was permissible to have miracles of saints from the people of Solomon, is it not permissible to have miracles of the Ummah of Muhammad (peace be upon him)? And Muhammad is better than Solomon, and his Ummah is better than his Ummah. If the opponent says: 'That miracle was through Solomon,' you say: 'This miracle is through Muhammad (peace be upon him).' And His saying: {And shake towards you the trunk of the palm tree} (Quran 19:25) – God Almighty brought forth from the dry tree fresh dates for Mary, honoring her with that, and Mary was not a prophet. And His saying: {Whenever Zachariah entered upon her in the sanctuary, he found with her provision. He said, 'O Mary, from where is this for you?' She said, 'It is from God'} (Quran 3:37). Similarly in the story of the People of the Cave, God Almighty honored them and they were not prophets. So when it was permissible among the earlier peoples, why should it not be permissible in the Ummah of Muhammad (peace be upon him) to have miracles of saints? And God Almighty said: {You are the best community ever brought forth for mankind, enjoining right and forbidding wrong} (Quran 3:110). If the opponent says: 'So-and-so goes in one night to the House of God and returns – this can never happen.' We say: Indeed God honored the Prophet (peace be upon him) with a miracle with which He never honored anyone else: when He took him by night and ascended him through the seven heavens and he reached what God willed, a distance of four thousand years, and he returned. Is there any miracle greater than this? And also it is said to the opponent: The believer is better than the disbeliever. For we have found that some disbelievers used to travel...
Source pages p. 19

Commentary
This passage is a definition and refutation with objection-and-answer. Samarqandi begins by stating that one must affirm the miracles (karamat) of saints; denial makes one an innovator. He then analyzes the position of someone who thinks that affirming karamat undermines the prophets' miracles (mu'jizat). He lists three possibilities: 1) Deny the Quranic verses about such miracles (e.g., Asaf, Mary, People of the Cave) – that is disbelief. 2) Accept the verses but claim those individuals were prophets – also disbelief. 3) Accept the verses and not claim prophethood – then it is established that these were karamat, and it is permissible. He then provides examples from the Quran: Asaf ibn Barkhiya bringing the throne of Bilqis (prophet Solomon's associate), the story of Mary and the dates, Zachariah's provision, and the People of the Cave. He argues that if such honors were given to non-prophets in past communities, then in the Ummah of Muhammad, which is the best community, it is even more appropriate. He then answers an objection: if someone says that such a feat (like traveling to Mecca in one night) is impossible, he cites the Isra and Mi'raj of the Prophet, which is even greater. The passage ends with an incomplete argument comparing believers and disbelievers in terms of travel speed.

Key Terms
كرامة الأولياء
آصف بن برخيا
أهل الكهف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Miracles like traveling long distances in one night are impossible for saints.
Correction: Samarqandi gives the example of Isra and Mi'raj, which is far greater, showing that Allah can do anything.

Checkpoint
What is the main argument Samarqandi uses to respond to the objection that traveling to Mecca in one night is impossible?
A) Such travel is physically possible with modern means.
B) The Prophet's Isra and Mi'raj is a greater miracle, so such a feat is possible.
C) Saints are actually prophets in disguise.
D) The objector is a disbeliever for denying Allah's power.

Key Takeaway
Allah's power is unlimited; He honors saints with miracles just as He honored previous righteous individuals, and the miracles of Muhammad's Ummah are even more fitting.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have learned that Allah honored non-prophets like Mary and the People of the Cave with miracles. Do you truly believe that He can honor you despite your shortcomings? What specific fear or doubt in your heart prevents you from expecting His extraordinary help in your life? Commit today to ask Him for something you consider impossible.
?
The Ummah of Muhammad is called the best community—but does your daily life reflect that honor? In what area have you settled for mediocrity or negligence, forgetting that you belong to the noblest group of people? Write down one change you will make this week to live up to that status.
?
If the Prophet's Isra and Mi'raj can overcome physical laws, why do you hesitate to trust that Allah can solve your unsolvable problems? Are you limiting His power based on your own reasoning? Repent for any secret disbelief in His ability to do what seems impossible to you, and ask Him for a breakthrough.
