Divine Transcendence
beginner
7 min
The Reality of the Quran in Writing and Recitation
Samarqandi establishes the reality of the Quran in mus'hafs and refutes metaphorical interpretation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Quran written in mus'hafs and recited is truly the actual speech of Allah, and you will be able to refute the claim that it is merely metaphorical.

Core Idea
The Quran in our hands and on our tongues is the real, uncreated speech of Allah.

Imam Samarqandi begins by firmly establishing a foundational doctrine: the Quran that we write, recite, and even what children learn is truly the Quran, not a representation. He then addresses an opponent who claims that the Quran in mus'hafs is not real, using a logical argument about how Jibril heard and conveyed it. This lesson will guide you through this crucial defense of the Quran's reality.

Guided Path
1
Read the initial assertions
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice how Samarqandi repeatedly affirms that what is written, recited, and even children's tablets contain the real Quran (bil-ḥaqīqa).
The term 'ḥaqīqa' (reality) is key—it contrasts with 'majāz' (metaphor).
2
Analyze the Jibril argument
Follow the reductio ad absurdum: if Jibril heard the Quran in reality but delivered it metaphorically, that would be concealment. But revelation is in reality, so denial is invalid.
Notice how the argument forces the opponent to choose between two untenable positions.
3
Reflect on the consequence
The author concludes that denying the reality of the Quran in mus'hafs leads to denying revelation itself, which is kufr. Consider the seriousness of this position.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما ذكرنا انه ينبغي له ان يعلم ان الذى كتب في المصاحف هو قرآن بالحقيقة ونحن نقرأ القرآن بالحقيقة وفينا القرآن وما يكتب الصبيان في الالواح هو قرآن واعلم ان القرآن كلام الله غير مخلوق ومن انكر وقال ان مافي المصاحف ليس يقرأن بالحقيقة فقل له ان جبريل عليه السلام سمع هذا القرآن بالحقيقة او بالمجاز فان كان سمعه بالحقيقة وانزل على محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم بالمجاز فقد كتم بالحقيقة وانه انزل على محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم بالحقيقة فلم ينكر انه كلام الله تعالى فان قال المخالف بعض من القرآن انزل بالحقيقة وبعضه انزل بالمجاز فقد صار القرأن قرأنين وهذا محال ومن انكر التنزيل فهو كافر بالله تعالى فان قال ليس فى الدنيا قرأن ولا فى المصاحف والكراريس فقل له اين قول الله تعالى تبارك الذى نزل الفرقان على عبده وقوله انا نحن نزلنا الذكر وقوله طه ما انزلنا عليك القرآن لتشقى وقوله هم تنزيل الكتاب وقوله لو انزلنا هذا القرأن على جبل وقوله وانه لتنزيل رب العالين ومن انكر وقال ليس مافي المصاحف قرأنا فقد انكر الايات كلها لان اسم الكتاب لا يقع الا على شيء يكون فيه مكتوبا وقد قال الله تعالى الم ذلك الكتاب لا ريب فيه يعنى لاشك فيه فالله تعالى امر بقراءة القرآن فقال فاقرؤا ماتيسر من القرآن والله تعالى امر بالاستماع لقوله تعالى واذا قرئ القرآن فاستمعوا له وانصتوا الاية وما تقرأ انت ونحن نسمع منك كلام الله تعالى بالحقيقة والدليل قوله تعالى يسمعون كلام الله ثم يحرفون من بعد ما عقلوه الآية وقوله حتى يسمع كلام الله فالله تعالى من على النبي صلى الله تعالى عليه وسلم وقال ولقد اتيناك سبعا من المثانى والقرآن العظيم فاذا لم يكن ما فى المصاحف فاتحة الكتاب. ولاما في الكراريس فبأي شيء من عليه قالله تعالى نهي عن مس المصاحف إلا بالطهارة لقوله تعالى لا يمسه الا المطهرون تنزيل من رب العالمين وقوله انه لقرآن كريم الاية فلولم يكن ما في المصاحف قرآنا ما نهى عن مسها واعلم ان الله تعالى قال هذا القرآن بلا هجاء وحرف وبلا تعليم بعد تعليم وبلا نغمة بعد نغمة وبلا صوت بعد صوت وبلا وقت بعد وقت فالله تعالى قال هذا القرأن بلا هجاء ولا حرف ولاصوت كما قلنا وسمع جبريل عليه السلام من الله تعالى مثل ذلك وقرأ جبريل على محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم بحرف وصوت ونحن نقرأ بصوت وحرف ونكتب بحرف وليس فرق بين الذي قال الله تعالى وبين الذي سمع جبريل من الله تعالى وبين الذى قرا جبريل على محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم وبين الذي قرأ محمد على خلق الله تعالى وما نقرأ بيننا لان كله كلام الله غير مخلوق
And what we mentioned: it is fitting for him to know that what is written in the mus'hafs is truly the Qur'an, and we truly recite the Qur'an, and within us is the Qur'an, and what children write on tablets is the Qur'an. Know that the Qur'an is the speech of Allah, uncreated. Whoever denies and says that what is in the mus'hafs is not truly recited, say to him: Did Jibril (peace be upon him) hear this Qur'an in reality or metaphorically? If he heard it in reality and sent it down to Muhammad (ﷺ) metaphorically, then he concealed the reality. But it was sent down to Muhammad (ﷺ) in reality, so do not deny that it is the speech of Allah. If the opponent says that part of the Qur'an was sent down in reality and part was sent down metaphorically, then the Qur'an would become two Qur'ans, and that is impossible. And whoever denies the revelation is a disbeliever in Allah. If he says there is no Qur'an in the world, neither in mus'hafs nor in books, then say to him: Where is the saying of Allah: 'Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion upon His servant' (25:1) and 'Indeed, it is We who sent down the Reminder' (15:9) and 'Ta Ha, We have not sent down the Qur'an upon you to cause you distress' (20:1-2) and 'Alif Lam Ra, a Book whose verses are perfected...' (11:1) and 'If We had sent down this Qur'an upon a mountain' (59:21) and 'And indeed, it is a revelation of the Lord of the worlds' (26:192). Whoever denies and says that what is in the mus'hafs is not the Qur'an has denied all the verses, because the name 'Book' only applies to something that is written. And Allah has commanded the recitation of the Qur'an: 'So recite what is easy from the Qur'an' (73:20). And Allah commanded listening: 'And when the Qur'an is recited, listen to it and be silent' (7:204). And you recite and we hear from you the speech of Allah in reality. The proof is His saying: 'They hear the speech of Allah and then distort it after they have understood it' (2:75) and 'Until he hears the speech of Allah' (9:6). Allah bestowed upon the Prophet and said: 'And We have given you seven of the oft-repeated and the great Qur'an' (15:87). So if what is in the mus'hafs is not the Opening of the Book nor what is in the books, then with what did He bestow upon him? Allah forbade touching the mus'hafs except with purity, for He says: 'None touch it except the purified' (56:79) – a revelation from the Lord of the worlds. And He says: 'Indeed, it is a noble Qur'an' (56:77). So if what is in the mus'hafs were not the Qur'an, He would not have forbidden touching them. And know that Allah said this Qur'an without letters and sounds, without education after education, without melody after melody, without sound after sound, without time after time. So Allah said this Qur'an without letters or sounds, as we have said. Jibril (peace be upon him) heard from Allah something like that, and Jibril recited to Muhammad (ﷺ) with letter and sound, and we recite with sound and letter and write with letters. There is no difference between what Allah said, what Jibril heard from Allah, what Jibril recited to Muhammad, what Muhammad recited to creation, and what we recite among ourselves, because all of it is the speech of Allah, uncreated.
Source pages p. 22

Commentary
This passage is a sustained defense of the doctrine that the Quran is the actual, uncreated speech of Allah, addressing and refuting opposing views. The author first establishes that what is written in mus'hafs, recited, and even written by children on tablets is truly the Quran (al-haqīqa). Then he affirms that the Quran is Allah's speech, uncreated. He refutes an opponent who claims that what is in mus'hafs is not real Quran: he argues via reductio about Jibril's hearing and revelation—if Jibril heard it in reality and sent it down metaphorically, that would be concealment; but it was sent down in reality. If part was sent down in reality and part metaphorically, that would create two Qur'ans, which is absurd. Denial of revelation is kufr. He then refutes another claim that there is no Quran in the world at all, citing numerous verses that refer to the Quran being sent down, and that the name 'Book' implies something written. He further uses the commands to recite and listen to the Quran as proof that the recited words are truly Allah's speech. He cites verses where the disbelievers 'hear the speech of Allah' and 'until he hears the speech of Allah' to show that the recited Quran is indeed Allah's speech. He argues that if the mus'hafs did not contain the real Quran, then Allah would not have bestowed the Opening and the great Quran upon the Prophet. The prohibition of touching mus'hafs except with purity (56:79) shows that what is inside is indeed the Quran. Finally, he clarifies the distinction between the uncreated divine speech (which is without letters, sounds, etc.) and its created forms of recitation and writing. Allah's speech in His essence is without letters or sounds; Jibril heard it in that manner and then recited it to the Prophet with letters and sounds. The recitation and writing are created acts, but what is recited and written is the uncreated speech of Allah. There is no difference in reality: all of it is the same uncreated speech of Allah.

كلام الله
غير مخلوق

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Quran in mus'hafs is not the real speech of Allah but only a metaphor or representation.
Correction: Samarqandi argues that if Jibril heard the Quran in reality and conveyed it in reality, then what is written and recited is truly the Quran, not a metaphor.

Checkpoint
According to Samarqandi, what is the consequence if someone says that part of the Quran was sent down in reality and part metaphorically?
A) It would be permissible.
B) The Quran would become two Qur'ans, which is impossible.
C) It would mean the Quran is created.
D) It would mean Jibril is mistaken.

Key Takeaway
The Quran written and recited is literally the speech of Allah, uncreated, and not a metaphor.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you pick up a mushaf, do you truly believe that the paper and ink contain the uncreated speech of the Creator? If you truly believed this, how would it change the way you hold it, read it, and live by it? Commit to one specific change in your reverence for the mushaf starting today.
?
The author warns that denying the revelation is disbelief. Have you ever allowed doubts about the Quran's divine origin to enter your heart—perhaps due to intellectual arguments or personal struggles? Take a moment to repent and affirm your belief in tanzil, and resolve to seek knowledge that strengthens your certainty.
?
If every letter you recite is literally Allah's speech, then your recitation is a direct encounter with Him. How does that make you feel? What will you do to ensure that your next recitation is filled with the awe and presence it demands? Decide on a practical step before you pray or read Quran today.

Divine Transcendence
beginner
8 min
Scriptural Proofs and the Distinction of Divine Speech
Samarqandi provides multiple scriptural proofs and distinguishes between uncreated speech and created recitation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Quran in mus'hafs is the uncreated speech of Allah, supported by verses, commands, and the prohibition of touching, and you will grasp the distinction between the divine speech and its created recitation.

Core Idea
The Quran is uncreated; its recitation and writing are created acts but convey the same uncreated speech.

In this second part, Imam Samarqandi provides multiple scriptural proofs that the Quran is the actual speech of Allah. He cites verses about its revelation, commands to recite and listen, and the prohibition of touching mus'hafs without purity. Finally, he clarifies a crucial point: Allah's speech in His essence is without letters or sounds, yet the recited Quran with letters and sounds is still that same uncreated speech. This lesson will take you through these proofs and the important distinction.

Guided Path
1
Examine the scriptural proofs
Read the list of verses that speak of the Quran being sent down (tanzīl). Notice how Samarqandi uses them to argue that denying the Quran in mus'hafs is denying all these verses.
Key verses include 25:1, 15:9, 20:1-2, etc.
2
Understand the commands
The author cites commands to recite (73:20) and to listen to the Quran (7:204). These commands imply that what is recited is truly Allah's speech.
Also consider the verse about hearing Allah's speech (2:75, 9:6).
3
Analyze the prohibition argument
The verse 'None touch it except the purified' (56:79) is used as evidence: if the mus'hafs did not contain the real Quran, the prohibition would be meaningless.
This is a powerful argument from sacred practice.
4
Grasp the distinction
Finally, Samarqandi distinguishes between Allah's uncreated speech (without letters, sounds, or time) and its created manifestation in recitation and writing, yet affirms they are the same speech.
This resolves the apparent tension between the uncreatedness and the created forms.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

مافي المصاحف ليس يقرأن بالحقيقة فقل له ان جبريل عليه السلام سمع هذا القرآن بالحقيقة او بالمجاز فان كان سمعه بالحقيقة وانزل على محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم بالمجاز فقد كتم بالحقيقة وانه انزل على محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم بالحقيقة فلم ينكر انه كلام الله تعالى فان قال المخالف بعض من القرآن انزل بالحقيقة وبعضه انزل بالمجاز فقد صار القرأن قرأنين وهذا محال ومن انكر التنزيل فهو كافر بالله تعالى فان قال ليس فى الدنيا قرأن ولا فى المصاحف والكراريس فقل له اين قول الله تعالى تبارك الذى نزل الفرقان على عبده وقوله انا نحن نزلنا الذكر وقوله طه ما انزلنا عليك القرآن لتشقى وقوله هم تنزيل الكتاب وقوله لو انزلنا هذا القرأن على جبل وقوله وانه لتنزيل رب العالين ومن انكر وقال ليس مافي المصاحف قرأنا فقد انكر الايات كلها لان اسم الكتاب لا يقع الا على شيء يكون فيه مكتوبا وقد قال الله تعالى الم ذلك الكتاب لا ريب فيه يعنى لاشك فيه فالله تعالى امر بقراءة القرآن فقال فاقرؤا ماتيسر من القرآن والله تعالى امر بالاستماع لقوله تعالى واذا قرئ القرآن فاستمعوا له وانصتوا الاية وما تقرأ انت ونحن نسمع منك كلام الله تعالى بالحقيقة والدليل قوله تعالى يسمعون كلام الله ثم يحرفون من بعد ما عقلوه الآية وقوله حتى يسمع كلام الله فالله تعالى من على النبي صلى الله تعالى عليه وسلم وقال ولقد اتيناك سبعا من المثانى والقرآن العظيم فاذا لم يكن ما فى المصاحف فاتحة الكتاب. ولاما في الكراريس فبأي شيء من عليه قالله تعالى نهي عن مس المصاحف إلا بالطهارة لقوله تعالى لا يمسه الا المطهرون تنزيل من رب العالمين وقوله انه لقرآن كريم الاية فلولم يكن ما في المصاحف قرآنا ما نهى عن مسها واعلم ان الله تعالى قال هذا القرآن بلا هجاء وحرف وبلا تعليم بعد تعليم وبلا نغمة بعد نغمة وبلا صوت بعد صوت وبلا وقت بعد وقت فالله تعالى قال هذا القرأن بلا هجاء ولا حرف ولاصوت كما قلنا وسمع جبريل عليه السلام من الله تعالى مثل ذلك وقرأ جبريل على محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم بحرف وصوت ونحن نقرأ بصوت وحرف ونكتب بحرف وليس فرق بين الذي قال الله تعالى وبين الذي سمع جبريل من الله تعالى وبين الذى قرا جبريل على محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم وبين الذي قرأ محمد على خلق الله تعالى وما نقرأ بيننا لان كله كلام الله غير مخلوق
And what we mentioned: it is fitting for him to know that what is written in the mus'hafs is truly the Qur'an, and we truly recite the Qur'an, and within us is the Qur'an, and what children write on tablets is the Qur'an. Know that the Qur'an is the speech of Allah, uncreated. Whoever denies and says that what is in the mus'hafs is not truly recited, say to him: Did Jibril (peace be upon him) hear this Qur'an in reality or metaphorically? If he heard it in reality and sent it down to Muhammad (ﷺ) metaphorically, then he concealed the reality. But it was sent down to Muhammad (ﷺ) in reality, so do not deny that it is the speech of Allah. If the opponent says that part of the Qur'an was sent down in reality and part was sent down metaphorically, then the Qur'an would become two Qur'ans, and that is impossible. And whoever denies the revelation is a disbeliever in Allah. If he says there is no Qur'an in the world, neither in mus'hafs nor in books, then say to him: Where is the saying of Allah: 'Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion upon His servant' (25:1) and 'Indeed, it is We who sent down the Reminder' (15:9) and 'Ta Ha, We have not sent down the Qur'an upon you to cause you distress' (20:1-2) and 'Alif Lam Ra, a Book whose verses are perfected...' (11:1) and 'If We had sent down this Qur'an upon a mountain' (59:21) and 'And indeed, it is a revelation of the Lord of the worlds' (26:192). Whoever denies and says that what is in the mus'hafs is not the Qur'an has denied all the verses, because the name 'Book' only applies to something that is written. And Allah has commanded the recitation of the Qur'an: 'So recite what is easy from the Qur'an' (73:20). And Allah commanded listening: 'And when the Qur'an is recited, listen to it and be silent' (7:204). And you recite and we hear from you the speech of Allah in reality. The proof is His saying: 'They hear the speech of Allah and then distort it after they have understood it' (2:75) and 'Until he hears the speech of Allah' (9:6). Allah bestowed upon the Prophet and said: 'And We have given you seven of the oft-repeated and the great Qur'an' (15:87). So if what is in the mus'hafs is not the Opening of the Book nor what is in the books, then with what did He bestow upon him? Allah forbade touching the mus'hafs except with purity, for He says: 'None touch it except the purified' (56:79) – a revelation from the Lord of the worlds. And He says: 'Indeed, it is a noble Qur'an' (56:77). So if what is in the mus'hafs were not the Qur'an, He would not have forbidden touching them. And know that Allah said this Qur'an without letters and sounds, without education after education, without melody after melody, without sound after sound, without time after time. So Allah said this Qur'an without letters or sounds, as we have said. Jibril (peace be upon him) heard from Allah something like that, and Jibril recited to Muhammad (ﷺ) with letter and sound, and we recite with sound and letter and write with letters. There is no difference between what Allah said, what Jibril heard from Allah, what Jibril recited to Muhammad, what Muhammad recited to creation, and what we recite among ourselves, because all of it is the speech of Allah, uncreated.
Source pages p. 22

Commentary
This passage is a sustained defense of the doctrine that the Quran is the actual, uncreated speech of Allah, addressing and refuting opposing views. The author first establishes that what is written in mus'hafs, recited, and even written by children on tablets is truly the Quran (al-haqīqa). Then he affirms that the Quran is Allah's speech, uncreated. He refutes an opponent who claims that what is in mus'hafs is not real Quran: he argues via reductio about Jibril's hearing and revelation—if Jibril heard it in reality and sent it down metaphorically, that would be concealment; but it was sent down in reality. If part was sent down in reality and part metaphorically, that would create two Qur'ans, which is absurd. Denial of revelation is kufr. He then refutes another claim that there is no Quran in the world at all, citing numerous verses that refer to the Quran being sent down, and that the name 'Book' implies something written. He further uses the commands to recite and listen to the Quran as proof that the recited words are truly Allah's speech. He cites verses where the disbelievers 'hear the speech of Allah' and 'until he hears the speech of Allah' to show that the recited Quran is indeed Allah's speech. He argues that if the mus'hafs did not contain the real Quran, then Allah would not have bestowed the Opening and the great Quran upon the Prophet. The prohibition of touching mus'hafs except with purity (56:79) shows that what is inside is indeed the Quran. Finally, he clarifies the distinction between the uncreated divine speech (which is without letters, sounds, etc.) and its created forms of recitation and writing. Allah's speech in His essence is without letters or sounds; Jibril heard it in that manner and then recited it to the Prophet with letters and sounds. The recitation and writing are created acts, but what is recited and written is the uncreated speech of Allah. There is no difference in reality: all of it is the same uncreated speech of Allah.

Key Terms
قرآن
كلام الله
غير مخلوق
حقيقة
مصحف
تنزيل
هجاء
حرف
صوت

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that the written or recited Quran is a created entity separate from the uncreated speech of Allah.
Correction: Samarqandi clarifies that the speech itself is uncreated, while the letters and sounds we use are created. But what is conveyed is the same uncreated speech.

Checkpoint
According to Samarqandi, why does the Quran verse 'None touch it except the purified' (56:79) prove that what is in the mus'hafs is truly the Quran?
A) Because it commands purification before prayer.
B) Because it shows that the mus'haf is sacred.
C) Because it would be meaningless to forbid touching something that is not the real Quran.
D) Because it indicates that only angels touch it.

Key Takeaway
The Quran we recite and write is truly the uncreated speech of Allah, as proven by numerous verses and the prohibition of touching mus'hafs without purity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You recite the Quran daily. Is your recitation an act of mere repetition, or do you truly believe that the words you utter are the uncreated speech of the Lord of the worlds? How would your recitation change if you fully internalized that you are handling something that is not a created book but Allah's eternal attribute? What will you change in your upcoming recitation?
?
When you hold a mus'haf, do you treat it as just a book, or as the container of Allah's uncreated speech? The lesson mentions Allah forbade touching it except with purity. Are you mindful of the purity of your hands and heart when you touch the Quran? If not, what steps will you take to show reverence?
?
The lesson says there is no difference between what Allah said, what Jibril heard, and what you recite. Does your heart tremble when you recite, knowing that you are uttering the very speech of Allah? If not, what is missing in your faith? Will you ask Allah to grant you the certainty that this is His uncreated speech?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
5 min
The Quran is Uncreated Speech
Defines the Quran as uncreated while its written form is created, refutes objections.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni distinction between the uncreated Quranic speech and the created physical script, and how to answer common objections.

Core Idea
The Quran as divine speech is uncreated; only the ink and paper are created.

This passage from Imam al-Samarqandi's Al-Sawad al-A'zam defends a central article of Sunni belief: that the Quran is Allah's uncreated attribute of speech. Yet we hold mushafs written by created human hands. How can both be true? The author clarifies the distinction between the eternal speech and its temporal expression, then arms the reader with crisp answers to skeptical questions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without interruption. Then read the English translation. Notice the author's confident tone and the structure: a positive statement, then a series of objections and answers.
2
Identify the core affirmation
The passage begins by affirming that the Quran we write and read is truly the Quran, without addition or subtraction. Then it distinguishes between the created materials (paper, ink, pen) and the uncreated written speech. This is the key distinction.
The author immediately labels anyone who says the Quran is created as a disbeliever — this shows the seriousness of the issue.
3
Study the objection-and-answer series
The passage lists eight objections from an opponent about the Quran's modality (when, how, where, etc.). For each, the author gives a concise answer that negates the attribute. Understand how each answer preserves divine transcendence.
The pattern: opponent asks about a category of creation (time, place, voice), and the answer denies it with 'bila' (without).
4
Reflect on the final argument about letters
The opponent argues that because he can change the length of letters, the Quran must be created. The author counters by showing that the 'letter-ness' remains regardless of length, and that variations in recitation or writing do not affect the uncreated essence of the speech.
This refutes the idea that human manipulation of the script implies the speech itself is created.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وانقرأ ونكتب فى المصاحف فهو قرآن لا نزيد فيه حرفا ولا ننقص منه، والورق والكاغد والمداد والقلم مخلوق والمكتوب كلام الله تعالى غير مخلوق بالحقيقة. ومن قال القرآن مخلوق فهو كافر بالله. فان قال المخالف هل قال الله تعالى الكلام؟ فقل نعم. فان قال متى؟ فقل بلا متى. فان قال كيف؟ فقل بلا كيف. فان قال أين؟ فقل بلا أين. فان قال كم؟ فقل بلا كم. فان قال حفضا أو رفعا؟ فقل لا حفضا ولا رفعا. فان قال بصوت أو بغير صوت؟ فقل بلا صوت ولا حرف. فان قال المخالف المكتوبات والحروف مخلوقة لاني اكتب ان شئت طولت وان شئت قصرت. فقل له: ان شئت تطويل الحروف أو تقصيرها فهل يرتفع عنها اسم الحرفية؟ واذا كان الناس يقولون فلان طويل القراءة فلان قصير القراءة، هل يجوز أن يقولوا القرآن طويل أو قصير؟ والذي يطول القراءة ويخفف ويقصر فكله كلام الله تعالى ليس فيه فرق. وكذلك من طول كتابته بالحروف أو قصر. ومن قال في القرآن شيء غير ما وصفنا فهو مبتدع. وهذا كفاية للعاقل.
And we read and write in the mushafs, so it is the Quran. We do not add a letter to it nor subtract from it. The paper, the sheet (kaghid), the ink, and the pen are created, and what is written is the speech of Allah Most High, uncreated in reality. Whoever says the Quran is created is a disbeliever in Allah. If the opponent says: Did Allah say speech? Say: Yes. If he says: When? Say: Without when. If he says: How? Say: Without how. If he says: Where? Say: Without where. If he says: How much? Say: Without how much. If he says: Lowered or raised? Say: Neither lowered nor raised. If he says: With a voice or without a voice? Say: Without voice and without letter. If the opponent says: The written things and letters are created because I write, if I wish I make them long and if I wish I make them short. Say to him: If you wish to lengthen the letters or shorten them, does the name of 'letter-ness' cease from them? And since people say: So-and-so has long recitation, so-and-so has short recitation, is it permissible that they say the Quran is long or short? And the one who lengthens recitation and lightens and shortens, all of it is the speech of Allah Most High, there is no difference in it. Similarly, the one who lengthens his writing with letters or shortens it. And whoever says about the Quran something other than what we have described is an innovator. And this is sufficient for the intelligent person.
Source pages p. 24

Commentary
This passage is a theological argument defending the Sunni doctrine that the Quran is the uncreated speech of Allah, while the physical materials in which it is written (paper, ink, pen) are created. It begins by affirming that the Quran we read and write in mushafs is indeed the Quran without addition or subtraction. The material components of the writing are created, but the 'written' — that is, the divine speech itself — is uncreated in reality. The author then delivers a strong judgment: whoever says the Quran is created is a disbeliever. Next, the passage presents a series of hypothetical objections from an opponent and provides concise responses: the opponent asks about the 'when,' 'how,' 'where,' 'how much,' whether it is raised or lowered, and whether it is with voice or without. Each answer negates the attribute implied (e.g., 'without when' denies temporal modality). The key objection: the opponent says the written letters are created because he can manipulate their length. The author responds by pointing out that even if one lengthens or shortens letters, the 'letter-ness' remains; similarly, one can speak of recitation as long or short, but that does not attribute length to the Quran itself. All variations in recitation or writing are accidents that do not affect the uncreated essence of the divine speech. The passage concludes that anyone who says anything contrary to this description is an innovator, and this is sufficient for the rational person.

مخلوق
غير مخلوق
كلام الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that because the mushaf (physical book) is created, the Quran itself is created.
Correction: The passage clarifies that the Quran as divine speech is uncreated; only the physical script and materials are created.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what is created?
A) The Quran as speech of Allah
B) The written letters and paper
C) Both the Quran and the letters
D) Neither the Quran nor the letters

Key Takeaway
The Quran as Allah's speech is uncreated; only the physical medium is created.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hold the mushaf, do you truly believe you are holding the uncreated speech of Allah, or do you see it as just a book? What change would it make in your heart to consider the ink and paper as created but the meaning as eternal?
?
The passage says that variations in recitation length do not affect the Quran's uncreated nature. Have you ever felt uneasy about different qira'at? What would it mean for your trust in the Quran if you fully accepted that the divine speech remains unchanged despite different sounds?
?
The author declares that whoever says the Quran is created is a disbeliever. Have you ever inadvertently thought of the Quran as created because it is written or recited? How deeply does the doctrine of the uncreated Quran affect your reverence when you recite or listen to it?

Divine Transcendence

The Impermissibility of Likening Allah to Creation
Samarqandi warns against comparing Allah to created things and instructs to recite the Quran when asked to describe Him.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why comparing Allah to created things is forbidden and how to respond when asked to describe Him.

Core Idea
Allah is beyond all comparison; describe Him by His own words.

This lesson from Imam al-Samarqandi's 'al-Sawad al-A'zam' addresses a fundamental principle: Allah is utterly unlike His creation. The author not only prohibits likening Allah to anything but also provides a clear method for responding to requests for a description of Allah—by reciting His own words.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its translation to grasp the overall argument.
The passage contains a clear chain of reasoning.
2
Identify the central prohibition
Notice the first statement: one must not liken Allah (تشبيه) to anything. This is the key command.
3
Examine the proof and analogy
The author cites Quran 42:11, then provides a logical argument: all things are created, so the Creator cannot be like them. The analogy of the doer and deed reinforces this.
Reflect on why the analogy is effective.
4
Understand the consequence and the response
The passage states that anyone who attributes a body or tongue to Allah has disbelieved (كفر). Finally, when asked to describe Allah, one should recite a Quranic verse rather than using human descriptions.
Note the practical takeaway for everyday faith.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الخامسة والاربعون
وماقلنا انه ينبغي له أن يعلم ان لا يشبه الله بشيء لان الله تعالى قاله ليس كمثله شيء فى الأرض ولا فى السماء وهو السميع البصير ) واعلم ان الاشياء كلها مخلوقة ولا بد للمخلوق من خالق ولا يشبه الخالق بالمخلوق كما ان العامل لا يشبه بالعمل فاذا كان الانسان لا يشبه نفسه بعلمه فالخالق اولى ان لا يشبه بالمخلوق ومن قال ان الله بدا اولسانا او جسما وما أشبه ذلك وقد كفر فان قال قائل صف لى ربك فاقرأ قوله تعالى بسم الله الرحمن (الرحيم)
Issue forty-five: And we said that it is befitting for him to know that he should not liken Allah to anything, because Allah Almighty said: 'There is nothing like unto Him' (Quran 42:11) in the earth or in the heavens, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing. And know that all things are created, and the created must have a Creator, and the Creator is not likened to the created, just as the doer is not likened to the deed. So if a human does not liken himself to his own knowledge, then the Creator is even more not to be likened to the created. And whoever says that Allah has a body or a tongue or the like has indeed disbelieved. So if a questioner says: 'Describe your Lord to me,' then recite the saying of Allah Almighty: 'In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful' [?]
Source pages p. 26

Commentary
This passage is a theological instruction on divine transcendence (tanzih). The author commands that one must not compare (يشبه) Allah to anything, citing Quran 42:11. The argument proceeds: all things are created (مخلوقة), and every created thing requires a Creator. The Creator cannot be likened to the created. The analogy given is that a doer (العامل) is not likened to his deed (العمل). If a human does not liken himself to his own knowledge (which is an attribute), then it is even more appropriate that the Creator not be likened to created things. The passage includes a takfir (declaration of disbelief) against anyone who says that Allah has a body (بدن), a tongue (لسان), or similar attributes implying anthropomorphism. Finally, the author provides a practical response for when someone asks 'Describe your Lord to me': recite a Quranic verse (he mentions 'بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم'—possibly a placeholder or part of a longer verse; the OCR cuts off). The intent is to use Allah's own words rather than human descriptions.

Key Terms
تشبيه
تنزيه
خالق
مخلوق
كفر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Allah can be described using human-like attributes or analogies.
Correction: Allah is absolutely unique and beyond comparison; any description must come from the Quran and Sunnah, not human imagination.

Checkpoint
What should one recite when asked to describe Allah?
A) A philosophical definition.
B) A verse from the Quran.
C) A description using analogies.
D) Nothing at all.

Key Takeaway
To describe Allah, one must rely on the Quran, not human analogies.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you try to imagine Allah, do you unconsciously picture a form or a shape? According to this lesson, that would be likening Him to creation. What step will you take today to replace those mental images with the Quran's own words?
?
The author says that if a human does not liken himself to his own knowledge, then it is even more befitting that Allah not be likened to creation. How does your own awareness of being separate from your thoughts and actions help you understand Allah's incomparability?
?
If someone suddenly asked you to describe your Lord right now, would you be prepared to recite a verse from the Quran affirming His transcendence? What is stopping you from memorizing such a verse, and when will you start?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
7 min
Allah is Unlike Anything: Refuting Tashbih
Samarqandi refutes anthropomorphism and instructs to recite Surah Ikhlas.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah cannot be likened to any created thing and how to respond when asked to describe Him.

Core Idea
Allah is absolutely unlike creation; describe Him only by reciting Surah Ikhlas.

This lesson addresses a fundamental principle: Allah's absolute transcendence (tanzih). Imam al-Samarqandi provides both a Quranic proof and a rational argument to show that likening Allah to anything is impossible and constitutes disbelief. He then gives a practical instruction: when asked to describe Allah, recite Surah al-Ikhlas, which perfectly encapsulates His oneness and incomparability.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage along with the literal translation. Notice the strong opening statement and the Quranic verse cited.
2
Understand the rational argument
Identify the analogy comparing the Creator to a worker and His creation to work. Ask yourself why this analogy is effective.
The worker is not his work; similarly, the Creator is not His creation.
3
Note the takfir statement
Samarqandi declares that anyone who describes Allah as a body or tongue has disbelieved. Reflect on the severity of anthropomorphism.
4
Learn the practical instruction
When asked to describe Allah, recite Surah al-Ikhlas. Understand why this surah is sufficient.
Surah al-Ikhlas affirms tawhid and negates all forms of similarity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الخامسة والاربعون: وما قلنا انه ينبغي له أن يعلم ان لا يشبه الله بشيء لان الله تعالى قاله ليس كمثله شيء فى الأرض ولا فى السماء وهو السميع البصير واعلم ان الاشياء كلها مخلوقة ولا بد للمخلوق من خالق ولا يشبه الخالق بالمخلوق كما ان العامل لا يشبه بالعمل فاذا كان الانسان لا يشبه نفسه بعلمه فالخالق اولى ان لا يشبه بالمخلوق ومن قال ان الله بدا اولسانا او جسما وما أشبه ذلك فقد كفر فان قال قائل صف لي ربك فاقرأ قوله تعالى بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم * قل هو الله احد الله الصمد لم يلد ولم يولد ولم يكن له كفوا احد * ونحن له مسلمون وفي هذا القدر ما هو كفاية للمعاقل والله اعلم
The forty-fifth question: And we said that it is necessary for him to know that Allah should not be likened to anything, because Allah, the Exalted, said: 'Nothing is like Him, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing.' And know that all things are created, and the created must have a Creator, and the Creator is not like the created, just as the worker is not like the work. So if a man does not liken himself to his knowledge, then the Creator is more worthy not to be likened to the created. And whoever says that Allah is a body or a tongue or a body or something like that has indeed disbelieved. So if someone says: 'Describe your Lord to me,' then recite the words of Allah, the Exalted: 'In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Say: He is Allah, the One. Allah, the Eternal. He begets not, nor was He begotten. And there is none comparable to Him.' And we are Muslims to Him. And in this there is sufficient for the intelligent. And Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 26

Commentary
This passage is a proof and instruction against tashbih (anthropomorphism). Imam al-Samarqandi begins by stating that it is necessary to know that Allah should not be likened (yushabbah) to anything, citing the Quranic verse 'Nothing is like Him' (Q 42:11). He then provides a rational argument: all things are created (makhluq), and the created needs a Creator; the Creator cannot resemble the created, just as a worker (al-'amil) does not resemble his work (al-'amal). He gives an analogy: a person does not resemble his own knowledge, so how much more the Creator is unlike His creation. Next, he issues a takfir: whoever says that Allah is a body, a tongue, or a body (likely 'jism' or 'lisan') or similar has disbelieved. He then offers a practical instruction: if someone asks you to describe your Lord, recite Surah al-Ikhlas (Q 112:1-4), because it contains a sufficient description of Allah's oneness and transcendence. He concludes that this is enough for the intelligent.

Key Terms
تشبيه
مخلوق
خالق
عامل
عمل
وصف
سورة الإخلاص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think it is acceptable to describe Allah using human terms as long as the meaning is purified.
Correction: Samarqandi explicitly declares that describing Allah as a body or tongue is kufr (disbelief). Even analogies must be avoided; the only safe description is what Allah and His Messenger have revealed, such as Surah Ikhlas.

Checkpoint
What does Samarqandi instruct one to recite when asked to describe Allah?
A) Ayat al-Kursi
B) Surah al-Ikhlas
C) The first verse of Surah al-Fatiha
D) The basmalah alone

Key Takeaway
The only proper way to describe Allah is to affirm His absolute transcendence and recite Surah al-Ikhlas, as any attempt to liken Him to creation leads to disbelief.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever mentally pictured Allah with human-like features, even if subconsciously? How does this passage expose that as a form of disbelief, and what concrete step will you take to train your heart to avoid such imagination?
?
If a non-Muslim or a child asks you, 'What does Allah look like?', will you recite Surah Ikhlas with full conviction, or will you feel the urge to add human descriptions? What does your answer reveal about your own attachment to anthropomorphic ideas?
?
The analogy says a worker is not like his work. You have knowledge, yet you are not that knowledge. Do you truly internalize that the Creator is infinitely more unlike His creation? When you venerate Allah in prayer, do you pause to shed all mental images, or do you rely on comfortable, familiar concepts that this passage declares kufr?

Negating Location and Anthropomorphism for Allah
Samarqandi negates place, coming, going, and anthropomorphism, citing the hadith of Musa.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why it is necessary to negate any notion of place, coming, going, or likeness to creatures from Allah, and the significance of the hadith of Musa.

Core Idea
Allah is not like His creation; we must affirm His transcendence without asking how.

Samarqandi begins by listing attributes that must be negated from Allah: place, coming, going, and any resemblance to creatures. He grounds this in a profound tradition addressed to Prophet Musa, where Allah commands him to know His divinity but not to occupy himself with the 'how' of His existence. This lesson establishes the foundational principle of tanzih (transcendence).

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Let the author's own words establish the tone.
Notice the directness of the command to negate.
2
Identify the negated attributes
List the four items mentioned: place, coming, going, and any attribute like creatures. Understand each.
These are all anthropomorphic concepts.
3
Reflect on the hadith of Musa
Consider why Allah told Musa to know Him but not occupy with 'how'. How does this guide our approach?
The hadith is a key proof for tanzih.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة السادسة والاربعون: وما ذكرنا انه ينبغي له ان لا ينبت له تعالى مكانا ولا مجيئا ولا ذهابا ولا صفة كصفة المخلوقين فلان تمام الايمان ان يعرف الله تعالى ولا يشتغل بكيفيته لان الله تعالى قال لموسى بن عمران عليه السلام يا موسى اعلم ولا تعلم علمين اعلم باني اله ولا تشتغل بكيفيتي واعلم انى رازق ولا تعلم من اين ارزق العباد والصواب في ذلك ان يعلم أنه تعالى ليس على مكان ولا هو محتاج الى مكان والعرش قائم بقدرته ولا يصفه بالمجئ والذهاب لان المجئ والذهاب لكل منهما ثلاثة معان اما ان يكون لا يرى فيدنو حتى يرى واما ان يكون لا يقدر فيدنو حتى يقدر واما ان يكون لا يسمع فيدنو حتى يسمع فمن شبه الله تعالى بهذه الاشياء فقد كفر واما الآيات المتشابهات والاخبار المتشابهات فينبغي له ان يؤمن بها ولا يفسر لان تفسيرها يدخل فى مذهب التعطيل فيصير مبتدعا واذا رأيت آية متشابه فدع ذلك الى الله تعالى ولا تفسره حتى تنجو لانه ليس فرضا عليك ان تعرف تفسيره بل الفرض عليك ان تؤمن به وفي هذا القدر كفاية للعاقل والله اعلم
The forty-sixth question: And what we mentioned that it is necessary for him not to affirm for Him, the Exalted, any place, nor coming, nor going, nor an attribute like the attribute of creatures. For the completion of faith is that one knows Allah, the Exalted, and does not occupy oneself with His how-ness (kayfiyya). Because Allah, the Exalted, said to Musa ibn 'Imran, peace be upon him: 'O Musa, know and do not know two knowledges: know that I am God, and do not occupy yourself with My how-ness; and know that I am the Provider, and do not know from where I provide the servants.' And the correct in that is to know that He, the Exalted, is not upon a place nor does He need a place, and the Throne stands by His power. And do not describe Him with coming and going, because coming and going each have three meanings: either He is not seen, so He draws near until He is seen; or He is not able, so He draws near until He is able; or He does not hear, so He draws near until He hears. So whoever likens Allah, the Exalted, to these things has disbelieved. And as for the ambiguous verses (ayat mutashabihat) and ambiguous reports, it is necessary for him to believe in them and not interpret them, because their interpretation enters into the school of ta'til (negation of attributes), and he becomes an innovator. And when you see an ambiguous verse, leave that to Allah, the Exalted, and do not interpret it until you are saved, because it is not obligatory upon you to know its interpretation; rather, the obligation upon you is to believe in it. And in this there is sufficient for the intelligent. And Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 27

Commentary
This passage is a detailed instruction on how to approach Allah's attributes, especially the ambiguous (mutashabih) texts. Imam al-Samarqandi begins by listing what must be negated from Allah: place (makan), coming (maji'), going (dhahab), and any attribute resembling creatures. The reason is that complete faith (tamam al-iman) requires knowing Allah without delving into 'how' (kayfiyya). He supports this with a tradition (hadith qudsi or saying) addressed to Musa: 'Know that I am God, and do not occupy yourself with My how-ness.' Then he states the correct (sawab): Allah is not on a place, not in need of a place, and the Throne (al-'arsh) stands by His power. He warns against describing Him with coming and going, which are anthropomorphic terms. He explains that coming and going each have three possible meanings that apply to creatures (not seeing, not power, not hearing), and applying these to Allah is tashbih and constitutes kufr. Then he addresses the mutashabihat (ambiguous verses and hadith): one must believe in them (iman biha) without interpretation (tafsir), because interpreting them leads to ta'til (negating attributes) and innovation (bid'a). He advises to leave the interpretation to Allah and not to engage in it, as it is not obligatory to know the interpretation but only to believe. This is the classical Salafi approach of tafwid (consigning the meaning to Allah).

Key Terms
مكان
مجيء
ذهاب
كيفية
موسى
عرش

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What did Allah command Musa to do according to the passage?
A) To know that He is God and to know His how-ness
B) To know that He is God but not to occupy with His how-ness
C) To know that He is the Provider and to know from where He provides
D) To occupy himself with the how-ness of God

Key Takeaway
Affirming Allah's transcendence requires negating all anthropomorphic attributes and accepting His oneness without asking how.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that Allah 'comes' on the Day of Judgment (e.g., Surah al-Fajr), do you find yourself trying to picture that? What does your struggle with that verse reveal about your heart's attachment to physicality?
?
The hadith tells Musa not to know 'from where' Allah provides. Where in your life do you obsess over the 'how' of provision—your job, your income—rather than simply trusting that Allah is the Provider? How can you shift that focus today?
?
If you truly internalized that 'the Throne stands by His power' and not by any support, would you still worry about who holds power in your life? What would change if you lived as if Allah alone upholds everything?

The Three Meanings of Coming and Going and the Kufr of Tashbih
Samarqandi explains the three meanings of coming/going and declares tashbih as kufr.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why describing Allah with coming and going in a literal sense constitutes disbelief, and the three possible meanings that apply only to creatures.

Core Idea
Coming and going are attributes of creatures; applying them to Allah is kufr.

Samarqandi provides a detailed analysis of the terms 'coming' and 'going' as they might be mistakenly ascribed to Allah. He explains that these terms, when used for creatures, imply change and need. Those who liken Allah to His creation fall into disbelief.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Focus on the specific warning about 'coming' and 'going'.
Note the three meanings listed.
2
List the three meanings
Identify the three possible meanings for creatures: not seeing then seeing, not power then power, not hearing then hearing.
3
Understand why likening leads to kufr
Reflect on why applying these to Allah is disbelief—it implies change and need in Allah.
This is tashbih (anthropomorphism).

Commentary

The Ruling on Mutashabihat: Belief without Interpretation
Samarqandi rules that mutashabihat must be believed without interpretation, warning against ta'til.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the correct approach to ambiguous verses: believe without interpretation, leaving their interpretation to Allah.

Core Idea
The only safety with mutashabihat is to believe and not interpret.

Samarqandi concludes with a crucial methodological rule: when encountering unclear verses or reports about Allah's attributes, one must believe in them without attempting to interpret them. Attempting interpretation leads to ta'til and innovation, and it is not an obligation to know their meaning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Pay attention to the commands: 'believe in them' and 'do not interpret'.
Note the warning about ta'til and bid'a.
2
Understand why interpretation is dangerous
Samarqandi says interpreting leads to ta'til (negating attributes) and innovation.
This is a key principle of the Salaf.
3
Know your obligation
The obligation is to believe in the verses, not to know their interpretation.
This relieves the believer from unnecessary speculation.

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
8 min
The Correct Belief Regarding Allah's Place and Attributes
Imam Samarqandi explains the necessity of avoiding anthropomorphism regarding Allah's place and movement.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why affirming place or movement for Allah leads to anthropomorphism (tashbih) and how to correctly approach ambiguous texts (mutashabih) by affirming without interpretation.

Core Idea
Perfect faith is to know Allah without asking 'how'.

Imam Samarqandi addresses a crucial aspect of Islamic creed: safeguarding Allah's transcendence. He warns against attributing place, movement, or any created attribute to Allah, and provides guidance on how to engage with scriptural passages that seem to imply such things.

Guided Path
1
Understand the principle of tanzih
Read the first part of the passage where the Imam prohibits attributing a place, coming/going, or any attribute of creatures to Allah. This establishes the core principle of divine transcendence.
The word 'kayfiyya' (how-ness) is key—do not delve into Allah's modality.
2
Reflect on the hadith of Musa
The Imam quotes a divine instruction to Prophet Musa: know Allah without occupying yourself with His how-ness, and know He is the Provider without knowing from where. This shows that seeking the 'how' of divine actions is beyond human capacity.
This hadith (whether prophetic or qudsi) is a cornerstone for the salaf approach.
3
Analyze the three anthropomorphic meanings of 'coming and going'
The Imam lists three possible literal interpretations of coming/going: moving from unseen to seen, from inability to ability, from not hearing to hearing. He declares that likening Allah to any of these is kufr.
These interpretations imply change and imperfection in Allah, which is impossible.
4
Learn the correct approach to ambiguous verses
For mutashabih verses and hadith, the Imam instructs to believe in them without interpretation, because interpreting leads to ta'til (denial of attributes) and bid'a. The obligation is to affirm without explaining the exact modality.
This is the way of the salaf: tafwid (consigning the meaning to Allah) while affirming the apparent meaning in a befitting manner.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما ذكرنا انه ينبغي له ان لا ينبت له تعالى مكانا ولا يحينا ولاذهابا ولا صفة كصفة المخلوقين فلان تمام الايمان ان يعرف الله تعالى ولا يشتغل بكيفيته لان الله تعالى قال لموسى بن عمران عليه السلام يا موسى اعلم ولا تعلم اثنين اعلام باني اله ولاشتغل بكيفيتي واعلم انى رازق ولا تعلم من اين ارزق العباد والصواب في ذلك ان يعلم أنه تعالى ليس على مكان ولاهو محتاج الى مكان والعرش قائم بقدرته ولا يصفه بالمجئ والذهاب لان المجئ والذهاب لكل منهما ثلاثة معان اما ان يكون لا يرى فيدنو حتى يرى واما ان يكون لا يقدر فيدنو حتى يقدر واما ان يكون لا يسمع فيدنو حتى يسمع فمن شبه الله تعالى بهذه الاشياء فقد كفرواما الآيات المتشابهات والاخبار المتشابهات فينبغي له ان يؤمن بها ولا يفسر لان تفسيرها يدخل فى مذهب التعطيل فيصير مبتدعا واذا رأيت آية المتشابه فدع ذلك الى الله تعالى ولا نفسره حتى تنجولانه ليس فرضا عليك ان تعرف تفسيره بل الفرض عليك ان تؤمن به وفى هذا القدر كفاية للعاقل والله اعلم
And what we mentioned: that he should not affirm for the Exalted a place, nor coming nor going, nor a attribute like the attribute of created beings, because the completeness of faith is to know Allah the Exalted and not to occupy oneself with His how-ness. For Allah the Exalted said to Musa ibn Imran, peace be upon him: 'O Musa, know and do not know two things: know that I am God, and do not occupy yourself with My how-ness; and know that I am the Provider, and do not know from where I provide for the servants.' And the correct in that is that he knows that He the Exalted is not upon a place, nor is He in need of a place, and the Throne is established by His power; and he does not describe Him with coming and going, because coming and going each have three meanings: either that He was not seen, so He draws near until He is seen; or that He was not able, so He draws near until He is able; or that He was not hearing, so He draws near until He hears. So whoever likens Allah the Exalted to these things has disbelieved. As for the ambiguous verses (āyāt mutashābihāt) and ambiguous reports (akhbār mutashābihāt), he should believe in them and not interpret them, because their interpretation enters into the way of taṭṭīl (denial of attributes), and he becomes an innovator. And when you see an ambiguous verse, leave that to Allah the Exalted and do not interpret it until you are safe. For it is not obligatory upon you to know its interpretation; rather the obligation upon you is to believe in it. And in this is sufficient for the intelligent. And Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 27

Commentary
This passage addresses the correct belief about Allah's attributes, specifically rejecting anthropomorphism and affirming tanzih (transcendence). The Imam states that one should not affirm for Allah a place, nor motion (coming/going), nor any attribute that resembles creatures. The reason is that perfect faith (tamam al-iman) is to know Allah without delving into 'how' (kayfiyya). He cites a hadith qudsi (or report) where Allah tells Musa: 'Know that I am God, and do not occupy yourself with My how-ness; know that I am the Provider, and do not know from where I provide.' This establishes that the how of Allah's essence and attributes is beyond human grasp. The correct position is to affirm that Allah is not located in a place nor dependent on space; the Throne ('arsh) is upheld by His power, not a resting place. Regarding 'coming and going' (maji' wa dhahab), the Imam explains these terms have three possible anthropomorphic meanings: (1) moving from not being seen to being seen, (2) from inability to ability, (3) from not hearing to hearing. Whoever likens Allah to any of these has committed kufr because they imply change. Then he discusses mutashabih (ambiguous) verses and hadith: one must believe in them without interpretation (tafsir), because interpreting them leads to ta'til (denying attributes) and innovation (bid'a). The obligation is to believe in their apparent meaning in a way befitting Allah, not to seek their exact interpretation. The passage ends with the statement that this is sufficient for the intelligent person.

Key Terms
تمام الإيمان
الكيفية
المجئ والذهاب
الآيات المتشابهات
التعطيل
البدعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Believing that Allah's attributes like 'coming' or 'going' imply literal movement or change in Allah.
Correction: The Imam explains that such literal interpretations attribute imperfection (like becoming able after being unable) and are acts of kufr. The correct belief is to affirm the attribute without modality.

Checkpoint
According to Imam Samarqandi, what is the correct approach to mutashabih (ambiguous) verses and hadith?
A) Interpret them metaphorically to avoid anthropomorphism.
B) Deny their apparent meaning entirely (ta'til).
C) Affirm them without interpretation and leave their meaning to Allah.
D) Accept only the verses that are clear (muhkam) and reject the ambiguous.

Key Takeaway
To preserve tawhid, one must affirm Allah's attributes without inquiring into how and must not interpret ambiguous texts in a way that resembles creation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a verse that seems to describe Allah in human terms, does your heart rest with, 'I believe in it as it came, without knowing how,' or do you feel an urgent need to 'figure it out'? What does that urgency reveal about your trust in Allah's wisdom?
?
The Prophet Musa was told: 'Know that I am God, and do not occupy yourself with My how-ness.' Are there areas of your faith where you are demanding explanations that Allah has not given? Will you surrender that demand today?
?
If someone asked you, 'Where is Allah?' would your answer reflect the imam's teaching that He is not upon a place nor in need of it? How would you respond without slipping into anthropomorphism or denial?

Tawfiq is Simultaneous With Action
Defines tawfiq as simultaneous with action, refutes two extremes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that divine assistance (tawfiq) coincides with human action, not before or after, and how this balanced view avoids the extremes of Qadariyya and Jabriyya.

Core Idea
Tawfiq accompanies sincere action, not before or after — a middle path between two extremes.

This passage from Imam Samarqandi addresses a key theological question about the timing of divine assistance. Some sects placed tawfiq before action, others after; both miss the mark. The author presents the Sunni position that tawfiq is simultaneous, contingent on the servant's intention. We'll explore the arguments and the proof from the Quran.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic text carefully along with the literal translation. Notice how the author states his thesis at the very beginning: al-tawfiq ma'a al-fi'l mustawiyan (tawfiq and the act are simultaneous).
Pay attention to the condemnatory language: 'Magians of this community'.
2
Identify the two erroneous positions
The passage explicitly names two wrong views: Qadariyya (tawfiq before the act) and Jabriyya (tawfiq after the act). Note the reasoning behind each and why they are deemed heretical.
The author uses a hadith to support that tawfiq is not given before action, but the power to act is.
3
Understand the correct view and its proof
The correct view is that tawfiq occurs simultaneously with the act, based on the servant's intention. The proof is Quran 29:69. Also note the consequences of the extremes: if Jabri view were true, disbelievers would be excused; if Qadari view were true, Allah would be described as incapable.
The passage ends by saying istita'a (capability) is also simultaneous with the act.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثانية والاربعون وما قلنا أنه ينبغي له أن يعلم أن التوفيق مع الفعل مستويان وينبغي أن لا يقول أن التوفيق قبل الفعل فان هذا مذهب القدرية، ومن قال أن التوفيق بعد الفعل فان هذا مذهب الجبرية. والقدرى والجبرى مجوس هذه الأمة. واعلم أن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم قال: العبد قد أعطى قوة العمل وكلف بذلك حتى يلزم عليه الحجة ولم يعط قوة التوفيق لأن التوفيق من الله تعالى. والقدرى يقول: الخير والشر كله مني وليس الله فيه صنع. والجبرى يقول: الخير والشر من الله تعالى وليس للعبد فيه صنع. فالقدرى أضاف الربوبية إلى نفسه، والجبرى أضاف العبودية إلى الله تعالى، وكلاهما مبتدع. والصواب في ذلك أن يعلم أن من كان غرضه وجهده ومراده طاعة الله تعالى ورضاه يجد التوفيق من الله تعالى مجد العمل، ومن كان غرضه وجهده ومراده معصية الله تعالى أصاب خذلان الله مجد العمل. والدليل قوله تعالى: "والذين جاهدوا فينا لنهدينهم سبلنا" . فان قال كما قال الجبرى لكان الكفار معذورين عند ربهم، وان قال كما قال القدرى كله منا وليس الله فيه صنع فقد وصف الله تعالى بالعجز وهذا محال فكفروا. واعلم أن الاستطاعة عند أهل العدل مع الفعل مستويان لا يتقدم ولا يتأخر. وقوله تعالى: "أنتم الفقراء إلى الله" وقد قال...
The forty-second question: And what we said: That he should know that tawfiq (divine assistance) and the act are simultaneous, and he should not say that tawfiq is before the act, for this is the doctrine of the Qadariyya, and whoever says that tawfiq is after the act, this is the doctrine of the Jabriyya. The Qadari and the Jabari are the Magians of this community. And know that the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: The servant has been given the power of action and charged with it so that the proof is established against him, but he has not been given the power of tawfiq, because tawfiq is from Allah Most High. The Qadari says: Good and evil are all from me, and Allah has no part in it. The Jabari says: Good and evil are from Allah Most High, and the servant has no part in it. So the Qadari attributed lordship to himself, and the Jabari attributed servitude to Allah Most High, and both are innovators. The correct view in this is to know that whoever's aim, effort, and intention is obedience to Allah Most High and His pleasure finds tawfiq from Allah Most High at the moment of the act, and whoever's aim, effort, and intention is disobedience to Allah Most High receives abandonment (khidhlan) from Allah at the moment of the act. The proof is His statement: 'And those who strive in Us, We will surely guide them to Our ways' (Quran 29:69). If he said as the Jabari said, then the disbelievers would be excused before their Lord, and if he said as the Qadari said, that everything is from us and Allah has no part, then he has described Allah with incapability, and this is impossible, so they have disbelieved. And know that capability (istita'a) according to the people of justice is simultaneous with the act, neither preceding nor following. And His statement: 'You are the poor toward Allah' (Quran 35:15) and He has said...
Source pages p. 25

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal clarification on the relationship between divine assistance (tawfiq) and human action. It begins by asserting the correct Sunni position: tawfiq is simultaneous with the act, not before (as the Qadariyya claim) nor after (as the Jabriyya claim). The Qadari and Jabari are condemned as 'Magians of this community'. The author then quotes a prophetic hadith to show that the servant is given the ability to act but not the ability to obtain tawfiq; tawfiq is solely from Allah. The Qadari error is attributing both good and evil entirely to oneself, denying Allah's role; the Jabari error is attributing everything to Allah, denying human agency. Both are innovators. The correct view is that one who sincerely intends obedience will find tawfiq from Allah at the moment of action, while one who intends disobedience will encounter divine abandonment. The proof is Quran 29:69, which promises guidance to those who strive. If the Jabari view were true, disbelievers would be excused for their unbelief; if the Qadari view were true, Allah would be described as incapable, which is impossible. Hence both extremes lead to disbelief. The passage closes by affirming that capability (istita'a) is simultaneous with the act, neither pre-existing nor coming after, and references Quran 35:15, though the text cuts off. This emphasizes human responsibility within divine sovereignty.

Key Terms
توفيق
فعل
قدرية
جبرية
استطاعة
خذلان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think tawfiq is given before action as a precondition or after action as a reward.
Correction: The passage clarifies that tawfiq accompanies the act itself, contingent on the sincerity of intention. It is neither pre-bestowed nor post-rewarded but simultaneous.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, when does tawfiq occur in relation to the act?
A) Before the act
B) After the act
C) Simultaneously with the act
D) It is not related to the act

Key Takeaway
Tawfiq from Allah coincides with the servant's sincere intention at the moment of action, avoiding the extremes of attributing all to self or all to God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you succeed in a good deed, do you secretly feel it was your own doing, forgetting that the ability and tawfiq came from Allah? How can you remind yourself of your poverty before Him, as the verse 'You are the poor toward Allah' demands?
?
When you commit a sin, do you ever catch yourself excusing it by feeling that it was unavoidable or decreed, similar to the Jabari view? What would it mean for your repentance if you truly believed Allah’s abandonment is tied to your intention at that moment?
?
Is your daily effort—your 'aim, effort, and intention'—genuinely directed toward Allah's pleasure, or do you often act out of habit? If you examine your heart right now, would you find tawfiq or khidhlan accompanying your current state?

The Imam's Hadath and Congregational Prayer
Explains the ruling on praying behind an imam who has hadath and the nullification of the follower's prayer.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the imam's ritual impurity (hadath) invalidates the congregation's prayer and the logical consequences of rejecting this principle.

Core Idea
The imam's hadath is the congregation's hadath – congregational prayer is one act.

In this passage, Imam al-Samarqandi addresses a crucial issue in congregational prayer: if the imam’s wudu breaks, does the congregation’s prayer become invalid? He argues that it does, because the imam’s state is directly linked to the followers. Using a hadith and a logical argument, he shows that rejecting this principle leads to absurd consequences. Let’s examine his reasoning step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Carefully read the Arabic passage and its English translation to get a sense of the argument.
Notice the author's use of a hadith and a hypothetical objection.
2
Identify the core principle
Find the sentence where the author states the principle that the imam's hadath is the congregation's hadath.
3
Understand the reductio argument
See how the author rebuts the objection by showing that if the principle is rejected, it would allow prayer behind non-Muslims, which is invalid.
This is a logical consequence argument.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثانية والخمسون - وما قلنا انه ينبغى له ان يرى ان حدث الامام حدث للقوم من لم يرحدث الامام حدثا للقوم لاتجوز الصلاة خلفه لان النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم قال الامام ضامن والمؤذن مؤتمن فان قال انا اصلى صلاتى والامام يصلى صلاته قتل بايبي يصير القوم مقتدين به وان كان كل واحد منهم يصلى صلاة نفسه ولا يكون حدث الامام حدثا للقوم فبأى شىء يكون فضل الجماعة فاذا كان كذلك فيبتني على قولك انه اذا كان الامام يهوديا او نصرانيا او مجوسيا او امرأة تجوز صلاتك خلفه وهذا يكفي ان شرح الله صدره للاسلام وفيه كفاية للعاقل والله اعلم
The fifty-second issue: And what we have said that it is necessary for him to hold that the hadath (ritual impurity) of the imam is the hadath of the congregation. Whoever does not consider the imam's hadath as the congregation's hadath – it is not permissible to pray behind him, because the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, said: 'The imam is the guarantor and the mu'adhdhin is the trustee.' If he says, 'I pray my prayer and the imam prays his prayer,' then I say: By what do the people become followers of him? If each one of them prays his own prayer, and the imam's hadath is not the congregation's hadath, then what is the virtue of congregational prayer? If that is the case, then it follows from your statement that if the imam is a Jew, Christian, Magian, or a woman, your prayer behind him would be permissible. And this is sufficient if Allah expands his heart for Islam. And in it is sufficiency for the intelligent. And Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 32

Commentary
This passage is a legal ruling (mas'alah) on a specific point of fiqh related to congregational prayer. The author asserts the principle that the ritual purity of the imam is considered as the purity of the congregation; if the imam's wudu is broken (hadath), the congregation's prayer is also invalidated because they are following him. He argues that whoever rejects this principle must explain how the congregation's prayer is valid if the imam's hadath does not affect them, and he uses the hadith 'the imam is a guarantor' to support the connection. The author then refutes a hypothetical objection: if someone claims each person prays for himself and the imam's impurity does not affect them, then logically one could pray behind a non-Muslim or a woman, which is invalid. Thus, the principle is necessary to differentiate valid congregational prayer from individual prayer. The passage ends with a note that this suffices for the intelligent.

Key Terms
الحدث
الامام
صلاة الجماعة
اقتداء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The imam's sinfulness affects the validity of the congregation's prayer.
Correction: The issue is specifically about ritual impurity (hadath), not the imam's moral state. The principle is about legal purity, not personal sin.

Checkpoint
According to the author, what is the consequence of not considering the imam's hadath as the congregation's hadath?
A) The congregational prayer has no special virtue
B) One could theoretically pray behind a non-Muslim imam
C) The prayer is still valid because each person prays for himself
D) The imam must repeat his prayer

Key Takeaway
The imam's ritual impurity invalidates the congregation's prayer because the imam's state is legally tied to the followers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you pray behind an imam, do you genuinely feel that your prayer is united with his? If not, what does that say about your understanding of the congregation as a single body in worship?
?
This lesson teaches that the imam's state directly impacts your prayer. Are you careful about the condition of the imam you follow? Or do you take it lightly, assuming your own prayer is independent?
?
The author warns that rejecting the principle leads to permitting prayer behind non-Muslims. Does your heart fully accept that the imam's hadath is your hadath? If you find resistance, what is the root of that resistance in your own assumptions?

Ruling on Wudu with Stagnant Water
Ruling on the permissibility of wudu with stagnant water versus flowing water.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the conditions for valid wudu regarding water types, specifically why stagnant water is impermissible and flowing water permissible.

Core Idea
Stagnant water cannot purify itself, so wudu with it is invalid.

This lesson examines a specific ruling from the Hanafi tradition regarding the types of water acceptable for wudu. Imam Samarqandi explains that small stagnant pools are not permissible due to their inability to flow and thus become impure more easily, while flowing water remains pure unless impurity is visible. The ruling has serious implications for one's prayer.

Guided Path
1
Identify the water types and their signs
Read the passage and distinguish between stagnant water (small pools that move as one) and flowing water (streams or rivers). Note the sign given for stagnant water.
The sign is a physical test: if moving one side moves the other, it is stagnant.
2
Understand the condition for permissibility
Contrast the rulings: stagnant water is never permissible for wudu (when small), while flowing water is permissible even in small amounts unless impurity is visibly present.
3
Grasp the consequence for dissent
The author declares that anyone who permits wudu with stagnant water is not to be prayed behind, implying their wudu is invalid. Reflect on the seriousness of this ruling.
This underscores the importance of correct fiqh in practice.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المسئلة الثالثة والخمسون - وما قلناه ان الوضوء لا يجوز بالماء القليل الراكد وعلامته اذا حرك جانبه تحرك الجانب الاخر فلا يجوز الوضوء منه وان كان الماء جاريا يجوز الوضوء منه وان قل اذا لم يربه اثر النجاسة ومن قال يجوز الوضوء من الماء الراكد لا تجوز الصلاة خلفه لانه لا يتوضأ ابدا وهذا كفاية للمعاقلى
The fifty-third issue: And what we said that wudu is not permissible with a small amount of stagnant water. Its sign is that if one side is moved, the other side moves – so wudu from it is not permissible. But if the water is flowing, wudu from it is permissible even if it is small, as long as he does not see a trace of impurity in it. And whoever says that wudu from stagnant water is permissible, it is not permissible to pray behind him, because he never performs wudu properly. And this is sufficient for the intelligent.
Source pages p. 32

Commentary
This passage is a legal ruling (mas'alah) on the conditions for valid wudu regarding water types. The author states that wudu is not allowed using a small amount of stagnant water – defined as water so still that moving one side causes the other side to move (i.e., a pool that does not flow). The reason is that such water becomes impure by mere contact with an impure thing, as it cannot purify itself. However, flowing water (e.g., a river or stream) is permissible for wudu even in small quantity, provided no visible trace of impurity is present. The author then declares that anyone who permits wudu from stagnant water is not to be prayed behind, because he never performs a valid wudu (due to using inherently impure water). This reflects the Hanafi view prevalent in the author's tradition. The passage ends with a statement of sufficiency for the intelligent.

Key Terms
الوضوء
الماء الراكد
الماء الجاري
النجاسة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: All stagnant water is impure and cannot be used for wudu.
Correction: This ruling applies specifically to small stagnant pools that are not flowing; large quantities of standing water (e.g., a lake) are not necessarily impure.

Checkpoint
What is the sign of stagnant water mentioned in the passage?
A) It has a bad smell
B) It is covered with foam
C) Moving one side causes the other side to move
D) It is less than two qullah

Key Takeaway
Wudu is only valid with flowing water or large quantities of standing water; small stagnant pools are impure and invalid for wudu.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever performed wudu with water that you suspected might be stagnant, choosing convenience over certainty? What will you do differently now to ensure your wudu is valid?
?
The author warns against praying behind someone who permits wudu with stagnant water. Are you confident in the fiqh knowledge of those you follow in prayer? How can you take responsibility for learning these rulings yourself?
?
This lesson distinguishes between 'good enough' and truly valid worship. Where else in your life do you settle for convenience rather than seeking the full correctness of your deeds before Allah? What is one step you will take today to act on this lesson?

beginner
